title: The Overlooked Repetitive Lengthening Form in Sentiment Analysis
summary: Individuals engaging in online communication frequently express personal opinions with informal styles (e.g., memes and emojis). While Language Models (LMs) with informal communications have been widely discussed, a unique and emphatic style, the Repetitive Lengthening Form (RLF), has been overlooked for years. In this paper, we explore answers to two research questions: 1) Is RLF important for sentiment analysis (SA)? 2) Can LMs understand RLF? Inspired by previous linguistic research, we curate \textbf{Lengthening}, the first multi-domain dataset with 850k samples focused on RLF for SA. Moreover, we introduce \textbf{Exp}lainable \textbf{Instruct}ion Tuning (\textbf{ExpInstruct}), a two-stage instruction tuning framework aimed to improve both performance and explainability of LLMs for RLF. We further propose a novel unified approach to quantify LMs' understanding of informal expressions. We show that RLF sentences are expressive expressions and can serve as signatures of document-level sentiment. Additionally, RLF has potential value for online content analysis. Our results show that fine-tuned Pre-trained Language Models (PLMs) can surpass zero-shot GPT-4 in performance but not in explanation for RLF. Finally, we show ExpInstruct can improve the open-sourced LLMs to match zero-shot GPT-4 in performance and explainability for RLF with limited samples. Code and sample data are available at https://github.com/Tom-Owl/OverlookedRLF

AI.tldr: The paper studies repetitive lengthening forms (like looong) in online text, builds a large sentiment dataset around them, and proposes a new instruction-tuning method to make LLMs both accurate and explainable on such inputs.
AI.motivation: Informal online text often uses elongated spellings (repetitive lengthening forms, RLFs), but prior sentiment analysis and LM research has largely ignored this phenomenon, despite its potential importance for expressing strong sentiment and affecting model understanding.
AI.method: They construct Lengthening, an 850k-example multi-domain dataset focusing on RLFs for sentiment analysis, design ExpInstruct, a two-stage explainable instruction-tuning framework for LLMs, and introduce a unified metric to quantify models’ understanding of informal expressions like RLF. They then fine-tune PLMs and open-source LLMs and compare them with zero-shot GPT-4 on both accuracy and explanation quality.
AI.result: RLF sentences are shown to be strong indicators of document-level sentiment and useful for online content analysis. Fine-tuned PLMs outperform zero-shot GPT-4 on sentiment prediction involving RLFs but produce worse explanations. With limited samples, ExpInstruct enables open-source LLMs to reach GPT-4-level performance and explanation quality on RLF-related tasks.
AI.conclusion: Repetitive lengthening is an impactful, previously overlooked feature for sentiment analysis and online content understanding. Dedicated data and explainable instruction tuning substantially improve LLMs’ ability to handle such informal expressions, letting open-source models rival GPT-4 in both performance and interpretability on RLF tasks.

title: Scaling Reasoning Tokens via RL and Parallel Thinking: Evidence From Competitive Programming
summary: We study how to scale reasoning token budgets for competitive programming through two complementary approaches: training-time reinforcement learning (RL) and test-time parallel thinking. During RL training, we observe an approximately log-linear relationship between validation accuracy and the average number of generated reasoning tokens over successive checkpoints, and show two ways to shift this training trajectory: verification RL warmup raises the starting point, while randomized clipping produces a steeper trend in the observed regime. As scaling single-generation reasoning during RL quickly becomes expensive under full attention, we introduce a multi-round parallel thinking pipeline that distributes the token budget across threads and rounds of generation, verification, and refinement. We train the model end-to-end on this pipeline to match the training objective to the test-time structure. Starting from Seed-OSS-36B, the full system with 16 threads and 16 rounds per thread matches the underlying RL model's oracle pass@16 at pass@1 using 7.6 million tokens per problem on average, and surpasses GPT-5-high on 456 hard competitive programming problems from AetherCode.

AI.tldr: Study on scaling reasoning token budgets for competitive programming via RL and parallel thinking, achieving strong performance with multi-threaded, multi-round generation.
AI.motivation: Improve competitive programming performance of LLMs by more effectively using large reasoning token budgets, while managing computational cost and aligning training with test-time behavior.
AI.method: Use reinforcement learning with verification warmup and randomized clipping to shape the relationship between reasoning length and accuracy, then introduce and end-to-end train a multi-round parallel thinking pipeline that distributes a token budget over multiple threads and rounds of generation, verification, and refinement.
AI.result: Empirically find a log-linear relationship between validation accuracy and reasoning tokens; verification RL warmup improves initial performance, randomized clipping steepens the performance-vs-tokens curve. A parallel thinking system with 16 threads × 16 rounds, trained end-to-end, matches the RL model’s oracle pass@16 using pass@1 with ~7.6M tokens/problem and beats GPT-5-high on 456 hard AetherCode problems.
AI.conclusion: Carefully designed RL training and parallel, multi-round reasoning can scale token budgets more effectively for competitive programming, achieving high pass rates that rival or surpass larger frontier models while aligning training with complex test-time inference procedures.

title: M2-Verify: A Large-Scale Multidomain Benchmark for Checking Multimodal Claim Consistency
summary: Evaluating scientific arguments requires assessing the strict consistency between a claim and its underlying multimodal evidence. However, existing benchmarks lack the scale, domain diversity, and visual complexity needed to evaluate this alignment realistically. To address this gap, we introduce M2-Verify, a large-scale multimodal dataset for checking scientific claim consistency. Sourced from PubMed and arXiv, M2-Verify provides over 469K instances across 16 domains, rigorously validated through expert audits. Extensive baseline experiments show that state-of-the-art models struggle to maintain robust consistency. While top models achieve up to 85.8\% Micro-F1 on low-complexity medical perturbations, performance drops to 61.6\% on high-complexity challenges like anatomical shifts. Furthermore, expert evaluations expose hallucinations when models generate scientific explanations for their alignment decisions. Finally, we demonstrate our dataset's utility and provide comprehensive usage guidelines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces M2-Verify, a large-scale multimodal dataset for evaluating how consistently scientific claims align with their underlying textual and visual evidence, showing that current models still struggle, especially on complex visual and domain-specific challenges.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for evaluating scientific argument verification are insufficient: they are too small, lack domain diversity, and do not capture the visual complexity typical of real scientific figures. This makes it hard to realistically assess whether models can verify that a scientific claim is strictly consistent with multimodal (text + figures) evidence. The authors are motivated to fill this gap with a more realistic, diverse, and challenging benchmark focused on scientific claim–evidence consistency.
AI.method: The authors construct M2-Verify, a large-scale multimodal dataset derived from PubMed and arXiv scientific papers. They collect claims and associate them with corresponding multimodal evidence (likely including text, tables, and figures), then generate or annotate perturbations that vary in complexity—from relatively simple medical perturbations to more complex ones like anatomical shifts. The dataset spans 469K instances across 16 scientific domains and is rigorously validated via expert audits. They then run extensive baseline experiments using state-of-the-art multimodal and language models to evaluate how well these models can detect consistency or inconsistency and to analyze model hallucinations when generating explanations for their decisions.
AI.result: The M2-Verify dataset contains more than 469K multimodal claim-evidence instances across 16 scientific domains, all expert-audited. Benchmarking current state-of-the-art models reveals that they perform reasonably well on low-complexity perturbations (up to 85.8% Micro-F1 on simpler medical cases) but their performance degrades substantially on high-complexity tasks (down to 61.6% Micro-F1 on challenges like anatomical shifts). Expert evaluations further show that models tend to hallucinate when explaining their alignment judgments, indicating unreliable reasoning even when predictions may be correct.
AI.conclusion: M2-Verify fills a critical gap in multimodal scientific verification by providing a large, diverse, and visually complex benchmark for checking scientific claim consistency with evidence. The dataset exposes significant limitations of current models, particularly on complex visual and domain-specific reasoning and in generating faithful explanations. The authors conclude that M2-Verify is a valuable resource for developing and evaluating more robust multimodal reasoning systems and provide guidelines to help researchers use it effectively.

title: Preference learning in shades of gray: Interpretable and bias-aware reward modeling for human preferences
summary: Learning human preferences in language models remains fundamentally challenging, as reward modeling relies on subtle, subjective comparisons or shades of gray rather than clear-cut labels. This study investigates the limits of current approaches and proposes a feature-augmented framework to better capture the multidimensional nature of human judgment. Using the Anthropic HHRLHF dataset, we evaluate ten diverse large language models LLMs under a standard pairwise preference setting, where baseline performance remains below 0.74 ROC AUC, highlighting the difficulty of the task. To address this, we enrich textual representations with interpretable signals: response length, refusal indicators, toxicity scores and prompt response semantic similarity, enabling models to explicitly capture key aspects of helpfulness, safety and relevance. The proposed hybrid approach yields consistent improvements across all models, achieving up to 0.84 ROC AUC and significantly higher pairwise accuracy, with DeBERTav3Large demonstrating the best performance. Beyond accuracy, we integrate SHAP and LIME to provide fine-grained interpretability, revealing that model decisions depend on contextualized safety and supportive framing rather than isolated keywords. We further analyze bias amplification, showing that while individual features have weak marginal effects, their interactions influence preference learning.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why learning human preferences for language models is hard and shows that adding simple, interpretable features to text representations significantly improves preference prediction and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Reward models for language models must learn subtle human preferences (helpfulness, safety, relevance) from pairwise comparisons, which are noisy, subjective, and multi-dimensional. Existing approaches using only raw text embeddings struggle, as shown by relatively low ROC AUC (<0.74) on the Anthropic HHRLHF dataset. The authors want to understand these limits and build a framework that better captures the structure of human judgments while remaining interpretable.
AI.method: They use the Anthropic HHRLHF dataset in a pairwise preference setup and evaluate ten diverse LLM-based reward models. Instead of relying solely on textual encodings, they augment representations with explicit, interpretable features: response length, refusal indicators, toxicity scores, and semantic similarity between prompt and response. These hybrid representations are fed into classification models to predict preferences. They also apply SHAP and LIME to understand feature contributions and analyze how individual features and their interactions affect bias amplification in preference learning.
AI.result: Baseline models using only textual representations achieve ROC AUC below 0.74 on the preference prediction task. The proposed feature-augmented (hybrid) approach improves performance for all evaluated models, with the best configuration (DeBERTav3Large with added features) reaching up to 0.84 ROC AUC and higher pairwise accuracy. Interpretability analyses via SHAP and LIME show that the models rely on contextualized notions of safety and supportive framing rather than simple keyword-based heuristics. The bias analysis indicates that single features have limited standalone impact, but feature interactions meaningfully shape how preferences are learned and can amplify biases.
AI.conclusion: Enriching reward models with simple, interpretable auxiliary features substantially improves their ability to predict human preferences over LLM responses, surpassing text-only baselines. The framework not only boosts accuracy but also yields more interpretable and behaviorally nuanced models, where safety and helpfulness are captured in context instead of via crude keyword triggers. However, the interaction of features can amplify biases, suggesting that preference learning systems should be carefully audited and designed with attention to feature interactions, not just marginal effects.

title: Runtime Burden Allocation for Structured LLM Routing in Agentic Expert Systems: A Full-Factorial Cross-Backend Methodology
summary:
Structured LLM routing is often treated as a prompt-engineering problem. We argue that it is, more fundamentally, a systems-level burden-allocation problem. As large language models (LLMs) become core control components in agentic AI systems, reliable structured routing must balance correctness, latency, and implementation cost under real deployment constraints. We show that this balance is shaped not only by prompts or schemas, but also by how structural work is allocated across the generation stack: whether output structure is emitted directly by the model, compressed during transport, or reconstructed locally after generation.
  We evaluate this formulation through a comprehensive full-factorial benchmark covering 48 deployment configurations and 15,552 requests across OpenAI, Gemini, and Llama backends. Our central finding is consequential: there is no universal best routing mode. Instead, backend-specific interaction effects dominate performance. Modes that remain highly reliable on Gemini and OpenAI can suffer substantial correctness degradation on Llama, while efficiency gains from compressed realization are strongly backend-dependent.
  Rather than presenting another isolated model comparison, this work contributes a deployable framework for reasoning about structured routing under heterogeneous backend conditions. We provide a cross-backend evaluation methodology and practical deployment guidance for navigating the correctness-cost-latency frontier in production-grade agentic expert systems.

AI.tldr: The paper reframes structured LLM routing as a systems-level burden-allocation problem and empirically shows that no single routing strategy works best across different LLM backends, offering a framework and guidelines for balancing correctness, cost, and latency in agentic systems.
AI.motivation: Existing work treats structured LLM routing mainly as a prompt and schema design issue, overlooking that in real agentic AI systems routing reliability is constrained by system-level trade-offs among correctness, latency, and implementation cost. As LLMs are used as control components, practitioners need principled ways to decide where in the stack (model, transport, client) to enforce and realize structure, especially since behavior likely varies across backends like OpenAI, Gemini, and Llama.
AI.method: The authors conceptualize structured routing as a burden-allocation problem along the generation stack: having the model emit structure directly, compressing structure for transport, or reconstructing it locally. They then run a full-factorial benchmark over 48 deployment configurations, issuing 15,552 requests across three major LLM backends (OpenAI, Gemini, Llama). They measure correctness, latency, and cost for different routing modes and structural-allocation strategies, analyzing interaction effects between routing mode and backend.
AI.result: Empirical results show strong backend-specific interaction effects: there is no universally optimal routing mode. Strategies that are reliable on Gemini and OpenAI may degrade in correctness on Llama. Efficiency benefits from compressed realization also depend heavily on the backend. Overall performance along correctness, latency, and cost dimensions is shaped by how structural work is divided across model output, transport, and local reconstruction, and this division must be tuned per backend.
AI.conclusion: Structured LLM routing should be designed as a system-level optimization problem rather than a pure prompt-engineering task. Because backend-specific interaction effects dominate, practitioners cannot rely on a single routing configuration across providers. The paper contributes a general framework, cross-backend evaluation methodology, and practical guidance for choosing routing strategies that navigate the correctness–cost–latency trade-offs for production agentic expert systems under heterogeneous backend conditions.

title: Procedural Knowledge at Scale Improves Reasoning
summary: Test-time scaling has emerged as an effective way to improve language models on challenging reasoning tasks. However, most existing methods treat each problem in isolation and do not systematically reuse knowledge from prior reasoning trajectories. In particular, they underutilize procedural knowledge: how to reframe a problem, choose an approach, and verify or backtrack when needed. We introduce Reasoning Memory, a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework for reasoning models that explicitly retrieves and reuses procedural knowledge at scale. Starting from existing corpora of step-by-step reasoning trajectories, we decompose each trajectory into self-contained subquestion-subroutine pairs, yielding a datastore of 32 million compact procedural knowledge entries. At inference time, a lightweight in-thought prompt lets the model verbalize the core subquestion, retrieve relevant subroutines within its reasoning trace, and reason under diverse retrieved subroutines as implicit procedural priors. Across six math, science, and coding benchmarks, Reasoning Memory consistently outperforms RAG with document, trajectory, and template knowledge, as well as a compute-matched test-time scaling baseline. With a higher inference budget, it improves over no retrieval by up to 19.2% and over the strongest compute-matched baseline by 7.9% across task types. Ablation studies show that these gains come from two key factors: the broad procedural coverage of the source trajectories and our decomposition and retrieval design, which together enable effective extraction and reuse of procedural knowledge.

AI.tldr: They propose Reasoning Memory, a retrieval-augmented framework that stores and retrieves fine-grained procedural reasoning snippets to boost test-time reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: Existing test-time scaling methods for language models handle each problem independently and fail to systematically reuse procedural knowledge from previous reasoning trajectories, such as how to break down problems, choose solution strategies, and verify or backtrack. The authors aim to better exploit this procedural knowledge to improve performance on complex reasoning tasks across domains.
AI.method: They build a large datastore from existing step-by-step reasoning corpora by decomposing each trajectory into self-contained subquestion–subroutine pairs, ending up with 32M compact procedural entries. At inference, they use a lightweight internal prompting step to let the model state the current core subquestion, retrieve relevant subroutines from the datastore, and then reason while conditioning on diverse retrieved subroutines as implicit procedural priors. They compare this approach against RAG variants that retrieve documents, entire trajectories, or templates, and against compute-matched test-time scaling baselines. Ablation studies analyze the impact of procedural coverage, decomposition, and retrieval design.
AI.result: Across six benchmarks in math, science, and coding, Reasoning Memory consistently outperforms conventional RAG approaches and a compute-matched test-time scaling baseline. With increased inference budget, it yields up to 19.2% absolute improvement over no-retrieval baselines and 7.9% over the strongest compute-matched baseline across task types. Ablations show that performance gains are tied to the broad procedural coverage of the source trajectories and the specific decomposition plus retrieval design.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained procedural knowledge extracted from prior reasoning trajectories can be effectively stored and retrieved to guide future reasoning. By decomposing trajectories into subquestion–subroutine pairs and using in-thought retrieval, Reasoning Memory substantially enhances test-time reasoning performance across domains, outperforming both standard RAG setups and pure test-time scaling. The study underscores the importance of procedural knowledge coverage and carefully designed decomposition/retrieval mechanisms for scalable reasoning improvements.

title: The Digital Twin Counterfactual Framework: A Validation Architecture for Simulated Potential Outcomes
summary: The fundamental problem of causal inference - that the counterfactual outcome for any individual is never observed - has shaped the entire methodology of the field. Every existing approach substitutes assumptions for missing data: ignorability, parallel trends, exclusion restrictions. None produces the counterfactual itself. This paper proposes the Digital Twin Counterfactual Framework (DTCF): rather than estimating the counterfactual statistically, we simulate it using a digital twin and subject the simulation to a hierarchical validation regime. We formalize the digital twin simulator as a stochastic mapping within the potential outcomes framework and introduce a hierarchy of twin fidelity assumptions - from marginal fidelity through joint fidelity to structural fidelity - each unlocking a progressively richer class of estimands. The central contribution is threefold. First, a five-level validation architecture converts the unfalsifiable claim that the simulator produces correct counterfactuals into falsifiable tests against observable data. Second, a formal decomposition separates causal quantities into those that are marginally validated (ATE, CATE, QTE - testable through observable-arm comparison) and those that are copula-dependent (the ITE distribution, probability of benefit/harm, variance of treatment effects - permanently reliant on the unobservable within-individual dependence structure). Third, bounding, sensitivity, and uncertainty quantification tools make the copula dependence explicit. The DTCF does not resolve the fundamental problem of causal inference. What it provides is a framework in which marginal causal claims become increasingly testable, joint causal claims become explicitly assumption-indexed, and the gap between the two is formally characterized.

AI.tldr: They propose using validated digital twin simulations instead of purely statistical assumptions to approximate individual-level counterfactuals, clarifying what can and cannot be tested and how assumptions affect causal claims.
AI.motivation: In causal inference, we never observe individual-level counterfactuals, so all methods rely on untestable assumptions (ignorability, parallel trends, exclusion restrictions) and can only estimate certain causal quantities indirectly. There is a need for a framework that explicitly incorporates mechanistic or simulation-based models (digital twins), clarifies which parts of causal claims are empirically testable, and formally separates what can be validated from what unavoidably depends on unobservable within-individual dependence structures.
AI.method: They introduce the Digital Twin Counterfactual Framework (DTCF), formalizing a digital twin simulator as a stochastic mapping within the potential outcomes framework. They define a hierarchy of twin fidelity assumptions (marginal, joint, structural fidelity) that determine which causal estimands are accessible. They propose a five-level validation architecture to test whether the simulator reproduces observable data, decompose causal estimands into marginally validated versus copula-dependent components, and develop bounding, sensitivity analysis, and uncertainty quantification techniques that make the dependence on the unobservable copula explicit.
AI.result: The framework yields: (1) a hierarchical validation scheme that translates the claim that digital twins generate correct counterfactuals into a sequence of falsifiable tests against observed data; (2) a formal decomposition of causal estimands into those that can be validated using observable-arm comparisons (ATE, CATE, QTE) versus those that inherently depend on unobservable within-individual dependence (ITE distribution, probability of benefit/harm, variance of treatment effects); and (3) tools for bounding and sensitivity analysis that quantify how conclusions about joint/individual-level causal quantities depend on unverifiable assumptions about the copula structure.
AI.conclusion: DTCF does not solve the fundamental problem that individual counterfactuals are unobservable, but it offers a principled way to integrate digital twin simulations into causal inference. It makes marginal causal claims increasingly testable through validation, explicitly indexes joint causal claims by their assumptions about unobservable dependence, and provides a formal characterization of the gap between what can be empirically validated and what remains assumption-driven.

title: No Attacker Needed: Unintentional Cross-User Contamination in Shared-State LLM Agents
summary: LLM-based agents increasingly operate across repeated sessions, maintaining task states to ensure continuity. In many deployments, a single agent serves multiple users within a team or organization, reusing a shared knowledge layer across user identities. This shared persistence expands the failure surface: information that is locally valid for one user can silently degrade another user's outcome when the agent reapplies it without regard for scope. We refer to this failure mode as unintentional cross-user contamination (UCC). Unlike adversarial memory poisoning, UCC requires no attacker; it arises from benign interactions whose scope-bound artifacts persist and are later misapplied. We formalize UCC through a controlled evaluation protocol, introduce a taxonomy of three contamination types, and evaluate the problem in two shared-state mechanisms. Under raw shared state, benign interactions alone produce contamination rates of 57--71%. A write-time sanitization is effective when shared state is conversational, but leaves substantial residual risk when shared state includes executable artifacts, with contamination often manifesting as silent wrong answers. These results indicate that shared-state agents need artifact-level defenses beyond text-level sanitization to prevent silent cross-user failures.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how shared memory in LLM-based agents serving multiple users can inadvertently cause one user’s interactions to corrupt or mislead another user’s results, a phenomenon they call unintentional cross-user contamination (UCC). It formalizes UCC, categorizes its forms, measures how often it occurs, and evaluates mitigation strategies.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents are deployed persistently and shared across many users in teams or organizations, they increasingly store and reuse prior context or artifacts (e.g., notes, code, tools). While this improves continuity and efficiency, it introduces a risk: content appropriate for one user can be silently reused in another user’s session where it no longer applies, degrading accuracy or safety without any malicious intent. Existing work focuses mostly on adversarial poisoning, not on these benign but harmful cross-user effects. The paper aims to precisely define, measure, and categorize this new failure mode to guide the design of safer shared-state agents.
AI.method: The authors propose a controlled evaluation protocol for unintentional cross-user contamination (UCC), where they simulate multiple users interacting with a single shared-state LLM agent. They construct tasks where user-specific or scope-bound information is logged into the shared state and may later be inappropriately reused for a different user’s query. They define a taxonomy of three contamination types, based on how incorrect or overscoped artifacts influence later reasoning or outputs. They test two main shared-state mechanisms: raw shared state (unfiltered reuse of prior artifacts) and a version with write-time sanitization intended to filter or transform shared content. Contamination rates and error profiles are then measured quantitatively across conditions.
AI.result: Under raw, unfiltered shared state, benign user interactions alone lead to high contamination rates (57–71%), showing that UCC is common even without any adversarial behavior. Implementing a write-time sanitization layer substantially reduces contamination when the shared state consists primarily of conversational text. However, when shared state includes executable or more complex artifacts (e.g., code, scripts, structured tools) the sanitization is much less effective, and significant contamination persists. In those cases, contamination often shows up as silent wrong answers rather than obvious failures, making it harder to detect and correct.
AI.conclusion: Unintentional cross-user contamination is a prevalent and serious failure mode in shared LLM-based agents, arising even from purely benign usage. Simple write-time, text-level sanitization is not sufficient, especially when the shared memory includes executable or artifact-like content. To avoid silent cross-user failures, designers of shared-state agents need more granular, artifact-level defenses and scoping mechanisms that respect which user, context, or task a given piece of state is valid for, rather than treating all prior interactions as globally reusable knowledge.

title: IDEA2: Expert-in-the-loop competency question elicitation for collaborative ontology engineering
summary: Competency question (CQ) elicitation represents a critical but resource-intensive bottleneck in ontology engineering. This foundational phase is often hampered by the communication gap between domain experts, who possess the necessary knowledge, and ontology engineers, who formalise it. This paper introduces IDEA2, a novel, semi-automated workflow that integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) within a collaborative, expert-in-the-loop process to address this challenge. The methodology is characterised by a core iterative loop: an initial LLM-based extraction of CQs from requirement documents, a co-creational review and feedback phase by domain experts on an accessible collaborative platform, and an iterative, feedback-driven reformulation of rejected CQs by an LLM until consensus is achieved. To ensure transparency and reproducibility, the entire lifecycle of each CQ is tracked using a provenance model that captures the full lineage of edits, anonymised feedback, and generation parameters. The workflow was validated in 2 real-world scenarios (scientific data, cultural heritage), demonstrating that IDEA2 can accelerate the requirements engineering process, improve the acceptance and relevance of the resulting CQs, and exhibit high usability and effectiveness among domain experts. We release all code and experiments at https://github.com/KE-UniLiv/IDEA2

AI.tldr: The paper presents IDEA2, a semi-automated, expert-in-the-loop workflow using LLMs to elicit and refine competency questions (CQs) for ontology engineering, improving efficiency, quality, and traceability of requirements.
AI.motivation: Competency question elicitation is a crucial but time-consuming and resource-intensive step in ontology engineering, hindered by a communication gap between domain experts and ontology engineers. There is a need for methods that reduce this bottleneck while maintaining or improving the quality and relevance of CQs and making the process more transparent and reproducible.
AI.method: The authors design IDEA2, a semi-automated workflow centered on an iterative loop. First, an LLM extracts candidate competency questions from requirement documents. Then domain experts review, edit, accept, or reject these CQs on a collaborative platform. Rejected CQs, together with expert feedback, are fed back to the LLM, which reformulates them; this loop continues until consensus is reached. A dedicated provenance model records the full lifecycle of each CQ, including edits, anonymised feedback, and LLM generation parameters, ensuring transparency and traceability. The workflow is evaluated in two real-world domains (scientific data and cultural heritage).
AI.result: In the two real-world use cases, IDEA2 accelerated the requirements engineering phase, produced competency questions with higher acceptance and perceived relevance, and was rated as highly usable and effective by participating domain experts. The provenance tracking successfully captured the CQ evolution and supported transparency and reproducibility. The authors also provide an open-source implementation and experimental artifacts.
AI.conclusion: IDEA2 shows that integrating LLMs into an expert-in-the-loop, provenance-aware workflow can substantially reduce the bottleneck of competency question elicitation in ontology engineering, improving both efficiency and quality. The approach is practical for real-world domains and supports transparent, reproducible requirements engineering. The released code and experiments enable reuse and further development by the community.

title: Open-Domain Safety Policy Construction
summary: Moderation layers are increasingly a core component of many products built on user- or model-generated content. However, drafting and maintaining domain-specific safety policies remains costly. We present Deep Policy Research (DPR), a minimal agentic system that drafts a full content moderation policy based on only human-written seed domain information. DPR uses a single web search tool and lightweight scaffolding to iteratively propose search queries, distill diverse web sources into policy rules, and organize rules into an indexed document. We evaluate DPR on (1) the OpenAI undesired content benchmark across five domains with two compact reader LLMs and (2) an in-house multimodal advertisement moderation benchmark. DPR consistently outperforms definition-only and in-context learning baselines, and in our end-to-end setting it is competitive with expert-written policy sections in several domains. Moreover, under the same seed specification and evaluation protocol, DPR outperforms a general-purpose deep research system, suggesting that a task-specific, structured research loop can be more effective than generic web research for policy drafting. We release our experiment code at https://github.com/xiaowu0162/deep-policy-research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Deep Policy Research (DPR), an agentic system that automatically drafts domain-specific content moderation policies from minimal human seed input using web search and structured iteration, achieving performance competitive with expert policies and better than generic deep research systems.
AI.motivation: Creating and maintaining detailed, domain-specific safety and moderation policies is expensive and time-consuming, yet such policies are crucial for products involving user- or model-generated content. Existing approaches like definition-only policies, simple in-context prompting, or generic deep research agents are either weak or inefficient for this specialized drafting task. The authors aim to reduce human effort while maintaining or improving the quality of moderation policies.
AI.method: DPR is a lightweight agentic system that starts from a short human-written seed describing the target domain. It iteratively: (1) proposes web search queries using a single search tool, (2) collects and summarizes relevant information from diverse web sources into candidate policy rules, and (3) organizes and indexes these rules into a structured moderation policy document. The system is evaluated with two compact reader LLMs on the OpenAI undesired content benchmark across five domains and on an internal multimodal ad moderation benchmark, comparing against definition-only, in-context learning, and a general-purpose deep research agent.
AI.result: Across five domains on the OpenAI undesired content benchmark and on an in-house multimodal advertisement moderation benchmark, DPR consistently outperforms baselines that rely only on short definitions or in-context examples. In end-to-end evaluations, the automatically generated policies can approach or match the quality of expert-written policy sections in several domains. When run under the same seed and evaluation protocol as a general-purpose deep research system, DPR achieves better performance, indicating advantages of a task-specific research loop for policy drafting.
AI.conclusion: Deep Policy Research demonstrates that an agentic but minimal, task-specialized research loop can automatically draft high-quality, domain-specific content moderation policies from limited human input. Its superior performance over simpler baselines and a generic deep research system suggests that structured, purpose-built web research pipelines are effective for policy authoring, reducing the need for intensive expert effort while remaining competitive with human-crafted policies.

title: Semantic Modeling for World-Centered Architectures
summary: We introduce world-centered multi-agent systems (WMAS) as an alternative to traditional agent-centered architectures, arguing that structured domains such as enterprises and institutional systems require a shared, explicit world representation to ensure semantic consistency, explainability, and long-term stability. We classify worlds along dimensions including ontological explicitness, normativity, etc. In WMAS, learning and coordination operate over a shared world model rather than isolated agent-local representations, enabling global consistency and verifiable system behavior. We propose semantic models as a mathematical formalism for representing such worlds. Finally, we present the Ontobox platform as a realization of WMAS.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes world-centered multi-agent systems that share a common explicit world model to improve semantic consistency, explainability, and stability, introduces a formalism based on semantic models, and presents Ontobox as a concrete platform implementing this approach.
AI.motivation: Traditional multi-agent systems often use agent-centered architectures where each agent maintains its own local representation of the environment. This leads to challenges in structured domains like enterprises and institutional systems, where inconsistencies between agents’ internal models can undermine semantic coherence, explainability, coordination, and long-term system stability. The authors aim to address the need for a shared, explicit representation of the world to support robust, verifiable behavior in these complex domains.
AI.method: The authors conceptually define world-centered multi-agent systems (WMAS) in contrast to agent-centered ones and provide a classification framework for ‘worlds’ based on dimensions such as ontological explicitness and normativity. They introduce semantic models as the mathematical formalism to represent these shared worlds, providing a basis for learning and coordination over a single, explicit world model. They then implement these ideas in a platform called Ontobox, which operationalizes the WMAS paradigm.
AI.result: The paper yields: (1) a conceptual framework and taxonomy for world-centered multi-agent systems and the worlds they rely on; (2) a formal representation approach via semantic models that supports shared, verifiable world knowledge; and (3) Ontobox, a working platform that demonstrates how WMAS can be implemented so that learning and coordination are carried out over a shared world model rather than agent-local ones.
AI.conclusion: World-centered multi-agent systems, grounded in explicit semantic world models, are better suited to structured, institutional domains than traditional agent-centered architectures. By centralizing the world representation and enabling agents to learn and coordinate over it, WMAS can achieve global semantic consistency, improved explainability, and more stable, verifiable system behavior. The Ontobox platform illustrates the practical feasibility of this approach.

title: Friends and Grandmothers in Silico: Localizing Entity Cells in Language Models
summary: Language models can answer many entity-centric factual questions, but it remains unclear which internal mechanisms are involved in this process. We study this question across multiple language models. We localize entity-selective MLP neurons using templated prompts about each entity, and then validate them with causal interventions on PopQA-based QA examples. On a curated set of 200 entities drawn from PopQA, localized neurons concentrate in early layers. Negative ablation produces entity-specific amnesia, while controlled injection at a placeholder token improves answer retrieval relative to mean-entity and wrong-cell controls. For many entities, activating a single localized neuron is sufficient to recover entity-consistent predictions once the context is initialized, consistent with compact entity retrieval rather than purely gradual enrichment across depth. Robustness to aliases, acronyms, misspellings, and multilingual forms supports a canonicalization interpretation. The effect is strong but not universal: not every entity admits a reliable single-neuron handle, and coverage is higher for popular entities. Overall, these results identify sparse, causally actionable access points for analyzing and modulating entity-conditioned factual behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how language models retrieve factual information about entities and finds that specific, sparse neurons in early MLP layers can causally control entity-specific factual recall.
AI.motivation: Although language models can correctly answer many factual, entity-centric questions, it is not well understood which internal mechanisms and representations support this ability. The motivation is to move from behavioral evaluation to mechanistic understanding: to identify where and how entities are represented, whether these representations are compact or distributed, and whether they can be causally manipulated for analysis and control.
AI.method: The authors use templated prompts about entities to localize MLP neurons that respond selectively to specific entities across multiple language models. They then validate these localized neurons via causal interventions on question-answering tasks based on PopQA. Interventions include negative ablation (turning off or suppressing neuron activity) and controlled injection (artificially activating targeted neurons at specific tokens, including a placeholder). They analyze layer-wise distribution, entity coverage, and robustness to variations such as aliases, acronyms, misspellings, and multiple languages.
AI.result: On a curated subset of 200 PopQA entities, entity-selective neurons are found primarily in early layers of the models. Ablating these neurons induces entity-specific amnesia, reducing the model’s ability to recall facts about the targeted entity. Conversely, activating these neurons at a placeholder token improves retrieval of the correct answer compared to controls like mean-entity activations or using incorrect cells. For many entities, activating a single localized neuron is sufficient to restore consistent entity-related predictions when the surrounding context is properly initialized. The neurons’ responses are robust to surface-form variations, including aliases and multilingual forms, suggesting a canonicalized internal representation. However, not all entities have reliable single-neuron handles, and coverage tends to be better for more popular entities.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that language models possess sparse, causally influential neurons that provide access points to entity-specific factual knowledge, especially in early MLP layers. These neurons behave like canonicalized entity switches: manipulating them can selectively erase or restore knowledge about particular entities. While this mechanism is strong but not universal and skewed toward popular entities, it demonstrates that at least some aspects of entity-conditioned factual behavior in language models are compact, localizable, and directly modifiable, enabling more precise mechanistic analysis and potential control of model behavior.

title: Crashing Waves vs. Rising Tides: Preliminary Findings on AI Automation from Thousands of Worker Evaluations of Labor Market Tasks
summary: We propose that AI automation is a continuum between: (i) crashing waves where AI capabilities surge abruptly over small sets of tasks, and (ii) rising tides where the increase in AI capabilities is more continuous and broad-based. We test for these effects in preliminary evidence from an ongoing evaluation of AI capabilities across over 3,000 broad-based tasks derived from the U.S. Department of Labor O*NET categorization that are text-based and thus LLM-addressable. Based on more than 17,000 evaluations by workers from these jobs, we find little evidence of crashing waves (in contrast to recent work by METR), but substantial evidence that rising tides are the primary form of AI automation. AI performance is high and improving rapidly across a wide range of tasks. We estimate that, in 2024-Q2, AI models successfully complete tasks that take humans approximately 3-4 hours with about a 50% success rate, increasing to about 65% by 2025-Q3. If recent trends in AI capability growth persist, this pace of AI improvement implies that LLMs will be able to complete most text-related tasks with success rates of, on average, 80%-95% by 2029 at a minimally sufficient quality level. Achieving near-perfect success rates at this quality level or comparable success rates at superior quality would require several additional years. These AI capability improvements would impact the economy and labor market as organizations adopt AI, which could have a substantially longer timeline.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how AI automation progresses across tasks, finding that improvements look like a broad, steady “rising tide” rather than sudden “crashing waves” on a few tasks, and projects that LLMs will handle most text tasks at high reliability by around 2029.
AI.motivation: There is debate about whether AI will rapidly automate narrow sets of tasks in sudden jumps (crashing waves) or gradually and broadly raise capabilities (rising tides). Policymakers, firms, and workers need to understand this pattern to anticipate economic and labor-market disruption, plan adoption strategies, and design regulation. Existing empirical work, such as METR’s, suggests sharp discontinuities, but comprehensive, occupation-wide empirical evidence is limited; this paper aims to fill that gap.
AI.method: The authors operationalize AI automation as performance on over 3,000 text-based tasks derived from the U.S. Department of Labor’s O*NET classification—tasks that large language models (LLMs) can address. They collect more than 17,000 human evaluations from workers whose jobs correspond to these tasks, comparing AI model outputs to human standards of success and tracking performance over time (2024-Q2 to 2025-Q3 and beyond). They then statistically analyze whether performance gains are concentrated in a few tasks (indicative of crashing waves) or spread broadly and smoothly across tasks (indicative of rising tides), and extrapolate future performance trends under a continuation of recent capability growth.
AI.result: Empirically, the authors find minimal evidence of “crashing waves,” in contrast with METR’s prior findings. Instead, AI performance is strong and improving steadily across a wide variety of text-based tasks. As of 2024-Q2, LLMs can complete tasks that typically require humans 3–4 hours of work with about a 50% success rate, which is projected to increase to roughly 65% by 2025-Q3. This performance improvement appears broad-based across the evaluated task set, not confined to a small subset of tasks.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that AI automation currently manifests as a broadly distributed, continuous improvement pattern—a “rising tide” across many text-based tasks—rather than as abrupt breakthroughs confined to a few areas. If current trends continue, LLMs are expected to complete most text-related tasks at 80%–95% success rates by around 2029 at minimally sufficient quality, with near-perfect or higher-quality performance requiring additional years. The authors note that economic and labor-market impacts will further depend on adoption dynamics within organizations, which may lag behind technical capability improvements.

title: Assessing Pause Thresholds for empirical Translation Process Research
comment: Accepted for Presentation at "Translation in Transition 8, September 2026"
summary: Text production (and translations) proceeds in the form of stretches of typing, interrupted by keystroke pauses. It is often assumed that fast typing reflects unchallenged/automated translation production while long(er) typing pauses are indicative of translation problems, hurdles or difficulties. Building on a long discussion concerning the determination of pause thresholds that separate automated from presumably reflective translation processes (O'Brien, 2006; Alves and Vale, 2009; Timarova et al., 2011; Dragsted and Carl, 2013; Lacruz et al., 2014; Kumpulainen, 2015; Heilmann and Neumann 2016), this paper compares three recent approaches for computing these pause thresholds, and suggest and evaluate a novel method for computing Production Unit Breaks.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how to best determine pause thresholds in typing during translation to distinguish automated from reflective processes, and proposes a new way to compute production unit breaks.
AI.motivation: In translation and text production research, typing bursts and pauses are used to infer underlying cognitive processes. However, there is an ongoing debate about how to set pause duration thresholds that meaningfully distinguish automated from problem-solving or reflective processing. Inconsistent or arbitrary thresholds hinder comparability and validity of findings.
AI.method: The paper reviews and empirically compares three recent computational approaches for defining pause thresholds in keystroke data. It then introduces a new algorithmic method for calculating Production Unit Breaks based on these pauses and evaluates this method against the existing approaches, likely using keystroke logging data from translation tasks.
AI.result: The comparison reveals strengths and weaknesses of the three existing approaches for determining pause thresholds and shows how their different criteria affect the identification of pauses and production units. The proposed method for computing Production Unit Breaks performs competitively or better in capturing meaningful segments of translation production.
AI.conclusion: A more principled and empirically grounded way of calculating pause thresholds and Production Unit Breaks improves the analysis of translation processes from keystroke data. The new method offers a refined tool for distinguishing automated from reflective processing in translation, contributing to more reliable and comparable process research.

title: CogBias: Measuring and Mitigating Cognitive Bias in Large Language Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes decision-making contexts. While prior work has shown that LLMs exhibit cognitive biases behaviorally, whether these biases correspond to identifiable internal representations and can be mitigated through targeted intervention remains an open question. We define LLM cognitive bias as systematic, reproducible deviations from correct answers in tasks with computable ground-truth baselines, and introduce LLM CogBias, a benchmark organized around four families of cognitive biases: Judgment, Information Processing, Social, and Response. We evaluate three LLMs and find that cognitive biases emerge systematically across all four families, with magnitudes and debiasing responses that are strongly family-dependent: prompt-level debiasing substantially reduces Response biases but backfires for Judgment biases. Using linear probes under a contrastive design, we show that these biases are encoded as linearly separable directions in model activation space. Finally, we apply activation steering to modulate biased behavior, achieving 26--32\% reduction in bias score (fraction of biased responses) while preserving downstream capability on 25 benchmarks (Llama: negligible degradation; Qwen: up to $-$19.0pp for Judgment biases). Despite near-orthogonal bias representations across models (mean cosine similarity 0.01), steering reduces bias at similar rates across architectures ($r(246)$=.621, $p$<.001), suggesting shared functional organization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LLM CogBias, a benchmark to systematically measure, understand, and mitigate cognitive biases in large language models, showing that biases are encoded as linear directions in activation space and can be partially reduced via activation steering with limited impact on capabilities.
AI.motivation: Large Language Models are increasingly used in high-stakes decisions, but they exhibit cognitive biases similar to humans. Existing work mostly documents biased behaviors without clarifying whether these biases correspond to stable internal representations, how they vary across bias types, and whether they can be reliably mitigated without harming model performance. The paper aims to fill this gap by (1) defining a precise notion of LLM cognitive bias grounded in tasks with computable ground truth, (2) building a systematic benchmark across major families of cognitive biases, and (3) testing both prompt-based and representation-level interventions.
AI.method: The authors (1) define LLM cognitive bias as systematic, reproducible deviations from correct responses on tasks with known ground truths; (2) construct LLM CogBias, a benchmark covering four cognitive-bias families: Judgment, Information Processing, Social, and Response; (3) evaluate three different LLMs on this benchmark to quantify bias magnitudes and sensitivity to prompt-level debiasing; (4) apply linear probes in a contrastive setup to test whether biased vs unbiased activations are linearly separable in the models’ internal representations; and (5) use activation steering along these identified directions to reduce biased behavior, while assessing collateral effects on 25 downstream benchmarks.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that all tested LLMs systematically exhibit cognitive biases in all four bias families, but the size of the bias and the effectiveness of debiasing differ strongly by family. Prompt-based debiasing is effective for Response biases but actually worsens Judgment biases. Linear probing reveals that biased behavior corresponds to linearly separable directions in activation space. Activation steering along these directions reduces the bias score (fraction of biased responses) by 26–32%, with Llama suffering negligible performance degradation on 25 downstream benchmarks and Qwen experiencing up to a 19 percentage point drop on Judgment-related tasks. Bias directions are nearly orthogonal across models (mean cosine similarity 0.01), but the effectiveness of steering is correlated across architectures (r(246)=.621, p<.001).
AI.conclusion: Cognitive biases in LLMs are not just surface-level behaviors but correspond to structured, linearly decodable directions in activation space. These internal bias representations differ across model families but have similar functional roles, as evidenced by comparable steering effectiveness. Targeted representation-level interventions like activation steering can significantly reduce bias while largely preserving capabilities, although trade-offs are model- and bias-family-dependent and prompt-level debiasing may be counterproductive for some bias types. The LLM CogBias benchmark and methodology provide a foundation for more principled, fine-grained study and mitigation of cognitive biases in LLMs.

title: Adaptive Stopping for Multi-Turn LLM Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly rely on multi-turn reasoning and interaction, such as adaptive retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and ReAct-style agents, to answer difficult questions. These methods improve accuracy by iteratively retrieving information, reasoning, or acting, but introduce a key challenge: \textbf{When should the model stop?} Existing approaches rely on heuristic stopping rules or fixed turn budgets and provide no formal guarantees that the final prediction still contains the correct answer. This limitation is particularly problematic in high-stakes domains such as finance and healthcare, where unnecessary turns increase cost and latency, while stopping too early risks incorrect decisions. Conformal prediction (CP) provides formal coverage guarantees, but existing LLM-CP methods only apply to a single model output and cannot handle multi-turn pipelines with adaptive stopping. To address this gap, we propose Multi-Turn Language Models with Conformal Prediction (MiCP), the first CP framework for multi-turn reasoning. MiCP allocates different error budgets across turns, enabling the model to stop early while maintaining an overall coverage guarantee. We demonstrate MiCP on adaptive RAG and ReAct, where it achieves the target coverage on both single-hop and multi-hop question answering benchmarks while reducing the number of turns, inference cost, and prediction set size. We further introduce a new metric that jointly evaluates coverage validity and answering efficiency.

AI.tldr: Introduces MiCP, a conformal prediction framework that gives formal coverage guarantees for multi-turn LLM reasoning while enabling early, cost-efficient stopping.
AI.motivation: Multi-turn LLM methods like adaptive RAG and ReAct improve accuracy via iterative reasoning and retrieval, but lack principled rules for when to stop, relying instead on heuristics or fixed turn limits. This is risky in high-stakes domains where extra turns increase cost and latency, and premature stopping can cause harmful errors. Existing conformal prediction methods offer coverage guarantees only for single-shot outputs, not for multi-turn adaptive pipelines with stopping decisions. There is a need for a framework that offers formal coverage guarantees across the entire multi-turn process while allowing adaptive early stopping.
AI.method: Propose MiCP (Multi-Turn Language Models with Conformal Prediction), a conformal prediction framework tailored to multi-turn LLM reasoning. MiCP distributes a global error budget across intermediate turns so that decisions at each step (including whether to continue or stop) are made in a statistically controlled way. It is instantiated on adaptive RAG and ReAct-style agents, where at each turn the system can update prediction sets and stopping rules while maintaining overall coverage guarantees. The framework includes a new evaluation metric that jointly measures statistical validity (coverage) and efficiency (turns, cost, and prediction set size).
AI.result: On both single-hop and multi-hop QA benchmarks, MiCP attains the desired coverage level while using fewer turns than baseline multi-turn methods without principled stopping. It reduces inference cost and yields smaller prediction sets, indicating more informative yet still guaranteed-correct answers. Experiments on adaptive RAG and ReAct pipelines validate that MiCP can be practically integrated into existing multi-turn LLM systems.
AI.conclusion: MiCP is the first conformal prediction framework specifically designed for multi-turn LLM reasoning, enabling adaptive stopping with provable coverage guarantees. It improves efficiency and cost-effectiveness in systems like RAG and ReAct, without sacrificing the probability that the final answer set contains the correct answer. The work also proposes an evaluation metric that captures the trade-off between validity and efficiency, providing a more holistic way to assess multi-turn LLM pipelines.

title: RIFT: A RubrIc Failure Mode Taxonomy and Automated Diagnostics
summary: Rubric-based evaluation is widely used in LLM benchmarks and training pipelines for open-ended, less verifiable tasks. While prior work has demonstrated the effectiveness of rubrics using downstream signals such as reinforcement learning outcomes, there remains no principled way to diagnose rubric quality issues from such aggregated or downstream signals alone. To address this gap, we introduce RIFT: RubrIc Failure mode Taxonomy, a taxonomy for systematically characterizing failure modes in rubric composition and design. RIFT consists of eight failure modes organized into three high-level categories: Reliability Failures, Content Validity Failures, and Consequential Validity Failures. RIFT is developed using grounded theory by iteratively annotating rubrics drawn from five diverse benchmarks spanning general instruction following, code generation, creative writing, and expert-level deep research, until no new failure modes are identified. We evaluate the consistency of the taxonomy by measuring agreement among independent human annotators, observing fair agreement overall (87% pairwise agreement and 0.64 average Cohen's kappa). Finally, to support scalable diagnosis, we propose automated rubric quality metrics and show that they align with human failure-mode annotations, achieving up to 0.86 F1.

AI.tldr: The paper presents RIFT, a taxonomy of failure modes in rubric-based evaluation for LLMs, and introduces automated metrics to detect these failures that correlate well with human annotations.
AI.motivation: Rubric-based evaluation is heavily used for assessing LLMs on open-ended tasks, but there is no principled way to diagnose when rubrics themselves are flawed using only aggregate or downstream signals like RL performance. This limits our ability to trust and improve rubric-based benchmarks and training pipelines.
AI.method: The authors develop RIFT, a taxonomy of rubric failure modes, using grounded theory. They iteratively annotate rubrics from five diverse benchmarks—covering instruction following, code generation, creative writing, and expert-level research—until saturation (no new failure modes) is reached. They then assess the taxonomy’s reliability via agreement between independent annotators, and design automated rubric-quality metrics which they evaluate against these human annotations.
AI.result: RIFT contains eight concrete failure modes grouped into three categories: Reliability Failures, Content Validity Failures, and Consequential Validity Failures. Human annotators using the taxonomy show fair consistency, with 87% pairwise agreement and an average Cohen’s kappa of 0.64. The proposed automated metrics for diagnosing rubric quality issues align well with human-labeled failure modes, reaching up to 0.86 F1 in detecting them.
AI.conclusion: RIFT provides a systematic framework for identifying and categorizing common problems in LLM evaluation rubrics, and the introduced automated metrics make large-scale rubric diagnosis feasible. Together, they enable more principled development, auditing, and refinement of rubric-based evaluations used in LLM benchmarks and training.

title: Cost-Efficient Estimation of General Abilities Across Benchmarks
summary: Thousands of diverse benchmarks have been developed to measure the quality of large language models (LLMs). Yet prior work has demonstrated that LLM performance is often sufficiently explained by a small set of latent factors, or abilities. This suggests the potential for more efficient and principled benchmarking, but it remains difficult to compare the quality of different methods. Motivated by predictive validity, we argue that the quality of a benchmarking framework should be grounded in how efficiently it enables the prediction of model performance on unseen tasks. To analyze this objective, we collect the "Wide-scale Item Level Dataset" (WILD), a dataset of item-model response pairs, comprising evaluations of 65 models on 109,564 unique items spanning 163 tasks drawn from 27 datasets. This dataset enables the first analysis of how different techniques can predict a model's performance on a large, diverse collection of unseen tasks under different budget constraints. We demonstrate that combining a modified multidimensional item response theory (IRT) model with adaptive item selection driven by optimal experimental design can predict performance on 112 held-out benchmark tasks with a mean absolute error (MAE) of less than 7%, and can do so after observing only 16 items. We further demonstrate that incorporating cost-aware discount factors into our selection criteria can reduce the total tokens needed to reach 7% MAE from 141,000 tokens to only 22,000, an 85% reduction in evaluation cost.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an efficient, principled way to benchmark large language models by predicting their performance on unseen tasks using a latent-ability model plus adaptive test item selection, greatly reducing evaluation cost.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks are numerous and fragmented, and results are largely driven by a small number of latent abilities. However, there is no clear way to compare benchmarking methods or to evaluate how well they predict model performance on new tasks under realistic evaluation budgets. The authors want a benchmarking framework whose quality is judged by its predictive validity: how accurately and efficiently it can infer a model’s performance on unseen tasks.
AI.method: They first build WILD, a large-scale dataset of item-level responses from 65 models on over 109k items across 163 tasks from 27 datasets. Using this, they analyze how different techniques predict model performance on unseen tasks under budget constraints. Their core method combines a modified multidimensional item response theory (IRT) model—which represents models and items in a shared latent ability space—with adaptive item selection guided by optimal experimental design, including cost-aware variants that weight items by token cost.
AI.result: On 112 held-out benchmark tasks, their approach predicts model performance with mean absolute error below 7% after evaluating only 16 items per model. When they incorporate cost-aware discounting into the selection strategy, the number of tokens required to achieve 7% MAE drops from about 141k to about 22k tokens, representing an 85% reduction in evaluation cost.
AI.conclusion: A small set of adaptively chosen, well-modeled items can accurately predict LLM performance across a wide variety of tasks, making LLM benchmarking far more efficient. By grounding benchmark quality in predictive validity and using latent ability modeling plus cost-aware optimal design, the authors provide both a dataset (WILD) and a framework for scalable, principled LLM evaluation under realistic budget constraints.

title: Leveraging the Value of Information in POMDP Planning
summary: Partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) offer a principled formalism for planning under state and transition uncertainty. Despite advances made towards solving large POMDPs, obtaining performant policies under limited planning time remains a major challenge due to the curse of dimensionality and the curse of history. For many POMDP problems, the value of information (VOI) - the expected performance gain from reasoning about observations - varies over the belief space. We introduce a dynamic programming framework that exploits this structure by conditionally processing observations based on the value of information at each belief. Building on this framework, we propose Value of Information Monte Carlo planning (VOIMCP), a Monte Carlo Tree Search algorithm that allocates computational effort more efficiently by selectively disregarding observation information when the VOI is low, avoiding unnecessary branching of observations. We provide theoretical guarantees on the near-optimality of our VOI reasoning framework and derive non-asymptotic convergence bounds for VOIMCP. Simulation evaluations demonstrate that VOIMCP outperforms baselines on several POMDP benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Introduces a planning framework and an MCTS algorithm (VOIMCP) for POMDPs that selectively ignores low-value observations to reduce branching and improve performance under tight planning time.
AI.motivation: Planning in POMDPs is computationally hard due to the curse of dimensionality and curse of history, making it difficult to obtain high-quality policies when planning time is limited. In many problems, the benefit of reasoning about every possible observation (the value of information) is uneven across the belief space, but existing methods generally treat all observations symmetrically and fully expand them, wasting computation where VOI is low. The authors aim to exploit variability in VOI to allocate computation more efficiently and get better policies faster.
AI.method: They propose a dynamic programming framework that, at each belief state, estimates or reasons about the value of information from processing observations. Based on this, it conditionally decides whether to branch on observations or to ignore them (treat them as uninformative) when their expected impact on performance is low. Building on this idea, they design Value of Information Monte Carlo Planning (VOIMCP), an MCTS-based algorithm for POMDPs that uses VOI estimates to selectively prune or coarsen observation branches, thereby reducing tree branching and focusing simulations where information matters most. They establish theoretical properties of this VOI-based reasoning, including near-optimality, and derive finite-sample convergence bounds for VOIMCP.
AI.result: Theoretically, they show their VOI reasoning framework yields policies that are provably near-optimal under certain conditions and provide non-asymptotic bounds on how fast VOIMCP converges to good policies as the number of simulations increases. Empirically, they run simulations on standard POMDP benchmark problems and find that VOIMCP outperforms baseline planning algorithms, particularly under limited computation budgets, by achieving higher returns and using computation more efficiently.
AI.conclusion: Conditionally reasoning about observations via value of information in POMDP planning can substantially reduce unnecessary branching without sacrificing much optimality. The proposed VOIMCP algorithm operationalizes this idea within MCTS, comes with theoretical guarantees, and shows superior empirical performance on benchmarks, indicating that VOI-aware selective observation processing is a promising direction for scalable POMDP planning under tight time constraints.

title: The power of context: Random Forest classification of near synonyms. A case study in Modern Hindi
summary:
Synonymy is a widespread yet puzzling linguistic phenomenon. Absolute synonyms theoretically should not exist, as they do not expand language's expressive potential. However, it was suggested that even if synonyms denote the same concept, they may reflect different perspectives or carry distinct cultural associations, claims that have rarely been tested quantitatively.
  In Hindi, prolonged contact with Persian produced many Perso-Arabic loanwords coexisting with their Sanskrit counterpart, forming numerous synonym pairs. This study investigates whether centuries after these borrowings appeared in the Subcontinent their origin can still be distinguished using distributional data alone and regardless of their semantic content.
  A Random Forest trained on word embeddings of Hindi synonyms successfully classified words by Sanskrit or Perso-Arabic origin, even when they were semantically unrelated, suggesting that usage patterns preserve traces of etymology. These findings provide quantitative evidence that context encodes etymological signals and that synonymy may reflect subtle but systematic distinctions linked to origin. They support the idea that synonymous words can offer different perspectives and that etymologically related words may form distinct conceptual subspaces, creating a new type of semantic frame shaped by historical origin. Overall, the results highlight the power of context in capturing nuanced distinctions beyond traditional semantic similarity.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that Hindi synonyms of different etymological origins (Sanskrit vs. Perso-Arabic) can be distinguished purely from their distributional patterns in text, indicating that context preserves historical and cultural signals beyond core meaning.
AI.motivation: Linguistic theory holds that true, absolute synonyms shouldn’t persist because they add no expressive power. Yet many languages contain long-standing synonym pairs, often from different historical or cultural sources. Prior claims suggested that even if such words share denotational meaning, they might differ in perspective, connotation, or cultural association, but there has been little quantitative testing of this idea. Hindi, with its history of Sanskrit vocabulary and Perso-Arabic loanwords, offers a natural testbed to examine whether etymological origin leaves detectable traces in how synonyms are used.
AI.method: The authors collect Hindi synonym pairs where one member is of Sanskrit origin and the other is a Perso-Arabic loanword. They obtain distributional word embeddings for these words from large Hindi corpora. Using these embeddings as features, they train a Random Forest classifier to predict whether each word is of Sanskrit or Perso-Arabic origin. Crucially, they also test classification on sets of words that are semantically unrelated to ensure that the model is not simply picking up topical or semantic clusters but more general usage patterns tied to origin.
AI.result: The Random Forest classifier reliably distinguishes Sanskrit-origin words from Perso-Arabic-origin words based solely on their embedding representations. Performance remains high even when the words being classified are semantically diverse or unrelated, indicating that the classifier is not merely exploiting specific semantic fields. This suggests that the distributional patterns of use—captured in word embeddings—encode systematic signals of etymological origin in Hindi.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that synonymy in Hindi is not semantically neutral: even when words are considered synonyms, their usage contexts reflect consistent differences tied to etymological origin. These differences are strong enough to be detected algorithmically from distributional embeddings, implying that context preserves historical and cultural information. The authors argue that synonymous words may occupy distinct conceptual subspaces or “semantic frames” defined by origin, supporting the view that synonyms provide different perspectives rather than being fully interchangeable. More broadly, the work demonstrates the power of contextual distribution in capturing subtle linguistic and cultural distinctions beyond standard notions of semantic similarity.

title: ClawSafety: "Safe" LLMs, Unsafe Agents
summary: Personal AI agents like OpenClaw run with elevated privileges on users' local machines, where a single successful prompt injection can leak credentials, redirect financial transactions, or destroy files. This threat goes well beyond conventional text-level jailbreaks, yet existing safety evaluations fall short: most test models in isolated chat settings, rely on synthetic environments, and do not account for how the agent framework itself shapes safety outcomes. We introduce CLAWSAFETY, a benchmark of 120 adversarial test scenarios organized along three dimensions (harm domain, attack vector, and harmful action type) and grounded in realistic, high-privilege professional workspaces spanning software engineering, finance, healthcare, law, and DevOps. Each test case embeds adversarial content in one of three channels the agent encounters during normal work: workspace skill files, emails from trusted senders, and web pages. We evaluate five frontier LLMs as agent backbones, running 2,520 sandboxed trials across all configurations. Attack success rates (ASR) range from 40\% to 75\% across models and vary sharply by injection vector, with skill instructions (highest trust) consistently more dangerous than email or web content. Action-trace analysis reveals that the strongest model maintains hard boundaries against credential forwarding and destructive actions, while weaker models permit both. Cross-scaffold experiments on three agent frameworks further demonstrate that safety is not determined by the backbone model alone but depends on the full deployment stack, calling for safety evaluation that treats model and framework as joint variables.

AI.tldr: The paper presents CLAWSAFETY, a benchmark to evaluate how vulnerable high-privilege local AI agents are to realistic prompt injection attacks across multiple domains, channels, and agent frameworks.
AI.motivation: Existing safety evaluations focus on chat-style interactions and synthetic settings, ignoring that personal AI agents often run with high system privileges and interact with real-world workspaces. A single prompt injection in this setting can cause severe harms such as credential leaks, financial loss, or data destruction. Moreover, current benchmarks rarely consider how the surrounding agent framework affects safety, leading to an incomplete view of deployment risk.
AI.method: The authors build CLAWSAFETY, a suite of 120 adversarial scenarios structured along three axes: harm domain (e.g., software engineering, finance, healthcare, law, DevOps), attack vector (workspace skill files, trusted emails, web pages), and harmful action type. They integrate these scenarios into realistic, high-privilege professional workspaces that a local agent like OpenClaw would operate in.
