We outline recommendations for building more challenging evaluation suites that better test the reasoning capabilities verification systems need.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes what kinds of reasoning current claim-verification benchmarks actually require, finding they mostly reward simple evidence extraction and lexical matching rather than complex reasoning.
AI.motivation: Although claim verification models have made rapid progress, it is unclear what reasoning skills existing benchmarks really test; the community lacks a systematic breakdown of reasoning types and error patterns across datasets and domains.
AI.method: The authors automatically generate structured reasoning traces for 24,000 claim-verification examples from 9 datasets using GPT-4o-mini, categorize the reasoning required (e.g., direct evidence extraction, multi-sentence synthesis, numerical reasoning), and analyze dataset-level distributions. They then train and use a compact 1B-parameter reasoning verifier to characterize five error types and compare error profiles across domains such as general, scientific, and mathematical verification.
AI.result: They find that most benchmarks are dominated by direct evidence extraction and lexical matching, with multi-sentence synthesis and numerical reasoning being rare. Different datasets exhibit strong biases: some almost exclusively require lexical matching, while others demand information synthesis in about half of cases. Error analysis with the 1B verifier reveals domain-specific error patterns: general-domain verification is mostly affected by lexical overlap bias, scientific verification by overcautiousness, and mathematical verification by arithmetic reasoning failures.
AI.conclusion: Current high performance on claim-verification benchmarks largely reflects retrieval-plus-entailment capability rather than robust reasoning. The authors recommend constructing new, more challenging evaluation suites that better cover multi-sentence, numerical, and domain-specific reasoning skills that real-world verification systems need.

title: NED-Tree: Bridging the Semantic Gap with Nonlinear Element Decomposition Tree for LLM Nonlinear Optimization Modeling
summary: Automating the translation of Operations Research (OR) problems from natural language to executable models is a critical challenge. While Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promise in linear tasks, they suffer from severe performance degradation in real-world nonlinear scenarios due to semantic misalignment between mathematical formulations and solver codes, as well as unstable information extraction. In this study, we introduce NED-Tree, a systematic framework designed to bridge the semantic gap. NED-Tree employs (a) a sentence-by-sentence extraction strategy to ensure robust parameter mapping and traceability; and (b) a recursive tree-based structure that adaptively decomposes complex nonlinear terms into solver-compatible sub-elements. Additionally, we present NEXTOR, a novel benchmark specifically designed for complex nonlinear, extensive-constraint OR problems. Experiments across 10 benchmarks demonstrate that NED-Tree establishes a new state-of-the-art with 72.51% average accuracy, NED-Tree is the first framework that drives LLMs to resolve nonlinear modeling difficulties through element decomposition, achieving alignment between modeling semantics and code semantics. The NED-Tree framework and benchmark are accessible in the anonymous repository https://anonymous.4open.science/r/NORA-NEXTOR.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes NED-Tree, a framework to translate natural-language Operations Research problems into executable nonlinear solver models, achieving new state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Translating real-world OR problems from natural language into executable optimization models is difficult for current LLMs, especially for nonlinear and constraint-rich problems. Existing approaches work relatively well for simpler linear tasks but break down in nonlinear settings due to semantic misalignment between human descriptions, mathematical formulations, and solver code, as well as unstable extraction of parameters and structures from text. The authors aim to create a robust, systematic method that can handle complex nonlinear modeling and close this semantic gap.
AI.method: The authors introduce NED-Tree, which has two key components: (a) a sentence-by-sentence information extraction strategy that incrementally maps natural-language descriptions to model parameters and constraints, ensuring robust mapping and traceability; and (b) a recursive tree-based decomposition mechanism that breaks complex nonlinear terms into simpler, solver-compatible sub-elements, aligning the structure of the model with what solvers expect. They also build NEXTOR, a benchmark focused on complex nonlinear OR problems with extensive constraints, to evaluate the approach against existing methods.
AI.result: On 10 different benchmarks, NED-Tree achieves an average accuracy of 72.51%, outperforming prior methods and establishing a new state-of-the-art for natural-language-to-OR-model translation in nonlinear, constraint-rich scenarios. The experiments show that the tree-based element decomposition strategy effectively helps LLMs handle nonlinear modeling tasks that they previously struggled with.
AI.conclusion: NED-Tree demonstrates that guiding LLMs with a structured, recursive decomposition framework and fine-grained, sentence-level extraction can substantially improve the translation from natural language OR descriptions to executable nonlinear optimization models. It is the first framework to systematically decompose nonlinear model elements for LLMs, aligning modeling and code semantics and setting a new performance baseline on the proposed NEXTOR benchmark, which together offer a foundation for more reliable automation of OR modeling from text.

title: PRCCF: A Persona-guided Retrieval and Causal-aware Cognitive Filtering Framework for Emotional Support Conversation
comment: 14 pages, 6 figures, 5 tables. Submitted to Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics (TACL)
summary: Emotional Support Conversation (ESC) aims to alleviate individual emotional distress by generating empathetic responses. However, existing methods face challenges in effectively supporting deep contextual understanding. To address this issue, we propose PRCCF, a Persona-guided Retrieval and Causality-aware Cognitive Filtering framework. Specifically, the framework incorporates a persona-guided retrieval mechanism that jointly models semantic compatibility and persona alignment to enhance response generation. Furthermore, it employs a causality-aware cognitive filtering module to prioritize causally relevant external knowledge, thereby improving contextual cognitive understanding for emotional reasoning. Extensive experiments on the ESConv dataset demonstrate that PRCCF outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on both automatic metrics and human evaluations. Our code is publicly available at: https://github.com/YancyLyx/PRCCF.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes PRCCF, a persona-guided retrieval and causality-aware cognitive filtering framework that enhances emotional support conversations by better leveraging persona information and causally relevant external knowledge, achieving state-of-the-art results on ESConv.
AI.motivation: Existing Emotional Support Conversation systems struggle with deep contextual understanding and emotional reasoning, particularly in effectively using external knowledge and aligning responses with a helper’s persona. There is a need for a framework that can both respect persona constraints and selectively focus on the most causally relevant knowledge to improve empathetic, supportive responses.
AI.method: The authors design PRCCF, which has two key components: (1) a persona-guided retrieval mechanism that jointly models semantic compatibility between conversation context and candidate knowledge as well as alignment with the supporter’s persona, to retrieve better-suited information; and (2) a causality-aware cognitive filtering module that ranks and filters external knowledge by its causal relevance to the emotional situation, so the model can focus on knowledge that truly explains, influences, or addresses the user’s emotional state, and then use this refined information to guide response generation.
AI.result: On the ESConv dataset, PRCCF surpasses existing state-of-the-art methods on standard automatic evaluation metrics and is also judged better in human evaluations, indicating stronger contextual understanding, emotional reasoning, and quality of supportive responses.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating persona-guided retrieval with causality-aware cognitive filtering significantly improves emotional support dialogue systems, enabling them to better understand context, reason about emotions, and generate more appropriate, empathetic responses. PRCCF represents a promising direction for combining persona alignment and causal knowledge selection in ESC and sets a new performance benchmark on ESConv.

title: ThinkTwice: Jointly Optimizing Large Language Models for Reasoning and Self-Refinement
summary: We introduce ThinkTwice, a simple two-phase framework that jointly optimizes LLMs to solve reasoning problems and refine the answers, based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). In each pair of training steps, ThinkTwice first optimizes the model on solving reasoning problems, then optimizes it on refining its own solutions to the same problems, using the same binary correctness reward in both phases without correctness signals or critique annotations. Across five mathematical reasoning benchmarks and two model families including Qwen3-4B and Olmo3-7B, ThinkTwice substantially improves both reasoning and refinement performance over competitive online policy optimization baselines. Specifically, on Qwen3-4B, ThinkTwice outperforms GRPO on AIME by 5 percentage points before refinement and by 11.5 points after one self-refinement step, measured by pass@4. Analysis of the training dynamics of ThinkTwice reveals an implicit rectify-then-fortify curriculum: refinement predominantly corrects errors early in training and naturally shifts toward preserving already-correct solutions as the model improves, yielding a more rectified reward signal. Our work establishes joint training of reasoning and self-refinement as a principled and effective methodology for RLVR.

AI.tldr: ThinkTwice is a two-phase RL framework that jointly trains LLMs to both solve reasoning tasks and refine their own answers, significantly boosting math reasoning performance over GRPO baselines without extra supervision.
AI.motivation: Standard RL-based reasoning optimization for LLMs focuses mainly on generating initial solutions, often neglecting systematic self-refinement and typically requiring richer supervision such as critiques or correctness signals per step. There is a need for a simple, supervision-efficient method that can improve both initial reasoning and self-refinement capabilities, and better exploit the feedback signal during policy optimization.
AI.method: They propose ThinkTwice, a two-phase training framework built on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). In each pair of training steps, the first phase optimizes the model to solve reasoning problems from scratch, and the second phase optimizes the model to refine its own previously generated solutions to the same problems. Both phases share the same binary correctness reward, with no additional critique labels or stepwise correctness signals. Training dynamics are analyzed to understand how refinement behavior evolves over time.
AI.result: Across five mathematical reasoning benchmarks and on two model families (Qwen3-4B and Olmo3-7B), ThinkTwice yields substantial gains in both initial reasoning and refinement performance relative to strong online policy optimization baselines. On AIME with Qwen3-4B, ThinkTwice improves over vanilla GRPO by 5 percentage points in pass@4 before refinement, and by 11.5 percentage points after one self-refinement step. The training log analysis shows that the refinement phase becomes increasingly focused on preserving and slightly improving already-correct solutions as the base reasoning ability improves.
AI.conclusion: Jointly training reasoning and self-refinement within a single RL framework, using only a simple binary correctness reward, is an effective and principled approach for reinforcement learning from verification (RLVR). ThinkTwice not only improves final performance but also induces an implicit rectify-then-fortify curriculum, where the model first learns to correct errors and later learns to stabilize correct reasoning, leading to a more informative and robust reward signal.

title: Do Large Language Models Mentalize When They Teach?
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures. Workshop paper at ICML 2026
summary: How do LLMs decide what to teach next: by reasoning about a learner's knowledge, or by using simpler rules of thumb? We test this in a controlled task previously used to study human teaching strategies. On each trial, a teacher LLM sees a hypothetical learner's trajectory through a reward-annotated directed graph and must reveal a single edge so the learner would choose a better path if they replanned. We run a range of LLMs as simulated teachers and fit their trial-by-trial choices with the same cognitive models used for humans: a Bayes-Optimal teacher that infers which transitions the learner is missing (inverse planning), weaker Bayesian variants, heuristic baselines (e.g., reward based), and non-mentalizing utility models. In a baseline experiment matched to the stimuli presented to human subjects, most LLMs perform well, show little change in strategy over trials, and their graph-by-graph performance is similar to that of humans. Model comparison (BIC) shows that Bayes-Optimal teaching best explains most models' choices. When given a scaffolding intervention, models follow auxiliary inference- or reward-focused prompts, but these scaffolds do not reliably improve later teaching on heuristic-incongruent test graphs and can sometimes reduce performance. Overall, cognitive model fits provide insight into LLM tutoring policies and show that prompt compliance does not guarantee better teaching decisions.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how LLMs choose what to teach next by comparing their behavior to cognitive models of human teaching in a controlled graph-navigation task.
AI.motivation: To understand whether LLM-based teachers make decisions by reasoning about a learner’s knowledge state (like Bayesian inverse planning) or by using simpler heuristics, and how this compares to human teaching strategies.
AI.method: Use a reward-annotated directed-graph teaching task where an LLM, acting as a teacher, sees a learner’s past trajectory and must reveal one edge to improve the learner’s future path. Run various LLMs on many such trials, then fit their trial-by-trial choices with cognitive models used in human studies: Bayes-Optimal inverse-planning teachers, weaker Bayesian variants, heuristic baselines, and non-mentalizing utility models. Also test scaffolding prompts that encourage inference- or reward-focused teaching and examine their impact on later performance.
AI.result: Most LLMs perform well on the baseline task, with stable strategies across trials and human-comparable performance per graph. Model comparison using BIC indicates that a Bayes-Optimal teaching model best explains the choices of most LLMs. Scaffolding prompts shift models’ immediate behavior toward inference- or reward-focused strategies but do not consistently improve performance on challenging, heuristic-incongruent test graphs and can even degrade it.
AI.conclusion: LLMs’ tutoring decisions in this task are best captured by Bayes-Optimal teaching models, suggesting they often behave as if they infer learners’ missing knowledge rather than relying solely on simple heuristics. However, externally imposed scaffolding prompts do not reliably enhance their underlying teaching quality, highlighting that prompt compliance is not the same as genuinely better pedagogical decisions.

title: On the Role of Reasoning Patterns in the Generalization Discrepancy of Long Chain-of-Thought Supervised Fine-Tuning
summary: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) trajectories has become a pivotal phase in building large reasoning models. However, how CoT trajectories from different sources influence the generalization performance of models remains an open question. In this paper, we conduct a comparative study using two sources of verified CoT trajectories generated by two competing models, \texttt{DeepSeek-R1-0528} and \texttt{gpt-oss-120b}, with their problem sets controlled to be identical. Despite their comparable performance, we uncover a striking paradox: lower training loss does not translate to better generalization. SFT on \texttt{DeepSeek-R1-0528} data achieves remarkably lower training loss, yet exhibits significantly worse generalization performance on reasoning benchmarks compared to those trained on \texttt{gpt-oss-120b}. To understand this paradox, we perform a multi-faceted analysis probing token-level SFT loss and step-level reasoning behaviors. Our analysis reveals a difference in reasoning patterns. \texttt{gpt-oss-120b} exhibits highly convergent and deductive trajectories, whereas \texttt{DeepSeek-R1-0528} favors a divergent and branch-heavy exploration pattern. Consequently, models trained with \texttt{DeepSeek-R1} data inherit inefficient exploration behaviors, often getting trapped in redundant exploratory branches that hinder them from reaching correct solutions. Building upon this insight, we propose a simple yet effective remedy of filtering out frequently branching trajectories to improve the generalization of SFT. Experiments show that training on selected \texttt{DeepSeek-R1-0528} subsets surprisingly improves reasoning performance by up to 5.1% on AIME25, 5.5% on BeyondAIME, and on average 3.6% on five benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how different sources of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) data used for supervised fine-tuning affect reasoning generalization, finding that lower training loss can hurt generalization when the CoT trajectories are overly exploratory and branchy, and proposes filtering such trajectories to improve performance.
AI.motivation: While long CoT supervised fine-tuning is standard for building strong reasoning LLMs, it is unclear how the characteristics of CoT trajectories from different teacher models impact downstream generalization. Notably, different models can achieve similar benchmark performance but generate qualitatively different reasoning paths. The authors are motivated to understand why training loss does not reliably predict generalization when using CoT data, and to diagnose which properties of CoT trajectories make them better or worse supervision signals.
AI.method: The authors collect two sets of verified CoT trajectories for the same problem sets, generated by two competitive models: DeepSeek-R1-0528 and gpt-oss-120b. They fine-tune student models with SFT on each dataset and compare training loss and generalization on multiple reasoning benchmarks. They then perform detailed analyses at the token level (SFT loss) and step level (reasoning behavior) to characterize differences in reasoning patterns, focusing on convergence, deduction, and branching structure. Based on these insights, they design a filtering strategy that removes trajectories with frequent branching to see if this improves generalization when training on DeepSeek-R1-0528 data subsets.
AI.result: Models trained on DeepSeek-R1-0528 CoT trajectories attain much lower training loss than those trained on gpt-oss-120b data but generalize significantly worse on reasoning benchmarks. Analysis shows that gpt-oss-120b trajectories are more convergent and deductive, whereas DeepSeek-R1-0528 trajectories are more divergent and branch-heavy, leading student models to inherit inefficient exploratory behaviors and get stuck in redundant branches. Applying a simple filter that removes frequently branching trajectories from the DeepSeek-R1-0528 dataset reverses this issue: the resulting models achieve improved reasoning performance, with gains up to 5.1% on AIME25, 5.5% on BeyondAIME, and an average of 3.6% across five benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Training loss on CoT SFT is not a reliable indicator of reasoning generalization; the structural properties of reasoning trajectories matter greatly. Divergent, branch-heavy CoT supervision can hurt downstream performance by teaching inefficient exploration patterns, even if it yields lower loss. More convergent and deductive trajectories, or filtered subsets that avoid excessive branching, lead to better generalization. Simple trajectory filtering based on branching behavior is an effective way to improve reasoning models without changing architectures or training pipelines.

title: ByteRover: Agent-Native Memory Through LLM-Curated Hierarchical Context
summary: Memory-Augmented Generation (MAG) extends large language models with external memory to support long-context reasoning, but existing approaches universally treat memory as an external service that agents call into, delegating storage to separate pipelines of chunking, embedding, and graph extraction. This architectural separation means the system that stores knowledge does not understand it, leading to semantic drift between what the agent intended to remember and what the pipeline actually captured, loss of coordination context across agents, and fragile recovery after failures. In this paper, we propose ByteRover, an agent-native memory architecture that inverts the memory pipeline: the same LLM that reasons about a task also curates, structures, and retrieves knowledge. ByteRover represents knowledge in a hierarchical Context Tree, a file-based knowledge graph organized as Domain, Topic, Subtopic, and Entry, where each entry carries explicit relations, provenance, and an Adaptive Knowledge Lifecycle (AKL) with importance scoring, maturity tiers, and recency decay. Retrieval uses a 5-tier progressive strategy that resolves most queries at sub-100 ms latency without LLM calls, escalating to agentic reasoning only for novel questions. Experiments on LoCoMo and LongMemEval demonstrate that ByteRover achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on LoCoMo and competitive results on LongMemEval while requiring zero external infrastructure, no vector database, no graph database, no embedding service, with all knowledge stored as human-readable markdown files on the local filesystem.

AI.tldr: ByteRover is an agent-native memory system where the same LLM both reasons and manages memory using a hierarchical, file-based context tree, achieving strong long-context performance without external infrastructure.
AI.motivation: Existing memory-augmented generation systems rely on external pipelines (chunking, embeddings, graph extraction) that are architecturally separated from the reasoning LLM. This causes semantic drift between what the agent wants to store and what is actually stored, breaks shared context across agents, and makes recovery from failures brittle. The authors want a memory system that the agent itself fully understands and controls, to improve coherence, robustness, and simplicity of deployment.
AI.method: The paper introduces ByteRover, an agent-native memory architecture in which the same LLM is responsible for curating, structuring, and retrieving knowledge. Knowledge is stored as a hierarchical Context Tree—a file-based knowledge graph with four levels: Domain, Topic, Subtopic, and Entry. Each entry includes explicit relations, provenance information, and an Adaptive Knowledge Lifecycle (AKL) that tracks importance, maturity tiers, and recency decay. Retrieval follows a 5-tier progressive strategy that starts from lightweight, non-LLM mechanisms and only escalates to LLM-based agentic reasoning for hard or novel queries, aiming for sub-100 ms latency for most lookups. All data is stored as human-readable markdown files on the local filesystem, eliminating the need for vector or graph databases.
AI.result: On the LoCoMo and LongMemEval benchmarks for long-context reasoning and memory, ByteRover attains state-of-the-art accuracy on LoCoMo and competitive performance on LongMemEval. It accomplishes this without any external infrastructure such as vector databases, graph databases, or embedding services, relying solely on local markdown files and the LLM itself for memory management. Latency is improved for common retrieval cases, with most queries resolved under 100 ms without needing an LLM call.
AI.conclusion: An LLM-centered, agent-native memory system can effectively replace traditional external memory pipelines while simplifying infrastructure and improving semantic alignment between the agent’s reasoning and its stored knowledge. By using a structured, file-based Context Tree with an adaptive lifecycle and progressive retrieval, ByteRover delivers strong long-context performance with low latency and no specialized external services, suggesting a practical and robust design pattern for memory-augmented generation systems.

title: Development and multi-center evaluation of domain-adapted speech recognition for human-AI teaming in real-world gastrointestinal endoscopy
comment: Under review at npj Digital Medicine
summary: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) is a critical interface for human-AI interaction in gastrointestinal endoscopy, yet its reliability in real-world clinical settings is limited by domain-specific terminology and complex acoustic conditions. Here, we present EndoASR, a domain-adapted ASR system designed for real-time deployment in endoscopic workflows. We develop a two-stage adaptation strategy based on synthetic endoscopy reports, targeting domain-specific language modeling and noise robustness. In retrospective evaluation across six endoscopists, EndoASR substantially improves both transcription accuracy and clinical usability, reducing character error rate (CER) from 20.52% to 14.14% and increasing medical term accuracy (Med ACC) from 54.30% to 87.59%. In a prospective multi-center study spanning five independent endoscopy centers, EndoASR demonstrates consistent generalization under heterogeneous real-world conditions. Compared with the baseline Paraformer model, CER is reduced from 16.20% to 14.97%, while Med ACC is improved from 61.63% to 84.16%, confirming its robustness in practical deployment scenarios. Notably, EndoASR achieves a real-time factor (RTF) of 0.005, significantly faster than Whisper-large-v3 (RTF 0.055), while maintaining a compact model size of 220M parameters, enabling efficient edge deployment. Furthermore, integration with large language models demonstrates that improved ASR quality directly enhances downstream structured information extraction and clinician-AI interaction. These results demonstrate that domain-adapted ASR can serve as a reliable interface for human-AI teaming in gastrointestinal endoscopy, with consistent performance validated across multi-center real-world clinical settings.

AI.tldr: EndoASR is a domain-adapted, real-time speech recognition system for gastrointestinal endoscopy that significantly improves transcription accuracy, especially for medical terms, while being fast and compact enough for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: Conventional ASR models perform poorly in gastrointestinal endoscopy because of domain-specific terminology, noisy clinical environments, and complex acoustic conditions. This limits reliable human-AI interaction and downstream automation (e.g., report generation, information extraction) during endoscopic procedures. There is a need for an ASR system tailored to this domain that works robustly in real-world, multi-center clinical settings and can run in real time on edge hardware.
AI.method: The authors build EndoASR, a domain-adapted ASR system using a two-stage adaptation strategy. First, they use synthetic endoscopy reports to adapt the language model toward domain-specific vocabulary and expressions. Second, they focus on acoustic adaptation and noise robustness to cope with the complex soundscapes in endoscopy rooms. They evaluate EndoASR retrospectively on data from six endoscopists and prospectively in a multi-center study involving five independent endoscopy centers, comparing against a strong baseline model (Paraformer) and Whisper-large-v3 in terms of accuracy, speed, and model size. They also integrate EndoASR outputs with large language models to test impacts on downstream tasks like structured information extraction and clinician-AI interaction.
AI.result: In retrospective tests, EndoASR reduces character error rate from 20.52% to 14.14% and boosts medical term accuracy from 54.30% to 87.59%. In a prospective multi-center study, it generalizes well: compared with the Paraformer baseline, CER drops from 16.20% to 14.97%, and Med ACC improves from 61.63% to 84.16%. EndoASR achieves a real-time factor of 0.005—about 11x faster than Whisper-large-v3 (0.055)—with a compact 220M-parameter model suited for edge deployment. Improved ASR outputs lead directly to better performance of large language models on structured information extraction and improved clinician-AI interaction quality.
AI.conclusion: EndoASR shows that domain-adapted ASR tailored to gastrointestinal endoscopy can substantially improve transcription accuracy, especially for specialized medical terminology, while remaining fast and lightweight enough for practical real-time use on edge devices. Its consistent performance across multiple clinical centers and its positive effect on downstream LLM-based tasks indicate that such domain-specific ASR can be a dependable interface for human-AI teaming in real-world endoscopic workflows.

title: MM-ReCoder: Advancing Chart-to-Code Generation with Reinforcement Learning and Self-Correction
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have recently demonstrated promising capabilities in multimodal coding tasks such as chart-to-code generation. However, existing methods primarily rely on supervised fine-tuning (SFT), which requires the model to learn code patterns through chart-code pairs but does not expose the model to a code execution environment. Moreover, while self-correction through execution feedback offers a potential route to improve coding quality, even state-of-the-art MLLMs have been shown to struggle with effective self-correction. In this work, we introduce MM-ReCoder, a chart-to-code generation model trained with reinforcement learning (RL) and equipped with self-correction ability. We propose a two-stage multi-turn self-correction RL strategy based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). The first stage enhances the model's self-correction ability via rolling out a shared first turn, while the second stage improves the coding capability with full-trajectory optimization. MM-ReCoder learns to produce more accurate and executable code through the interaction with the environment and by iteratively correcting its own outputs. Our results on three chart-to-code benchmarks demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance of MM-ReCoder.

AI.tldr: The paper presents MM-ReCoder, an RL-trained multimodal large language model for chart-to-code generation that uses a two-stage self-correction strategy to achieve state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLM-based chart-to-code systems rely mostly on supervised fine-tuning with static chart–code pairs and have no access to a code execution environment during training. This limits their ability to learn from execution outcomes and hinders robust self-correction, as current MLLMs struggle to effectively refine their own code based on feedback. The authors aim to overcome these limitations by integrating reinforcement learning with explicit execution feedback and structured self-correction to improve coding quality and reliability.
AI.method: The authors design MM-ReCoder, a chart-to-code generation model trained via reinforcement learning using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). They introduce a two-stage, multi-turn self-correction RL framework: (1) a first stage that focuses on cultivating self-correction ability by rolling out multiple correction attempts from a shared initial turn, leveraging execution feedback; and (2) a second stage that performs full-trajectory optimization, refining the entire multi-turn interaction from initial code generation through subsequent corrections. The model interacts with an execution environment, receives rewards based on code correctness and executability, and iteratively updates its policy to generate better code and self-corrections.
AI.result: On three chart-to-code benchmarks, MM-ReCoder achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming prior MLLM-based and SFT-based approaches. The model generates more accurate and executable code and shows improved ability to refine its own outputs across multiple turns when guided by execution feedback.
AI.conclusion: Integrating reinforcement learning with a structured, two-stage self-correction process significantly enhances chart-to-code generation in multimodal LLMs. By training MM-ReCoder to interact with an execution environment and iteratively update its own code, the model attains superior accuracy and executability compared with purely supervised methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of GRPO-based multi-turn RL for multimodal coding tasks.

title: Memory in the LLM Era: Modular Architectures and Strategies in a Unified Framework
summary: Memory emerges as the core module in the large language model (LLM)-based agents for long-horizon complex tasks (e.g., multi-turn dialogue, game playing, scientific discovery), where memory can enable knowledge accumulation, iterative reasoning and self-evolution. A number of memory methods have been proposed in the literature. However, these methods have not been systematically and comprehensively compared under the same experimental settings. In this paper, we first summarize a unified framework that incorporates all the existing agent memory methods from a high-level perspective. We then extensively compare representative agent memory methods on two well-known benchmarks and examine the effectiveness of all methods, providing a thorough analysis of those methods. As a byproduct of our experimental analysis, we also design a new memory method by exploiting modules in the existing methods, which outperforms the state-of-the-art methods. Finally, based on these findings, we offer promising future research opportunities. We believe that a deeper understanding of the behavior of existing methods can provide valuable new insights for future research.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically studies memory modules in LLM-based agents, proposing a unified framework, running extensive comparisons of existing methods, and introducing a new, better-performing memory method plus future research directions.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents tackling long-horizon tasks need effective memory to support knowledge accumulation, iterative reasoning, and self-improvement. Many memory methods exist, but they have not been evaluated or compared under a unified, controlled setting, leaving practitioners unsure which methods work best and why.
AI.method: The authors (1) formalize a unified high-level framework that encompasses existing memory mechanisms in LLM agents; (2) select representative memory methods from the literature and evaluate them under consistent experimental settings on two widely used benchmarks; (3) conduct thorough empirical analysis across methods; and (4) compose a new hybrid memory method by recombining modules from the evaluated approaches.
AI.result: Under the unified framework and common benchmarks, the authors obtain a systematic performance comparison of representative memory methods and detailed insights into their behavior. Their newly designed hybrid memory method achieves performance superior to the state-of-the-art baselines on these benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A unified understanding and rigorous comparison of memory mechanisms in LLM-based agents reveals strengths and weaknesses of existing approaches and points to promising directions for improving agent memory. The proposed hybrid method demonstrates that combining effective modules from prior work can advance the state of the art, and the analysis can guide future research on memory design for long-horizon LLM agents.

title: CRaFT: Circuit-Guided Refusal Feature Selection via Cross-Layer Transcoders
summary: As safety concerns around large language models (LLMs) grow, understanding the internal mechanisms underlying refusal behavior has become increasingly important. Recent work has studied this behavior by identifying internal features associated with refusal and manipulating them to induce compliance with harmful requests. However, existing refusal feature selection methods rely on how strongly features activate on harmful prompts, which tends to capture superficial signals rather than the causal factors underlying the refusal decision. We propose CRaFT, a circuit-guided refusal feature selection framework that ranks features by their influence on the model's refusal-compliance decision using prompts near the refusal boundary. On Gemma-3-1B-it, CRaFT improves attack success rate (ASR) from 6.7% to 48.2% and outperforms baseline methods across multiple jailbreak benchmarks. These results suggest that circuit influence is a more reliable criterion than activation magnitude for identifying features that causally mediate refusal behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CRaFT, a circuit-guided method to identify and rank internal LLM features that causally drive refusal vs. compliance decisions, enabling much stronger jailbreaks than prior activation-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing work on understanding and attacking LLM refusal behavior focuses on features that strongly activate on harmful prompts, but such activation-based selection often captures superficial correlations rather than the true causal mechanisms determining whether the model refuses or complies. This limits both mechanistic understanding and the effectiveness of jailbreak attacks. There is a need for a more causally grounded way to identify refusal-related features inside the model.
AI.method: The authors propose CRaFT, a circuit-guided refusal feature selection framework. Instead of ranking features by how strongly they activate on clearly harmful prompts, CRaFT focuses on prompts that lie near the refusal/compliance decision boundary and evaluates each feature by its influence on the final refusal outcome within a refusal-related circuit. Features are ranked by this causal influence measure, and these identified features are then manipulated to push the model toward compliance, enabling stronger jailbreak attacks. Experiments are run on the Gemma-3-1B-it model and evaluated on several jailbreak benchmarks.
AI.result: On Gemma-3-1B-it, features selected by CRaFT, when manipulated, increase the attack success rate from 6.7% to 48.2%, a large improvement over previous methods that rely on activation magnitude. Across multiple jailbreak benchmarks, CRaFT consistently outperforms baseline feature selection approaches, demonstrating that its circuit-based influence criterion better identifies features that truly control refusal behavior.
AI.conclusion: Circuit influence within refusal-related circuits is a more reliable signal than raw activation magnitude for finding internal features that causally mediate LLM refusal decisions. By using prompts near the refusal boundary and ranking features by their impact on the refusal/compliance outcome, CRaFT yields a more faithful mechanistic characterization of refusal behavior and enables far more effective jailbreak attacks than prior activation-based techniques.

title: Human-Guided Reasoning with Large Language Models for Vietnamese Speech Emotion Recognition
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures. Dataset of 2,764 Vietnamese speech samples across three emotion classes
summary: Vietnamese Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) remains challenging due to ambiguous acoustic patterns and the lack of reliable annotated data, especially in real-world conditions where emotional boundaries are not clearly separable. To address this problem, this paper proposes a human-machine collaborative framework that integrates human knowledge into the learning process rather than relying solely on data-driven models. The proposed framework is centered around LLM-based reasoning, where acoustic feature-based models are used to provide auxiliary signals such as confidence and feature-level evidence. A confidence-based routing mechanism is introduced to distinguish between easy and ambiguous samples, allowing uncertain cases to be delegated to LLMs for deeper reasoning guided by structured rules derived from human annotation behavior. In addition, an iterative refinement strategy is employed to continuously improve system performance through error analysis and rule updates. Experiments are conducted on a Vietnamese speech dataset of 2,764 samples across three emotion classes (calm, angry, panic), with high inter-annotator agreement (Fleiss Kappa = 0.8574), ensuring reliable ground truth. The proposed method achieves strong performance, reaching up to 86.59% accuracy and Macro F1 around 0.85-0.86, demonstrating its effectiveness in handling ambiguous and hard-to-classify cases. Overall, this work highlights the importance of combining data-driven models with human reasoning, providing a robust and model-agnostic approach for speech emotion recognition in low-resource settings.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a human-machine collaborative framework for Vietnamese speech emotion recognition that routes easy samples to acoustic models and ambiguous ones to an LLM-based reasoning module using human-inspired rules, achieving about 86% accuracy and strong macro F1 on a 3-class dataset.
AI.motivation: Vietnamese SER is difficult due to ambiguous acoustic patterns, unclear emotional boundaries in real-world speech, and a lack of high-quality labeled data, especially in low-resource settings. Purely data-driven models struggle with these ambiguous and hard-to-classify cases, motivating a framework that explicitly incorporates human knowledge and reasoning into the learning and inference process.
AI.method: The authors design a human-machine collaborative framework centered on LLM-based reasoning. First, acoustic feature-based SER models generate predictions plus auxiliary signals such as confidence scores and feature-level evidence. A confidence-based routing mechanism then separates easy from ambiguous samples. High-confidence samples are handled directly by the acoustic model, while low-confidence ones are routed to an LLM that performs deeper reasoning based on structured rules reflecting human annotation behavior. An iterative refinement strategy uses error analysis to update and expand these rules, improving the system over multiple iterations. The approach is model-agnostic with respect to the underlying acoustic classifier.
AI.result: On a Vietnamese speech dataset with 2,764 samples and three emotion classes (calm, angry, panic), and with high inter-annotator agreement (Fleiss’ Kappa = 0.8574), the proposed framework attains up to 86.59% accuracy and Macro F1 around 0.85–0.86. It is particularly effective on ambiguous and hard-to-classify samples, showing that routing such cases to LLM-based reasoning improves overall SER performance.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating human knowledge through LLM-based reasoning and confidence-based routing significantly improves Vietnamese SER, especially for ambiguous emotional expressions, and offers a robust, model-agnostic solution suitable for low-resource settings. The work underscores the value of combining data-driven acoustic models with structured human-inspired rules and iterative refinement for more reliable emotion recognition in real-world scenarios.

title: From Multi-Agent to Single-Agent: When Is Skill Distillation Beneficial?
summary: Multi-agent systems (MAS) tackle complex tasks by distributing expertise, though this often comes at the cost of heavy coordination overhead, context fragmentation, and brittle phase ordering. Distilling a MAS into a single-agent skill can bypass these costs, but this conversion lacks a principled answer for when and what to distill. Instead, the empirical outcome is surprisingly inconsistent: skill lift ranges from a 28% improvement to a 2% degradation across metrics of the exact same task. In this work, we reveal that skill utility is governed not by the task, but by the evaluation metric. We introduce Metric Freedom ($F$), the first a priori predictor of skill utility. $F$ measures the topological rigidity of a metric's scoring landscape by quantifying how output diversity couples with score variance via a Mantel test. Guided by $F$, we propose a two-stage adaptive distillation framework. Stage 1 acts as a selective extraction mechanism, extracting tools and knowledge while discarding restrictive structures on "free" metrics to preserve exploration. Stage 2 targets computationally intensive iterative refinement exclusively toward "rigid" metrics ($F \lesssim 0.6$) to eliminate trajectory-local overfitting. Evaluating across 4 tasks, 11 datasets, and 6 metrics, $F$ strongly predicts skill utility ($ρ= -0.62$, $p < 0.05$). Strikingly, identical agent trajectories yield diametrically opposite skill lifts under rigid versus free metrics, demonstrating that skill utility is fundamentally a metric-level property. Driven by this signal, our adaptive agent matches or exceeds the original MAS while reducing cost up to 8$\times$ and latency by up to 15$\times$.

AI.tldr: They study when distilling a multi-agent system into a single-agent “skill” actually helps, and show it depends on the evaluation metric, not the task. They introduce Metric Freedom (F), a statistic that predicts whether distillation will improve performance, and use it to build an adaptive distillation framework that cuts cost and latency while matching or beating the original system.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent systems are powerful for complex problems but incur heavy coordination cost, fragmented context, and rigid phase ordering. Distilling them into a single agent could reduce cost and complexity, but currently there is no principled way to know when this is beneficial. Empirical results are inconsistent: the same distillation pipeline can both help and hurt depending on how performance is measured. The authors want a theory-driven criterion that predicts when distillation will yield a useful skill.
AI.method: 1) Analyze the relationship between evaluation metrics and the utility of a distilled skill, discovering that metrics, not tasks, govern whether skill performance improves. 2) Define Metric Freedom (F) as a topological property of a metric’s scoring landscape: using a Mantel test, they quantify how diversity among outputs correlates with variance in scores. Low F (rigid metrics) means small changes in outputs cause large score changes; high F (free metrics) means the opposite. 3) Propose a two-stage adaptive distillation framework: Stage 1 selectively extracts tools/knowledge from the MAS while avoiding encoding rigid structures for metrics with high F to maintain exploration; Stage 2 focuses computationally intensive, iterative refinement only on rigid metrics (F≲0.6) to prevent local overfitting of trajectories. 4) Evaluate across multiple tasks, datasets, and metrics, correlating F with observed skill lift.
AI.result: Metric Freedom correlates strongly and negatively with skill utility (ρ = -0.62, p < 0.05), meaning metrics with lower F (more rigid) tend to benefit more from skill distillation. The same underlying agent trajectories can show opposite skill lifts depending on whether the evaluation metric is rigid or free, empirically confirming that skill utility is metric-dependent. The adaptive agent guided by F achieves performance that matches or surpasses the original multi-agent system while reducing computational cost by up to 8× and latency by up to 15× across 4 tasks, 11 datasets, and 6 metrics.
AI.conclusion: Skill distillation from multi-agent to single-agent systems cannot be judged at the task level; it is fundamentally a property of the evaluation metric. Metric Freedom provides the first a priori, quantitative predictor of whether distillation will help. By using F to tailor which parts of a MAS to distill and where to apply intensive refinement, one can design adaptive agents that retain or exceed MAS performance while substantially reducing resource usage. This reframes skill design as a metric-aware process and offers a practical recipe for cost-effective, high-performing distilled agents.

title: Detecting Toxic Language: Ontology and BERT-based Approaches for Bulgarian Text
summary: Toxic content detection in online communication remains a significant challenge, with current solutions often inadvertently blocking valuable information, including medical terms and text related to minority groups. This paper presents a more nu-anced approach to identifying toxicity in Bulgarian text while preserving access to essential information. The research explores two distinct methodologies for detecting toxic content. The developed methodologies have po-tential applications across diverse online platforms and content moderation systems. First, we propose an ontology that models the potentially toxic words in Bulgarian language. Then, we compose a dataset that comprises 4,384 manually anno-tated sentences from Bulgarian online forums across four categories: toxic language, medical terminology, non-toxic lan-guage, and terms related to minority communities. We then train a BERT-based model for toxic language classification, which reaches a 0.89 F1 macro score. The trained model is directly applicable in a real environment and can be integrated as a com-ponent of toxic content detection systems.

AI.tldr: The paper develops an ontology and a BERT-based classifier to detect toxic Bulgarian text while avoiding mislabeling medical and minority-related terms as toxic.
AI.motivation: Existing toxic content detection systems in online communication often over-block useful or sensitive but legitimate information, such as medical terminology or references to minority groups. There is a need for more nuanced approaches in Bulgarian that can separate genuinely toxic language from non-toxic but potentially sensitive terms.
AI.method: The authors first construct an ontology that models potentially toxic words in Bulgarian. They then build a dataset of 4,384 manually annotated sentences from Bulgarian online forums, labeled into four classes: toxic language, medical terminology, non-toxic language, and minority-related terms. Using this dataset, they train a BERT-based model for toxic language classification.
AI.result: The BERT-based classifier trained on the constructed dataset achieves a macro F1 score of 0.89 on the toxic language classification task, indicating strong performance across the four categories.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ontology and trained BERT-based model provide an effective and more nuanced method for toxic content detection in Bulgarian. The model is ready for deployment and can be integrated as a component in online content moderation systems to better distinguish between harmful and legitimate content.

title: GraphWalk: Enabling Reasoning in Large Language Models through Tool-Based Graph Navigation
summary: The use of knowledge graphs for grounding agents in real-world Q&A applications has become increasingly common. Answering complex queries often requires multi-hop reasoning and the ability to navigate vast relational structures. Standard approaches rely on prompting techniques that steer large language models to reason over raw graph context, or retrieval-augmented generation pipelines where relevant subgraphs are injected into the context. These, however, face severe limitations with enterprise-scale KGs that cannot fit in even the largest context windows available today. We present GraphWalk, a problem-agnostic, training-free, tool-based framework that allows off-the-shelf LLMs to reason through sequential graph navigation, dramatically increasing performance across different tasks. Unlike task-specific agent frameworks that encode domain knowledge into specialized tools, GraphWalk equips the LLM with a minimal set of orthogonal graph operations sufficient to traverse any graph structure. We evaluate whether models equipped with GraphWalk can compose these operations into correct multi-step reasoning chains, where each tool call represents a verifiable step creating a transparent execution trace. We first demonstrate our approach on maze traversal, a problem non-reasoning models are completely unable to solve, then present results on graphs resembling real-world enterprise knowledge graphs. To isolate structural reasoning from world knowledge, we evaluate on entirely synthetic graphs with random, non-semantic labels. Our benchmark spans 12 query templates from basic retrieval to compound first-order logic queries. Results show that tool-based traversal yields substantial and consistent gains over in-context baselines across all model families tested, with gains becoming more pronounced as scale increases, precisely where in-context approaches fail catastrophically.

AI.tldr: GraphWalk is a training-free tool framework that lets LLMs navigate knowledge graphs step-by-step with simple graph operations, enabling transparent multi-hop reasoning and outperforming in-context graph reasoning baselines, especially at scale.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for using LLMs over large knowledge graphs rely on stuffing subgraphs into the context or prompt-based reasoning over raw graph text, which breaks down on enterprise-scale graphs that cannot fit in context windows and on tasks needing explicit multi-hop structural reasoning. A general, problem-agnostic way to let LLMs interact with graphs as tools, rather than context, is needed to improve scalability, reasoning accuracy, and transparency of the reasoning steps.
AI.method: They propose GraphWalk, a tool-based interface where an off-the-shelf LLM can call a small set of generic graph operations (e.g., get neighbors, follow edges, retrieve node info) to sequentially traverse and query any graph. The framework is training-free and problem-agnostic: the LLM composes these operations into multi-step reasoning chains, with each tool call being a concrete, verifiable action. They test this on maze traversal and on synthetic knowledge-graph-like structures using 12 query templates from simple retrieval to compound first-order logic queries, comparing tool-based traversal against in-context prompting baselines across multiple LLM families and scales.
AI.result: In maze traversal, non-reasoning baselines fail, while LLMs using GraphWalk successfully solve the tasks by sequential navigation. On synthetic enterprise-like graphs with random labels, models using GraphWalk achieve substantially higher accuracy than in-context baselines for all 12 query types and across all model families. The performance gains grow with model and task scale, exactly where in-context approaches suffer severe degradation or fail outright. Each solution is accompanied by an explicit execution trace of graph operations.
AI.conclusion: Equipping LLMs with a minimal, general-purpose set of graph traversal tools enables effective, scalable multi-hop reasoning over large knowledge graphs without additional training. GraphWalk overcomes context window limitations, improves performance and robustness across tasks and model sizes, and yields interpretable, verifiable reasoning traces, suggesting that tool-based graph navigation is a superior paradigm to pure in-context graph reasoning for real-world, enterprise-scale KG applications.

title: LiveMathematicianBench: A Live Benchmark for Mathematician-Level Reasoning with Proof Sketches
summary: Mathematical reasoning is a hallmark of human intelligence, and whether large language models (LLMs) can meaningfully perform it remains a central question in artificial intelligence and cognitive science. As LLMs are increasingly integrated into scientific workflows, rigorous evaluation of their mathematical capabilities becomes a practical necessity. Existing benchmarks are limited by synthetic settings and data contamination. We present LiveMathematicianBench, a dynamic multiple-choice benchmark for research-level mathematical reasoning built from recent arXiv papers published after model training cutoffs. By grounding evaluation in newly published theorems, it provides a realistic testbed beyond memorized patterns. The benchmark introduces a thirteen-category logical taxonomy of theorem types (e.g., implication, equivalence, existence, uniqueness), enabling fine-grained evaluation across reasoning forms. It employs a proof-sketch-guided distractor pipeline that uses high-level proof strategies to construct plausible but invalid answer choices reflecting misleading proof directions, increasing sensitivity to genuine understanding over surface-level matching. We also introduce a substitution-resistant mechanism to distinguish answer recognition from substantive reasoning. Evaluation shows the benchmark is far from saturated: Gemini-3.1-pro-preview, the best model, achieves only 43.5%. Under substitution-resistant evaluation, accuracy drops sharply: GPT-5.4 scores highest at 30.6%, while Gemini-3.1-pro-preview falls to 17.6%, below the 20% random baseline. A dual-mode protocol reveals that proof-sketch access yields consistent accuracy gains, suggesting models can leverage high-level proof strategies for reasoning. Overall, LiveMathematicianBench offers a scalable, contamination-resistant testbed for studying research-level mathematical reasoning in LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LiveMathematicianBench, a dynamic benchmark built from recent arXiv theorems to rigorously test research-level mathematical reasoning in LLMs, revealing substantial performance gaps and contamination resistance.
AI.motivation: Existing mathematical benchmarks for LLMs suffer from synthetic problems and potential data contamination, making it unclear whether models truly reason or simply recall memorized patterns. As LLMs become embedded in scientific workflows, there is a need for a rigorous, realistic, and up-to-date benchmark that evaluates genuine research-level mathematical reasoning across diverse logical forms.
AI.method: The authors construct LiveMathematicianBench from new theorems in arXiv papers that appear after the training cutoffs of major LLMs, minimizing contamination. Each question is multiple-choice and categorized into a 13-type logical taxonomy (e.g., implication, equivalence, existence, uniqueness) to assess different reasoning forms. They design a proof-sketch-guided distractor pipeline, using high-level proof ideas to generate plausible but incorrect alternatives that reflect common misleading proof directions. Additionally, they implement a substitution-resistant mechanism to separate mere pattern recognition from true reasoning by altering answer representations so that simple memorization or substitution strategies fail. A dual-mode protocol evaluates models both with and without access to proof sketches, to measure the extent to which high-level proof strategies help.
AI.result: Benchmark evaluation shows that current state-of-the-art LLMs perform far from human-expert level. Gemini-3.1-pro-preview attains 43.5% accuracy in the standard setting, while under the stricter substitution-resistant evaluation, accuracies drop significantly: GPT-5.4 leads with 30.6%, and Gemini-3.1-pro-preview falls to 17.6%, below the 20% random-guess baseline. The dual-mode experiments reveal consistent accuracy improvements when models are given proof sketches, indicating that they can exploit high-level proof strategies but still struggle overall.
AI.conclusion: LiveMathematicianBench provides a scalable, dynamic, and contamination-resistant benchmark for evaluating research-level mathematical reasoning in LLMs. The low and often sub-random accuracies, particularly under substitution-resistant conditions, suggest that current models have limited genuine reasoning ability and may rely heavily on pattern recognition. The taxonomy and proof-sketch-based design enable fine-grained analysis of reasoning forms and show that access to high-level proof strategies helps but does not close the gap, establishing LiveMathematicianBench as a valuable tool for future work on improving mathematical reasoning in AI systems.

title: Analysis of LLM Performance on AWS Bedrock: Receipt-item Categorisation Case Study
comment: Preprint. Accepted to the 19th International Conference on Evaluation of Novel Approaches to Software Engineering (ENASE 2026). Final version to be published by SCITEPRESS, http://www.scitepress.org
summary: This paper presents a systematic, cost-aware evaluation of large language models (LLMs) for receipt-item categorisation within a production-oriented classification framework. We compare four instruction-tuned models available through AWS Bedrock: Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Claude 4 Sonnet, Mixtral 8x7B Instruct, and Mistral 7B Instruct. The aim of the study was (1) to assess performance across accuracy, response stability, and token-level cost, and (2) to investigate what prompting methods, zero-shot or few-shot, are especially appropriate both in terms of accuracy and in terms of incurred costs. Results of our experiments demonstrated that Claude 3.7 Sonnet achieves the most favourable balance between classification accuracy and cost efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates several instruction-tuned large language models for receipt-item categorisation, focusing on both accuracy and cost, and finds Claude 3.7 Sonnet offers the best trade-off.
AI.motivation: There is a need to reliably categorise receipt items in a production setting using LLMs, where not only classification accuracy but also response stability and operational cost matter. Existing work often emphasises accuracy alone without systematically accounting for token-level costs and prompt design trade-offs.
AI.method: The authors perform a systematic, cost-aware evaluation of four AWS Bedrock-hosted instruction-tuned LLMs—Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Claude 4 Sonnet, Mixtral 8x7B Instruct, and Mistral 7B Instruct—on a receipt-item categorisation task. They compare models along three dimensions: classification accuracy, response stability, and token-level cost, and additionally examine the impact of zero-shot vs. few-shot prompting strategies on both accuracy and cost.
AI.result: Claude 3.7 Sonnet shows the best overall balance between accuracy and cost efficiency among the evaluated models. The experiments also reveal how different prompting strategies (zero-shot vs. few-shot) affect both performance and incurred token costs, though the abstract highlights only the superior trade-off achieved by Claude 3.7 Sonnet.
AI.conclusion: Among the tested LLMs and prompting strategies for production-oriented receipt-item categorisation, Claude 3.7 Sonnet delivers the most favourable trade-off between accuracy and token-level cost, suggesting it is the most suitable choice in this context. The study underscores the importance of accounting for both performance and economic factors when deploying LLM-based classifiers in real-world systems.

title: Taming CATS: Controllable Automatic Text Simplification through Instruction Fine-Tuning with Control Tokens
summary: Controllable Automatic Text Simplification (CATS) produces user-tailored outputs, yet controllability is often treated as a decoding problem and evaluated with metrics that are not reflective to the measure of control. We observe that controllability in ATS is significantly constrained by data and evaluation. To this end, we introduce a domain-agnostic CATS framework based on instruction fine-tuning with discrete control tokens, steering open-source models to target readability levels and compression rates. Across three model families with different model sizes (Llama, Mistral, Qwen; 1-14B) and four domains (medicine, public administration, news, encyclopedic text), we find that smaller models (1-3B) can be competitive, but reliable controllability strongly depends on whether the training data encodes sufficient variation in the target attribute. Readability control (FKGL, ARI, Dale-Chall) is learned consistently, whereas compression control underperforms due to limited signal variability in the existing corpora. We further show that standard simplification and similarity metrics are insufficient for measuring control, motivating error-based measures for target-output alignment. Finally, our sampling and stratification experiments demonstrate that naive splits can introduce distributional mismatch that undermines both training and evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a controllable automatic text simplification (CATS) framework using instruction fine-tuning with discrete control tokens, showing that control is limited mainly by data variation and evaluation practices rather than by decoding alone.
AI.motivation: Existing controllable automatic text simplification methods mostly treat controllability as a decoding issue and evaluate with generic simplification metrics that do not directly measure how well the model follows control constraints (e.g., target readability or compression). The authors observe that controllability is in practice constrained by the training data and by inadequate evaluation measures, motivating a more principled framework that is domain-agnostic, explicitly control-aware, and better evaluated against target attributes.
AI.method: They develop an instruction fine-tuning framework for controllable ATS that uses discrete control tokens to steer open-source language models toward specified readability levels and compression rates. The framework is applied across several model families (Llama, Mistral, Qwen) and parameter sizes (1–14B), and tested on four domains (medicine, public administration, news, encyclopedic text). The training data is organized so that control tokens correspond to target attribute values (e.g., ranges of FKGL, ARI, Dale–Chall or compression ratios). They also design evaluation protocols that focus on target-output alignment, including error-based measures that quantify deviations from requested control values, and perform sampling and stratification experiments to study how data splits affect both training and evaluation reliability.
AI.result: Smaller open-source models (1–3B parameters) can perform competitively within this CATS framework, but reliable controllability is only achieved when the training data exhibits enough variation in the controlled attributes. Readability control, as measured by FKGL, ARI, and Dale–Chall indices, is learned robustly across models and domains. In contrast, control over compression rate is substantially weaker, which the authors attribute to limited variability in compression signals in existing corpora. They also find that common simplification and similarity metrics (e.g., general text quality or similarity scores) fail to capture how well models follow the requested controls, whereas their error-based measures better reflect control performance. Furthermore, they show that naive data splits cause distribution shifts between train and test that degrade both controllability and the validity of evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Controllability in automatic text simplification is primarily constrained by the variability and structure of the training data and by evaluation methods that do not explicitly measure control. Instruction fine-tuning with discrete control tokens can yield robust, domain-agnostic control over readability—even with relatively small models—provided that the data encodes sufficient attribute variation. Compression control remains challenging under current corpora. The authors advocate for control-aware, error-based evaluation metrics and for carefully sampled and stratified data splits to avoid distributional mismatches that compromise both model training and fair assessment of controllability.

title: OSCAR: Orchestrated Self-verification and Cross-path Refinement
summary: Diffusion language models (DLMs) expose their denoising trajectories, offering a natural handle for inference-time control; accordingly, an ideal hallucination mitigation framework should intervene during generation using this model-native signal rather than relying on an externally trained hallucination classifier. Toward this, we formulate commitment uncertainty localization: given a denoising trajectory, identify token positions whose cross-chain entropy exceeds an unsupervised threshold before factually unreliable commitments propagate into self-consistent but incorrect outputs. We introduce a suite of trajectory-level assessments, including a cross-chain divergence-at-hallucination (CDH) metric, for principled comparison of localization methods. We also introduce OSCAR, a training-free inference-time framework operationalizing this formulation. OSCAR runs N parallel denoising chains with randomized reveal orders, computes cross-chain Shannon entropy to detect high-uncertainty positions, and then performs targeted remasking conditioned on retrieved evidence. Ablations confirm that localization and correction contribute complementary gains, robust across N in {4, 8, 16}. On TriviaQA, HotpotQA, RAGTruth, and CommonsenseQA using LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B, OSCAR enhances generation quality by significantly reducing hallucinated content and improving factual accuracy through uncertainty-guided remasking, which also facilitates more effective integration of retrieved evidence. Its native entropy-based uncertainty signal surpasses that of specialized trained detectors, highlighting an inherent capacity of diffusion language models to identify factual uncertainty that is not present in the sequential token commitment structure of autoregressive models. We are releasing the codebase1 to support future research on localization and uncertainty-aware generation in DLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OSCAR, an inference-time hallucination mitigation framework for diffusion language models that uses native entropy signals from multiple denoising trajectories to localize and correct uncertain, potentially hallucinated tokens via targeted remasking and retrieval conditioning.
AI.motivation: Diffusion language models reveal full denoising trajectories, providing rich internal uncertainty signals that could be exploited to detect and mitigate hallucinations. Existing approaches mostly rely on external hallucination classifiers or are designed for autoregressive models, missing this opportunity. The authors aim to leverage trajectory-level information in DLMs to identify and correct factually unreliable content during generation without extra training.
AI.method: They formalize “commitment uncertainty localization,” defining how to detect token positions in a denoising trajectory whose cross-chain entropy exceeds an unsupervised threshold, signaling unreliable commitments. They propose trajectory-level evaluation tools, including the cross-chain divergence-at-hallucination (CDH) metric, to compare localization strategies. Building on this, they design OSCAR, a training-free framework that runs N parallel denoising chains with randomized token reveal orders, computes cross-chain Shannon entropy at each position, flags high-uncertainty tokens, and then remasks and regenerates those tokens using retrieved external evidence to steer the model towards factual outputs.
AI.result: Ablation studies show that both the localization (uncertainty detection) and correction (remasking with retrieval) components contribute complementary performance gains, stable for N in {4, 8, 16}. On benchmarks like TriviaQA, HotpotQA, RAGTruth, and CommonsenseQA with LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B, OSCAR significantly reduces hallucinations and improves factual accuracy. Their entropy-based native uncertainty signal outperforms specialized trained hallucination detectors, confirming that DLMs inherently encode useful signals for factual uncertainty that autoregressive models lack in their sequential commitment process.
AI.conclusion: OSCAR demonstrates that diffusion language models can natively support effective hallucination mitigation by exploiting cross-trajectory entropy to localize uncertain tokens and remask them in conjunction with retrieved evidence. The proposed commitment uncertainty localization formulation, metrics such as CDH, and released codebase together establish a foundation for future work on uncertainty-aware generation and trajectory-level control in DLMs, and highlight advantages of DLMs over autoregressive LMs for controlling hallucinations.

title: DEFT: Distribution-guided Efficient Fine-Tuning for Human Alignment
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), using algorithms like Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), aligns Large Language Models (LLMs) with human values but is costly and unstable. Alternatives have been proposed to replace PPO or integrate Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and contrastive learning for direct fine-tuning and value alignment. However, these methods still require voluminous data to learn preferences and may weaken the generalization ability of LLMs. To further enhance alignment efficiency and performance while mitigating the loss of generalization ability, this paper introduces Distribution-guided Efficient Fine-Tuning (DEFT), an efficient alignment framework incorporating data filtering and distributional guidance by calculating the differential distribution reward based on the output distribution of language model and the discrepancy distribution of preference data. A small yet high-quality subset is filtered from the raw data using a differential distribution reward, which is then incorporated into existing alignment methods to guide the model's output distribution. Experimental results demonstrate that the methods enhanced by DEFT outperform the original methods in both alignment capability and generalization ability, with significantly reduced training time.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes DEFT, a data-efficient alignment framework that filters a small, high-quality subset of preference data using a distribution-based reward and uses it to guide existing alignment methods, improving both alignment and generalization while reducing training cost.
AI.motivation: Existing RLHF approaches such as PPO are computationally expensive, unstable, and require large volumes of human feedback data. Newer alternatives that mix SFT and contrastive learning still depend on a lot of preference data and can hurt the generalization ability of LLMs. There is a need for an alignment method that is more data-efficient, stable, and that preserves or improves generalization.
AI.method: The authors introduce Distribution-guided Efficient Fine-Tuning (DEFT). DEFT computes a "differential distribution reward" based on (1) the output distribution of the language model and (2) the discrepancy distribution derived from preference data. Using this reward, the framework filters a small, high-quality subset of alignment data from the raw preference dataset. This curated subset is then plugged into existing alignment pipelines (e.g., RLHF variants or direct preference optimization methods) to guide the model’s output distribution more effectively and efficiently.
AI.result: When existing alignment methods are augmented with DEFT’s distribution-guided data filtering and training, they achieve better alignment with human preferences and better generalization performance than the same methods without DEFT. Additionally, the training becomes more efficient, with significantly reduced training time due to the use of a smaller, higher-quality data subset.
AI.conclusion: DEFT provides an efficient and effective way to align LLMs with human preferences by leveraging distribution-based rewards to select and use only the most informative alignment data. This improves both alignment quality and generalization, while lowering computational cost and training time, and can be integrated into a variety of existing alignment methods.

title: Exploring Robust Multi-Agent Workflows for Environmental Data Management
summary: Embedding LLM-driven agents into environmental FAIR data management is compelling - they can externalize operational knowledge and scale curation across heterogeneous data and evolving conventions. However, replacing deterministic components with probabilistic workflows changes the failure mode: LLM pipelines may generate plausible but incorrect outputs that pass superficial checks and propagate into irreversible actions such as DOI minting and public release. We introduce EnviSmart, a production data management system deployed on campus-wide storage infrastructure for environmental research. EnviSmart treats reliability as an architectural property through two mechanisms: a three-track knowledge architecture that externalizes behaviors (governance constraints), domain knowledge (retrievable context), and skills (tool-using procedures) as persistent, interlocking artifacts; and a role-separated multi-agent design where deterministic validators and audited handoffs restore fail-stop semantics at trust boundaries before irreversible steps. We compare two production deployments. The University's GIS Center Ecological Archive (849 curated datasets) serves as a single-agent baseline. SF2Bench, a compound flooding benchmark comprising 2,452 monitoring stations and 8,557 published files spanning 39 years, validates the multi-agent workflow. The multi-agent approach improved both efficiency - completed by a single operator in two days with repeated artifact reuse across deployments - and reliability: audited handoffs detected and blocked a coordinate transformation error affecting all 2,452 stations before publication. A representative incident (ISS-004) demonstrated boundary-based containment with 10-minute detection latency, zero user exposure, and 80-minute resolution. This paper has been accepted at PEARC 2026.

AI.tldr: The paper presents EnviSmart, a production system that integrates LLM agents into environmental FAIR data management while preserving reliability through a structured knowledge architecture and role-separated multi-agent workflow.
AI.motivation: Environmental data management needs scalable, flexible curation across heterogeneous datasets and evolving standards. LLM-based agents can help, but they introduce probabilistic failures, where outputs may look correct yet be wrong and lead to irreversible errors such as incorrect DOIs or faulty public datasets. The authors aim to gain the benefits of LLMs without sacrificing the fail-stop reliability traditionally expected in data infrastructure.
AI.method: They design and deploy EnviSmart, a data management system embedded into a campus-wide storage infrastructure.
