The system enforces reliability via (1) a three-track knowledge architecture that separates and persists governance constraints (behaviors), domain knowledge (retrievable context), and skills (tool-using procedures), and (2) a role-separated multi-agent design where deterministic validators and audited handoffs re-establish fail-stop semantics at key trust boundaries before irreversible actions. They evaluate EnviSmart in two production deployments: a single-agent baseline in the University's GIS Center Ecological Archive and a multi-agent workflow for SF2Bench, a large compound flooding benchmark dataset.
AI.result: In the SF2Bench deployment, a single operator curated 2,452 monitoring stations and 8,557 files over 39 years of data in two days by reusing artifacts, illustrating efficiency gains. Reliability improved as well: the multi-agent workflow’s audited handoffs detected and blocked a systematic coordinate transformation error that would have affected all 2,452 stations pre-publication. In an incident (ISS-004), the system showed rapid detection (10 minutes), no exposure to users, and full resolution within 80 minutes, demonstrating effective error containment at trust boundaries.
AI.conclusion: EnviSmart shows that LLM-driven data curation can be made production-grade by treating reliability as an architectural property. Separating knowledge into governance, domain context, and skills, and using role-separated multi-agent workflows with deterministic validators and audited handoffs, enables both scalable efficiency and robust failure containment for environmental FAIR data management. The system’s real-world deployments confirm that it can catch serious errors before publication while significantly speeding up curation work.

title: PLOT: Enhancing Preference Learning via Optimal Transport
summary: Preference learning in Large Language Models (LLMs) has advanced significantly, yet existing methods remain limited by modest performance gains, high computational costs, hyperparameter sensitivity, and insufficient modeling of global token-level relationships. We introduce PLOT, which enhances Preference Learning in fine-tuning-based alignment through a token-level loss derived from Optimal Transport. By formulating preference learning as an Optimal Transport Problem, PLOT aligns model outputs with human preferences while preserving the original distribution of LLMs, ensuring stability and robustness. Furthermore, PLOT leverages token embeddings to capture semantic relationships, enabling globally informed optimization. Experiments across two preference categories - Human Values and Logic & Problem Solving - spanning seven subpreferences demonstrate that PLOT consistently improves alignment performance while maintaining fluency and coherence. These results substantiate optimal transport as a principled methodology for preference learning, establishing a theoretically grounded framework that provides new insights for preference learning of LLMs.

AI.tldr: PLOT is a token-level preference learning method for aligning LLMs using an optimal-transport-based loss, improving alignment with human values and reasoning while remaining stable and fluent.
AI.motivation: Existing preference-learning-based alignment methods for LLMs provide only modest performance gains, are computationally heavy, require careful hyperparameter tuning, and fail to adequately model global token-level relationships between model outputs and human preference signals. The authors want a more principled, stable, and semantically aware way to align LLMs with human preferences.
AI.method: They formulate preference learning as an Optimal Transport (OT) problem between model outputs and human-preferred outputs at the token level. Based on this, they define PLOT, a new token-level loss for fine-tuning-based alignment. PLOT uses token embeddings to define a cost structure for OT, capturing semantic relationships among tokens and enabling globally informed optimization across whole sequences rather than only local or pairwise comparisons. This loss is used during fine-tuning to adjust model parameters so that outputs are transported towards human-preferred distributions while staying close to the original LLM distribution.
AI.result: Across two broad preference categories—Human Values and Logic & Problem Solving—with seven sub-preferences, PLOT yields consistent improvements in alignment metrics compared to baselines, without degrading generation quality in terms of fluency and coherence. Empirical results show that the OT-based loss improves alignment robustness and effectiveness over prior preference-learning approaches.
AI.conclusion: Optimal Transport offers a principled and theoretically grounded way to structure preference learning for LLM alignment. PLOT, the proposed OT-based token-level loss, achieves better alignment with human values and reasoning preferences while preserving the fluency and stability of the base model, suggesting that OT-based formulations are a promising direction for future alignment research.

title: From Guessing to Placeholding: A Cost-Theoretic Framework for Uncertainty-Aware Code Completion
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional proficiency in code completion, they typically adhere to a Hard Completion (HC) paradigm, compelling the generation of fully concrete code even amidst insufficient context. Our analysis of 3 million real-world interactions exposes the limitations of this strategy: 61% of the generated suggestions were either edited after acceptance or rejected despite exhibiting over 80% similarity to the user's subsequent code, suggesting that models frequently make erroneous predictions at specific token positions. Motivated by this observation, we propose Adaptive Placeholder Completion (APC), a collaborative framework that extends HC by strategically outputting explicit placeholders at high-entropy positions, allowing users to fill directly via IDE navigation. Theoretically, we formulate code completion as a cost-minimization problem under uncertainty. Premised on the observation that filling placeholders incurs lower cost than correcting errors, we prove the existence of a critical entropy threshold above which APC achieves strictly lower expected cost than HC. We instantiate this framework by constructing training data from filtered real-world edit logs and design a cost-based reward function for reinforcement learning. Extensive evaluations across 1.5B--14B parameter models demonstrate that APC reduces expected editing costs from 19% to 50% while preserving standard HC performance. Our work provides both a theoretical foundation and a practical training framework for uncertainty-aware code completion, demonstrating that adaptive abstention can be learned end-to-end without sacrificing conventional completion quality.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Adaptive Placeholder Completion (APC), an uncertainty-aware code completion framework where the model can emit placeholders at high-entropy (uncertain) positions, letting users fill them, which theoretically and empirically reduces editing cost compared to standard hard completion (HC).
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based code completion tools follow a Hard Completion paradigm that always produces fully concrete code, even when the model is uncertain. Large-scale logs show that many suggestions are very similar to final user code but still require edits at a few incorrect positions, indicating that enforced full predictions cause costly errors. The authors aim to address this inefficiency by enabling models to explicitly abstain at uncertain positions rather than guessing and being wrong.
AI.method: They model code completion as a cost-minimization problem under uncertainty, comparing the costs of (1) users correcting wrong tokens from HC versus (2) users filling placeholders at uncertain positions. They prove that if filling placeholders is cheaper than fixing errors, there exists a critical entropy threshold above which the model should abstain (output placeholders). They then implement Adaptive Placeholder Completion by: (a) mining and filtering real-world IDE edit logs to construct training data with information about where placeholders would have been beneficial, and (b) designing a cost-based reward function for reinforcement learning so the model learns where to emit placeholders vs. concrete tokens. This approach is applied to LLMs ranging from 1.5B to 14B parameters.
AI.result: On extensive evaluations with models between 1.5B and 14B parameters, APC reduces expected editing cost between 19% and 50% relative to standard hard completion, while maintaining typical HC performance metrics. Empirical results confirm that APC’s selective use of placeholders leads to fewer costly edits without degrading the overall quality of concrete code completions.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive Placeholder Completion offers a principled and practical way to make code completion uncertainty-aware by allowing models to abstain selectively through placeholders. There exists a provable entropy threshold where APC is strictly better than HC in expected cost, and experiments show substantial reductions in user editing effort without harm to standard completion quality. Thus, adaptive abstention can be learned end-to-end and integrated into real-world code completion systems to improve usability and efficiency.

title: CORAL: Towards Autonomous Multi-Agent Evolution for Open-Ended Discovery
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based evolution is a promising approach for open-ended discovery, where progress requires sustained search and knowledge accumulation. Existing methods still rely heavily on fixed heuristics and hard-coded exploration rules, which limit the autonomy of LLM agents. We present CORAL, the first framework for autonomous multi-agent evolution on open-ended problems. CORAL replaces rigid control with long-running agents that explore, reflect, and collaborate through shared persistent memory, asynchronous multi-agent execution, and heartbeat-based interventions. It also provides practical safeguards, including isolated workspaces, evaluator separation, resource management, and agent session and health management. Evaluated on diverse mathematical, algorithmic, and systems optimization tasks, CORAL sets new state-of-the-art results on 10 tasks, achieving 3-10 times higher improvement rates with far fewer evaluations than fixed evolutionary search baselines across tasks. On Anthropic's kernel engineering task, four co-evolving agents improve the best known score from 1363 to 1103 cycles. Mechanistic analyses further show how these gains arise from knowledge reuse and multi-agent exploration and communication. Together, these results suggest that greater agent autonomy and multi-agent evolution can substantially improve open-ended discovery. Code is available at https://github.com/Human-Agent-Society/CORAL.

AI.tldr: CORAL is a framework for autonomous, multi-agent, LLM-driven evolution that improves open-ended problem-solving performance by enabling agents to explore, reflect, and collaborate using shared memory and robust runtime management.
AI.motivation: Open-ended discovery tasks (e.g., math, algorithms, systems optimization) need sustained, flexible exploration and knowledge accumulation. Existing LLM-based evolutionary methods still depend on rigid, hand-crafted heuristics and exploration rules, which constrain agent autonomy, limit reuse of learned knowledge, and often require many evaluations to make progress.
AI.method: The authors build CORAL, a long-running, multi-agent LLM framework that replaces fixed evolutionary control with autonomous agents that (1) explore solution spaces, (2) reflect on their attempts, and (3) collaborate via a shared persistent memory. The system uses asynchronous multi-agent execution and heartbeat-based interventions to keep agents active and coordinated. CORAL also includes engineering safeguards: isolated workspaces for safety, separation between solution generators and evaluators, explicit resource management, and mechanisms for session and health monitoring of agents. They evaluate CORAL on a suite of mathematical, algorithmic, and systems optimization benchmarks, including Anthropic's kernel engineering task, and compare it against fixed evolutionary search baselines.
AI.result: Across diverse tasks, CORAL achieves state-of-the-art results on 10 benchmarks, attaining 3–10× higher improvement rates while using significantly fewer evaluations than fixed-rule evolutionary baselines. In the kernel engineering task, a population of four co-evolving agents improves the best known performance score from 1363 to 1103 cycles. Mechanistic analyses show that the performance gains stem from effective knowledge reuse in shared memory, diverse exploration strategies across agents, and beneficial inter-agent communication.
AI.conclusion: Autonomous, long-running multi-agent LLM systems with shared memory and minimal hard-coded control can significantly enhance open-ended discovery. By enabling agents to evolve collaboratively, reuse accumulated knowledge, and operate under robust runtime management, CORAL outperforms traditional fixed-heuristic evolutionary approaches and establishes a new practical paradigm for open-ended problem solving.

title: Beyond Detection: Ethical Foundations for Automated Dyslexic Error Attribution
summary: Dyslexic spelling errors exhibit systematic phonological and orthographic patterns that distinguish them from the errors produced by typically developing writers. While this observation has motivated dyslexic-specific spell-checking and assistive writing tools, prior work has focused predominantly on error correction rather than attribution, and has largely neglected the ethical risks. The risk of harmful labelling, covert screening, algorithmic bias, and institutional misuse that automated classification of learners entails requires the development of robust ethical and legal frameworks for research in this area. This paper addresses both gaps. We formulate dyslexic error attribution as a binary classification task. Given a misspelt word and its correct target form, determine whether the error pattern is characteristic of a dyslexic or non-dyslexic writer. We develop a comprehensive feature set capturing orthographic, phonological, and morphological properties of each error, and propose a twin-input neural model evaluated against traditional machine learning baselines under writer-independent conditions. The neural model achieves 93.01% accuracy and an F1-score of 94.01%, with phonetically plausible errors and vowel confusions emerging as the strongest attribution signals. We situate these technical results within an explicit ethics-first framework, analysing fairness across subgroups, the interpretability requirements of educational deployment, and the conditions, consent, transparency, human oversight, and recourse, under which a system could be responsibly used. We provide concrete guidelines for ethical deployment and an open discussion of the systems limitations and misuse potential. Our results demonstrate that dyslexic error attribution is feasible at high accuracy while underscoring that feasibility alone is insufficient for deployment in high-stakes educational contexts.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a classifier that distinguishes dyslexic from non-dyslexic spelling error patterns with high accuracy and embeds this work in an ethics-first framework for responsible educational deployment.
AI.motivation: Dyslexic spelling errors show systematic patterns different from those of typically developing writers. While this has inspired assistive tools, prior work emphasizes correcting errors, not attributing them to dyslexia, and pays little attention to the ethical risks of automated classification such as harmful labelling, covert screening, bias, and misuse in educational settings. The authors aim to fill both the technical and ethical gaps.
AI.method: They cast dyslexic error attribution as a binary classification problem: given a misspelt word and its correct form, decide if the error is characteristic of a dyslexic or non-dyslexic writer. They design a rich feature set capturing orthographic, phonological, and morphological characteristics of each error, and propose a twin-input neural network model that takes both the misspelling and target word. This model is evaluated under writer-independent conditions against traditional machine learning baselines.
AI.result: The twin-input neural model outperforms baselines, achieving 93.01% accuracy and a 94.01% F1-score on the attribution task. Analysis shows that phonetically plausible errors and vowel confusions are the strongest predictive signals for dyslexic-like errors. They also examine subgroup fairness, interpretability, and other properties relevant to deployment.
AI.conclusion: Dyslexic error attribution from spelling patterns is technically feasible at high accuracy using a tailored neural model and rich linguistic features. However, the authors emphasize that technical feasibility is not sufficient for real-world, high-stakes educational use. They outline an ethics-first framework specifying fairness analysis, interpretability, informed consent, transparency, human oversight, and avenues for recourse as preconditions for any responsible deployment, and they discuss limitations and misuse risks of such systems.

title: Ontology-Aware Design Patterns for Clinical AI Systems: Translating Reification Theory into Software Architecture
comment: 7 design patterns, 3 tables, 1 figure, arXiv cs.AI preprint
summary: Clinical AI systems routinely train on health data structurally distorted by documentation workflows, billing incentives, and terminology fragmentation. Prior work has characterised the mechanisms of this distortion: the three-forces model of documentary enactment, the reification feedback loop through which AI may amplify coding artefacts, and terminology governance failures that allow semantic drift to accumulate. Yet translating these insights into implementable software architecture remains an open problem. This paper proposes seven ontology-aware design patterns in Gang-of-Four pattern language for building clinical AI pipelines resilient to ontological distortion. The patterns address data ingestion validation (Ontological Checkpoint), low-frequency signal preservation (Dormancy-Aware Pipeline), continuous drift monitoring (Drift Sentinel), parallel representation maintenance (Dual-Ontology Layer), feedback loop interruption (Reification Circuit Breaker), terminology evolution management (Terminology Version Gate), and pluggable regulatory compliance (Regulatory Compliance Adapter). Each pattern is specified with Problem, Forces, Solution, Consequences, Known Uses, and Related Patterns. We illustrate their composition in a reference architecture for a primary care AI system and provide a walkthrough tracing all seven patterns through a diabetes risk prediction scenario. This paper does not report empirical validation; it offers a design vocabulary grounded in theoretical analysis, subject to future evaluation in production systems. Three patterns have partial precedent in existing systems; the remaining four have not been formally described. Limitations include the absence of runtime benchmarks and restriction to the German and EU regulatory context.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes seven reusable software design patterns to make clinical AI pipelines robust against distortions caused by medical documentation and terminology issues, offering a theoretically grounded architecture but no empirical evaluation yet.
AI.motivation: Clinical AI models are often trained on electronic health record data that are systematically distorted by documentation practices, billing incentives, and fragmented or drifting terminologies. Prior conceptual work has described how these distortions arise and how AI can reinforce them, but there is a gap in translating these theoretical insights into concrete, implementable software architectures that practitioners can adopt when building AI systems for healthcare.
AI.method: The authors use a design-patterns approach, following the Gang-of-Four style, to formalize recurrent architectural solutions as ontology-aware design patterns for clinical AI systems. For each pattern, they specify the Problem, Forces, Solution, Consequences, Known Uses, and Related Patterns. They then assemble these patterns into a reference architecture for a primary care AI system and demonstrate their use through a detailed diabetes risk prediction scenario. The work is conceptual and architectural rather than empirical.
AI.result: The paper defines seven patterns—Ontological Checkpoint, Dormancy-Aware Pipeline, Drift Sentinel, Dual-Ontology Layer, Reification Circuit Breaker, Terminology Version Gate, and Regulatory Compliance Adapter—describing how each can mitigate specific forms of ontological distortion in clinical AI pipelines. It shows how they compose into a reference architecture and notes that three patterns have precedents in existing systems while four are novel in formal description. No runtime metrics or empirical performance results are provided.
AI.conclusion: Ontology-aware design patterns can provide a shared vocabulary and set of architectural strategies for building clinical AI systems that are more robust to documentation bias and terminology drift. While the patterns are theoretically grounded and partially informed by existing practice, they remain to be validated, benchmarked, and potentially adapted beyond the German and EU regulatory contexts. The work is intended as a foundation for future empirical and practical refinement rather than as a complete, validated solution.

title: SURE: Synergistic Uncertainty-aware Reasoning for Multimodal Emotion Recognition in Conversations
summary: Multimodal emotion recognition in conversations (MERC) requires integrating multimodal signals while being robust to noise and modeling contextual reasoning. Existing approaches often emphasize fusion but overlook uncertainty in noisy features and fine-grained reasoning. We propose SURE (Synergistic Uncertainty-aware REasoning) for MERC, a framework that improves robustness and contextual modeling. SURE consists of three components: an Uncertainty-Aware Mixture-of-Experts module to handle modality-specific noise, an Iterative Reasoning module for multi-turn reasoning over context, and a Transformer Gate module to capture intra- and inter-modal interactions. Experiments on benchmark MERC datasets show that SURE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating its effectiveness in robust multimodal reasoning. These results highlight the importance of uncertainty modeling and iterative reasoning in advancing emotion recognition in conversational settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SURE, a multimodal emotion recognition framework that is robust to noisy inputs and performs better contextual reasoning in conversations.
AI.motivation: Multimodal emotion recognition in conversations needs to combine signals like text, audio, and video while dealing with noise and capturing context across turns. Existing work largely focuses on how to fuse modalities, but treats all features as equally reliable and often lacks fine-grained, multi-turn reasoning. This leads to poor robustness when modalities are noisy and weak exploitation of conversational context.
AI.method: The authors propose SURE (Synergistic Uncertainty-aware REasoning), which has three main components: (1) an Uncertainty-Aware Mixture-of-Experts module that assesses and down-weights noisy or unreliable modality-specific features; (2) an Iterative Reasoning module that performs multi-step reasoning across conversational turns to refine emotional understanding using context; and (3) a Transformer Gate module that models both intra-modal and inter-modal interactions, controlling information flow between modalities and within each modality over time.
AI.result: On standard MERC benchmark datasets, SURE achieves consistently better performance than prior state-of-the-art multimodal emotion recognition methods, indicating improved robustness and contextual modeling. The experiments empirically validate that explicitly modeling uncertainty and using iterative reasoning enhances recognition accuracy in conversational settings.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that robust multimodal emotion recognition in conversations benefits substantially from explicitly handling modality-specific uncertainty and leveraging iterative, context-aware reasoning. SURE offers a principled framework that integrates these ideas and sets new performance levels on benchmark MERC datasets, suggesting a promising direction for future work in multimodal conversational understanding.

title: ContextBudget: Budget-Aware Context Management for Long-Horizon Search Agents
summary: LLM-based agents show strong potential for long-horizon reasoning, yet their context size is limited by deployment factors (e.g., memory, latency, and cost), yielding a constrained context budget. As interaction histories grow, this induces a trade-off between retaining past information and staying within the context limit. To address this challenge, we propose Budget-Aware Context Management (BACM), which formulates context management as a sequential decision problem with a context budget constraint. It enables agents to assess the available budget before incorporating new observations and decide when and how much of the interaction history to compress. We further develop BACM-RL, an end-to-end curriculum-based reinforcement learning approach that learns compression strategies under varying context budgets. Experiments on compositional multi-objective QA and long-horizon web browsing benchmarks show that BACM-RL consistently outperforms prior methods across model scales and task complexities, achieving over $1.6\times$ gains over strong baselines in high-complexity settings, while maintaining strong advantages as budgets shrink, where most methods exhibit a downward performance trend.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Budget-Aware Context Management (BACM), a framework that treats context management for LLM-based agents as a budgeted sequential decision problem, and BACM-RL, a reinforcement-learning method that learns when and how much to compress history under varying context budgets, yielding large performance gains on long-horizon tasks compared to prior methods, especially under tight context limits.
AI.motivation: LLM agents are promising for long-horizon reasoning and interaction, but they are bottlenecked by limited context windows due to memory, latency, and cost constraints. As conversations or interaction histories grow, models must choose what to keep, discard, or compress. Existing context management or summarization methods are typically heuristic, static, or not explicitly optimized for a constrained context budget, causing degraded performance on complex or long-horizon tasks. The paper aims to design a principled, learnable mechanism that optimally manages context under explicit budget constraints so that agents can reason effectively over long histories without exceeding deployment limits.
AI.method: The authors propose Budget-Aware Context Management (BACM), which casts the problem of deciding which past information to retain or compress as a sequential decision-making problem with an explicit context budget. At each step, the agent observes the current budget and new observations, then decides whether and how much of the interaction history to compress so that future reasoning quality is maximized while remaining within budget. On top of this framework, they develop BACM-RL, an end-to-end reinforcement learning approach with curriculum training. BACM-RL learns compression policies by interacting with tasks that vary in context budget and complexity, gradually increasing difficulty so the model can adapt its strategies. The policy learns not just what to compress but when to compress, optimizing for downstream task performance rather than heuristic objectives.
AI.result: On compositional multi-objective QA and long-horizon web browsing benchmarks, BACM-RL consistently outperforms previous context management and compression methods across various model sizes and task difficulties. In high-complexity settings, it achieves more than a 1.6× performance improvement over strong baselines. Unlike many existing approaches whose performance drops sharply as context budgets become smaller, BACM-RL maintains strong performance advantages even under tight budget constraints, indicating robust learned strategies for context compression and retention.
AI.conclusion: Treating context management as a budgeted sequential decision problem and training with reinforcement learning leads to more effective context usage for LLM-based agents on long-horizon tasks. BACM and BACM-RL enable agents to dynamically reason about their remaining context budget and adapt their compression behavior accordingly, yielding substantial performance gains over heuristic or static approaches, especially under challenging, high-complexity, and low-budget conditions. This suggests that learned, budget-aware context strategies are a promising direction for scaling LLM agents to longer interactions within practical deployment constraints.

title: Is Clinical Text Enough? A Multimodal Study on Mortality Prediction in Heart Failure Patients
summary: Accurate short-term mortality prediction in heart failure (HF) remains challenging, particularly when relying on structured electronic health record (EHR) data alone. We evaluate transformer-based models on a French HF cohort, comparing text-only, structured-only, multimodal, and LLM-based approaches. Our results show that enriching clinical text with entity-level representations improves prediction over CLS embeddings alone, and that supervised multimodal fusion of text and structured variables achieves the best overall performance. In contrast, large language models perform inconsistently across modalities and decoding strategies, with text-only prompts outperforming structured or multimodal inputs. These findings highlight that entity-aware multimodal transformers offer the most reliable solution for short-term HF outcome prediction, while current LLM prompting remains limited for clinical decision support.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates short-term mortality prediction in heart failure using different transformer-based models and data modalities, finding that entity-aware multimodal transformers outperform both text-only and LLM prompting approaches.
AI.motivation: Short-term mortality prediction in heart failure is clinically important but difficult when using only structured EHR data. There is growing interest in leveraging unstructured clinical text and recent transformer and LLM advances, yet it is unclear how best to combine structured variables, clinical narratives, and modern language models for reliable risk prediction in real-world cohorts.
AI.method: The authors evaluate multiple transformer-based architectures on a French heart failure cohort, systematically comparing: (1) text-only models using clinical notes; (2) structured-only models using coded EHR variables; (3) multimodal models that fuse text and structured data; and (4) large language model (LLM) prompting strategies. For text models, they contrast simple CLS-based document embeddings with enriched representations that incorporate entity-level information extracted from the clinical text. For multimodal models, they use supervised fusion mechanisms to integrate text embeddings with structured features for predicting short-term mortality. They also test different ways of feeding text, structured, or combined inputs into LLMs and different decoding strategies for prediction.
AI.result: Enriching clinical text with entity-level representations significantly improves mortality prediction compared with using only CLS embeddings. Supervised multimodal fusion of text and structured EHR variables yields the best overall predictive performance among all evaluated approaches. Large language models, when used via prompting, produce inconsistent results across modalities and decoding strategies; unexpectedly, text-only prompts perform better than those that include structured or multimodal inputs.
AI.conclusion: Entity-aware, supervised multimodal transformer models that jointly exploit clinical text and structured EHR data currently provide the most robust and accurate short-term mortality prediction in heart failure. In contrast, off-the-shelf LLM prompting is not yet reliable enough for clinical decision support in this setting, indicating that task-specific, multimodal transformer architectures remain preferable to generic LLM-based prompting for critical risk prediction tasks.

title: M3D-BFS: a Multi-stage Dynamic Fusion Strategy for Sample-Adaptive Multi-Modal Brain Network Analysis
summary: Multi-modal fusion is of great significance in neuroscience which integrates information from different modalities and can achieve better performance than uni-modal methods in downstream tasks. Current multi-modal fusion methods in brain networks, which mainly focus on structural connectivity (SC) and functional connectivity (FC) modalities, are static in nature. They feed different samples into the same model with identical computation, ignoring inherent difference between input samples. This lack of sample adaptation hinders model's further performance. To this end, we innovatively propose a multi-stage dynamic fusion strategy (M3D-BFS) for sample-adaptive multi-modal brain network analysis. Unlike other static fusion methods, we design different mixture-of-experts (MoEs) for uni- and multi-modal representations where modules can adaptively change as input sample changes during inference. To alleviate issue of MoE where training of experts may be collapsed, we divide our method into 3 stages. We first train uni-modal encoders respectively, then pretrain single experts of MoEs before finally finetuning the whole model. A multi-modal disentanglement loss is designed to enhance the final representations. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work for dynamic fusion for multi-modal brain network analysis. Extensive experiments on different real-world datasets demonstrates the superiority of M3D-BFS.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a dynamic, sample-adaptive multi-modal brain network fusion framework (M3D-BFS) based on mixture-of-experts, achieving better performance than static fusion methods on real datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-modal brain network fusion methods (typically combining structural and functional connectivity) treat all samples identically with static architectures and computations. This ignores inter-subject variability and limits performance in downstream neuroscience tasks. There is a need for sample-adaptive, dynamic fusion mechanisms that can better exploit complementary information across modalities while remaining trainable and stable.
AI.method: They introduce M3D-BFS, a multi-stage dynamic fusion strategy for multi-modal brain network analysis. The model uses separate mixture-of-experts (MoE) modules for uni-modal and multi-modal representations so that different experts are activated depending on each input sample, enabling dynamic computation. To address expert collapse in MoE training, they adopt a three-stage training pipeline: (1) independently train uni-modal encoders for each modality, (2) pretrain MoE with single experts, and (3) jointly finetune the full model with all experts active. Additionally, they design a multi-modal disentanglement loss to encourage more informative and complementary fused representations.
AI.result: On several real-world brain network datasets, M3D-BFS outperforms existing static multi-modal fusion baselines in downstream tasks, demonstrating improved effectiveness and robustness of dynamic fusion. The experiments support the benefits of both the MoE-based dynamic architecture and the training strategy with disentanglement loss.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, sample-adaptive fusion via mixture-of-experts is more effective than static approaches for multi-modal brain network analysis. The proposed M3D-BFS framework, with its staged training and disentanglement loss, offers a practical and superior solution for integrating structural and functional connectivity, and sets a new direction for dynamic multi-modal fusion in neuroscience applications.

title: ImplicitBBQ: Benchmarking Implicit Bias in Large Language Models through Characteristic Based Cues
summary: Large Language Models increasingly suppress biased outputs when demographic identity is stated explicitly, yet may still exhibit implicit biases when identity is conveyed indirectly. Existing benchmarks use name based proxies to detect implicit biases, which carry weak associations with many social demographics and cannot extend to dimensions like age or socioeconomic status. We introduce ImplicitBBQ, a QA benchmark that evaluates implicit bias through characteristic based cues, culturally associated attributes that signal implicitly, across age, gender, region, religion, caste, and socioeconomic status. Evaluating 11 models, we find that implicit bias in ambiguous contexts is over six times higher than explicit bias in open weight models. Safety prompting and chain-of-thought reasoning fail to substantially close this gap; even few-shot prompting, which reduces implicit bias by 84%, leaves caste bias at four times the level of any other dimension. These findings indicate that current alignment and prompting strategies address the surface of bias evaluation while leaving culturally grounded stereotypic associations largely unresolved. We publicly release our code and dataset for model providers and researchers to benchmark potential mitigation techniques.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ImplicitBBQ, a benchmark to measure implicit biases in large language models using indirect characteristic-based cues rather than explicit demographic labels or name proxies.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations show that LLMs suppress biased outputs when demographic identities are explicit, but they may still harbor implicit biases when those identities are only suggested. Existing implicit bias benchmarks rely mostly on names, which are weak or inapplicable proxies for many demographics (e.g., age, socioeconomic status) and do not robustly capture culturally grounded stereotypes. There is a need for a systematic way to test whether alignment methods genuinely reduce such implicit, culturally embedded biases.
AI.method: The authors design ImplicitBBQ, a question-answering benchmark where demographic information is conveyed implicitly via characteristic-based cues and culturally associated attributes, spanning multiple dimensions: age, gender, region, religion, caste, and socioeconomic status. They pose ambiguous and explicit contexts that can trigger stereotypes, then evaluate 11 language models on their responses. They compare levels of implicit versus explicit bias and test mitigation approaches including safety prompting, chain-of-thought reasoning, and few-shot prompting.
AI.result: Across 11 models, implicit bias in ambiguous settings is more than six times higher than explicit bias in open-weight models. Safety prompts and chain-of-thought reasoning yield little reduction in this gap. Few-shot prompting substantially reduces implicit bias overall (by about 84%), but even then, caste-related bias remains roughly four times higher than bias for other demographic dimensions.
AI.conclusion: Current alignment and prompting strategies mainly suppress bias where it is directly measurable via explicit demographic labels, but they leave deeper, culturally grounded implicit stereotypes largely intact. ImplicitBBQ exposes this discrepancy and offers a public benchmark and codebase for model developers and researchers to evaluate and develop more robust bias mitigation techniques that target implicit, characteristic-based cues.

title: Hierarchical Memory Orchestration for Personalized Persistent Agents
summary: While long-term memory is essential for intelligent agents to maintain consistent historical awareness, the accumulation of extensive interaction data often leads to performance bottlenecks. Naive storage expansion increases retrieval noise and computational latency, overwhelming the reasoning capacity of models deployed on constrained personal devices. To address this, we propose Hierarchical Memory Orchestration (HMO), a framework that organizes interaction history into a three-tiered directory driven by user-centric contextual relevance. Our system maintains a compact primary cache, coupling recent and pivotal memories with an evolving user profile to ensure agent reasoning remains aligned with individual behavioral traits. This primary cache is complemented by a high-priority secondary layer, both of which are managed within a global archive of the full interaction history. Crucially, the user persona dictates memory redistribution across this hierarchy, promoting records mapped to long-term patterns toward more active tiers while relegating less relevant information. This targeted orchestration surfaces historical knowledge precisely when needed while maintaining a lean and efficient active search space. Evaluations on multiple benchmarks achieve state-of-the-art performance. Real-world deployments in ecosystems like OpenClaw demonstrate that HMO significantly enhances agent fluidity and personalization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Hierarchical Memory Orchestration (HMO), a three-tier memory management framework that keeps language agents efficient and personalized by organizing interaction history based on user-centric relevance.
AI.motivation: Long-term memory is crucial for intelligent agents to maintain consistent historical awareness and personalization, but accumulating large interaction histories causes retrieval noise, higher latency, and performance bottlenecks, especially on resource-constrained personal devices. The paper aims to overcome these limitations while preserving the benefits of rich history.
AI.method: The authors propose Hierarchical Memory Orchestration (HMO), which structures an agent’s memory into three layers: (1) a compact primary cache containing recent, pivotal memories and an evolving user profile, (2) a high-priority secondary layer, and (3) a global archive with the full interaction history. A user persona model dynamically redistributes memories across tiers according to user-centric contextual relevance and long-term behavioral patterns, promoting important records to active tiers and demoting less relevant ones.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that HMO achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to alternative memory management methods. In real-world deployments, such as in the OpenClaw ecosystem, HMO improves agent fluidity (responsiveness and coherence) and the degree of personalization perceived by users.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical Memory Orchestration is an effective way to manage long-term interaction histories for intelligent agents, enabling precise surfacing of relevant historical knowledge while keeping the active search space compact. This approach mitigates latency and noise issues, supports operation on constrained devices, and enhances both performance and personalization in real-world applications.

title: Reliable News or Propagandist News? A Neurosymbolic Model Using Genre, Topic, and Persuasion Techniques to Improve Robustness in Classification
summary:
Among news disorders, propagandist news are particularly insidious, because they tend to mix oriented messages with factual reports intended to look like reliable news. To detect propaganda, extant approaches based on Language Models such as BERT are promising but often overfit their training datasets, due to biases in data collection. To enhance classification robustness and improve generalization to new sources, we propose a neurosymbolic approach combining non-contextual text embeddings (fastText) with symbolic conceptual features such as genre, topic, and persuasion techniques. Results show improvements over equivalent text-only methods, and ablation studies as well as explainability analyses confirm the benefits of the added features.
  Keywords: Information disorder, Fake news, Propaganda, Classification, Topic modeling, Hybrid method, Neurosymbolic model, Ablation, Robustness

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a neurosymbolic model that combines fastText embeddings with symbolic conceptual features (genre, topic, persuasion techniques) to improve robustness and generalization in propaganda news detection, outperforming text-only baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing propaganda detection models based on contextual language models like BERT tend to overfit their training datasets, largely because of biases introduced during data collection. This limits their ability to generalize to new news sources and unseen data. The authors aim to create a more robust, generalizable approach to detecting propagandist news, which are particularly dangerous as they mix factual reporting with manipulative content.
AI.method: The authors design a neurosymbolic classification approach that combines non-contextual word embeddings (fastText) with higher-level, symbolic conceptual features. These features include inferred genre, topic distributions (likely from topic modeling), and indicators of specific persuasion techniques. The model integrates these heterogeneous representations to classify whether a news item is propagandist or not. They perform comparative experiments against text-only baselines and conduct ablation studies and explainability analyses.
AI.result: The neurosymbolic model consistently outperforms equivalent methods that rely only on text embeddings. Ablation studies show that removing symbolic conceptual features degrades performance, indicating their contribution to accuracy and robustness. Explainability analyses further support that genre, topic, and persuasion-technique features provide meaningful signals for detecting propaganda and make the model’s decisions more interpretable.
AI.conclusion: Combining non-contextual embeddings with symbolic conceptual features yields a more robust and generalizable approach to propaganda detection than text-only models. Neurosymbolic integration helps mitigate overfitting driven by dataset biases, improves performance on new sources, and increases interpretability. The approach demonstrates that enriching neural models with structured high-level information is a promising direction for tackling information disorder tasks.

title: Can Heterogeneous Language Models Be Fused?
summary: Model merging aims to integrate multiple expert models into a single model that inherits their complementary strengths without incurring the inference-time cost of ensembling. Recent progress has shown that merging can be highly effective when all source models are \emph{homogeneous}, i.e., derived from the same pretrained backbone and therefore share aligned parameter coordinates or compatible task vectors. Yet this assumption is increasingly unrealistic in open model ecosystems, where useful experts are often built on different families such as Llama, Qwen, and Mistral. In such \emph{heterogeneous} settings, direct weight-space fusion becomes ill-posed due to architectural mismatch, latent basis misalignment, and amplified cross-source conflict. We address this problem with \texttt{HeteroFusion} for heterogeneous language model fusion, which consists of two key components: topology-based alignment that transfers knowledge across heterogeneous backbones by matching functional module structures instead of raw tensor coordinates, and conflict-aware denoising that suppresses incompatible or noisy transfer signals during fusion. We further provide analytical justification showing that preserving the target adapter basis while predicting structured updates leads to a stable and well-conditioned transfer process. Across heterogeneous transfer, multi-source fusion, noisy-source robustness, and cross-family generalization settings, \texttt{HeteroFusion} consistently outperforms strong merging, fusion, and ensemble baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes HeteroFusion, a method to merge heterogeneous language models (from different families like Llama, Qwen, Mistral) into a single model that outperforms existing merging, fusion, and ensemble baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing model merging techniques work well only for homogeneous models that share the same backbone, which is unrealistic in open ecosystems where models come from different architectures. Direct weight fusion across heterogeneous models fails due to architectural mismatch, misaligned latent bases, and conflicting parameters, motivating a method that can reliably merge such heterogeneous experts.
AI.method: HeteroFusion introduces (1) topology-based alignment that transfers knowledge by matching functional module structures rather than raw parameter coordinates, and (2) conflict-aware denoising that suppresses incompatible or noisy transfer signals during fusion. The method preserves the target adapter basis while learning structured updates, which is analytically shown to yield a stable and well-conditioned transfer process.
AI.result: In experiments covering heterogeneous transfer, multi-source fusion, robustness to noisy sources, and cross-family generalization, HeteroFusion consistently outperforms strong baselines in merging, fusion, and ensembling of language models built on different backbones.
AI.conclusion: HeteroFusion effectively enables reliable fusion of heterogeneous language model experts by aligning models at the functional/topological level and denoising conflicting signals, providing a stable way to integrate complementary strengths across different model families and improving over prior merging and ensemble methods.

title: How to measure the optimality of word or gesture order with respect to the principle of swap distance minimization
summary: The structure of all the permutations of a sequence can be represented as a permutohedron, a graph where vertices are permutations and two vertices are linked if a swap of adjacent elements in the permutation of one of the vertices produces the permutation of the other vertex. It has been hypothesized that word orders in languages minimize the swap distance in the permutohedron: given a source order, word orders that are closer in the permutohedron should be less costly and thus more likely. Here we explain how to measure the degree of optimality of word order variation with respect to swap distance minimization. We illustrate the power of our novel mathematical framework by showing that crosslinguistic gestures are at least $77\%$ optimal. It is unlikely that the multiple times where crosslinguistic gestures hit optimality are due to chance. We establish the theoretical foundations for research on the optimality of word or gesture order with respect to swap distance minimization in communication systems. Finally, we introduce the quadratic assignment problem (QAP) into language research as an umbrella for multiple optimization problems and, accordingly, postulate a general principle of optimal assignment that unifies various linguistic principles including swap distance minimization.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how word and gesture orders in languages might be organized to minimize the number of adjacent swaps between alternative orders, using the geometry of the permutohedron.
AI.motivation: To test the hypothesis that observed word (and gesture) orders across languages are not arbitrary but tend to be arranged so that moving from one order to another requires few adjacent swaps, which would make them less cognitively or communicatively costly. The authors want a rigorous way to quantify and test this kind of optimality.
AI.method: They formalize word-order variation as positions on a permutohedron, with distance defined as the number of adjacent swaps (swap distance). They then define a mathematical framework to measure how close observed systems of orders (especially crosslinguistic gestures) are to an optimally swap-minimizing configuration. They cast the optimization as a quadratic assignment problem (QAP), linking it to a broader class of assignment/optimization problems in linguistics.
AI.result: Applying their framework to crosslinguistic gesture orders, they find that these systems are at least 77% as good as an ideal, perfectly swap-distance-minimizing system. They also observe several cases where the empirical configurations appear fully optimal, and show that such hits are unlikely to arise by chance alone.
AI.conclusion: Word and gesture orders in communication systems appear to be strongly shaped by the pressure to minimize adjacent-swap distances in the permutohedron, supporting an underlying optimality principle. The QAP provides a unifying formal umbrella for swap distance minimization and other linguistic optimization principles, suggesting a general principle of optimal assignment governing order patterns.

title: EvoSkills: Self-Evolving Agent Skills via Co-Evolutionary Verification
summary: Anthropic proposes the concept of skills for LLM agents to tackle multi-step professional tasks that simple tool invocations cannot address. A tool is a single, self-contained function, whereas a skill is a structured bundle of interdependent multi-file artifacts. Currently, skill generation is not only label-intensive due to manual authoring, but also may suffer from human--machine cognitive misalignment, which can lead to degraded agent performance, as evidenced by evaluations on SkillsBench. Therefore, we aim to enable agents to autonomously generate skills. However, existing self-evolving methods designed for tools cannot be directly applied to skills due to their increased complexity. To address these issues, we propose EvoSkills, a self-evolving skills framework that enables agents to autonomously construct complex, multi-file skill packages. Specifically, EvoSkills couples a Skill Generator that iteratively refines skills with a Surrogate Verifier that co-evolves to provide informative and actionable feedback without access to ground-truth test content. On SkillsBench, EvoSkills achieves the highest pass rate among five baselines on both Claude Code and Codex, and also exhibits strong generalization capabilities to six additional LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EvoSkills, a framework that lets LLM agents autonomously generate and refine complex multi-file “skills,” outperforming existing methods on SkillsBench and generalizing across multiple LLMs.
AI.motivation: Manual authoring of complex skills for LLM agents is labor-intensive and can create cognitive misalignment between human designers and model usage, reducing agent performance. Existing self-evolving approaches focus on simple tools (single functions) and do not handle the higher complexity and structure of multi-file skills. There is a need for an autonomous way for agents to construct and refine such skills without heavy labeling or ground-truth test content.
AI.method: The authors define skills as structured bundles of interdependent multi-file artifacts, distinct from single-function tools. They propose EvoSkills, which consists of two co-evolving components: (1) a Skill Generator that iteratively constructs and refines complex skill packages, and (2) a Surrogate Verifier that provides rich, actionable feedback to guide refinement. The Verifier learns to evaluate skills and offer guidance without relying on access to ground-truth test content, enabling closed-loop self-improvement. EvoSkills is evaluated on the SkillsBench benchmark with different LLM backbones.
AI.result: On SkillsBench, EvoSkills achieves the highest task pass rate among five baseline methods when used with Claude Code and Codex. The skills produced with EvoSkills also generalize well, improving performance when transferred to six additional LLMs, indicating robustness and cross-model applicability.
AI.conclusion: EvoSkills demonstrates that LLM agents can autonomously construct and refine complex, multi-file skills beyond simple tool usage. By coupling a self-improving Skill Generator with a co-evolving Surrogate Verifier that does not require ground-truth tests, the framework reduces human labeling effort, mitigates cognitive misalignment issues, and yields superior performance and generalization compared to existing self-evolving tool-centric baselines.

title: Diagnosing Translated Benchmarks: An Automated Quality Assurance Study of the EU20 Benchmark Suite
summary: Machine-translated benchmark datasets reduce costs and offer scale, but noise, loss of structure, and uneven quality weaken confidence. What matters is not merely whether we can translate, but also whether we can measure and verify translation reliability at scale. We study translation quality in the EU20 benchmark suite, which comprises five established benchmarks translated into 20 languages, via a three-step automated quality assurance approach: (i) a structural corpus audit with targeted fixes; (ii) quality profiling using a neural metric (COMET, reference-free and reference-based) with translation service comparisons (DeepL / ChatGPT / Google); and (iii) an LLM-based span-level translation error landscape. Trends are consistent: datasets with lower COMET scores exhibit a higher share of accuracy/mistranslation errors at span level (notably HellaSwag; ARC is comparatively clean). Reference-based COMET on MMLU against human-edited samples points in the same direction. We release cleaned/corrected versions of the EU20 datasets, and code for reproducibility. In sum, automated quality assurance offers practical, scalable indicators that help prioritize review -- complementing, not replacing, human gold standards.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates and improves the quality of a large machine‑translated benchmark suite (EU20) by introducing an automated, multi-step quality assurance pipeline, showing that automatic metrics and LLM-based analyses can reliably flag problematic data and guide selective human review.
AI.motivation: Machine-translated benchmarks are cheap and scalable but often noisy and unreliable, which can undermine any conclusions drawn from them. The authors want to understand how good these translated benchmarks actually are, identify where and how they fail, and develop scalable methods to assess and improve their quality without relying exclusively on costly human evaluation.
AI.method: They analyze the EU20 benchmark suite (five well-known benchmarks translated into 20 languages) using a three-step automated QA pipeline: (1) run a structural corpus audit to detect and patch structural inconsistencies or obvious artifacts; (2) perform quality profiling with the COMET neural quality metric (both reference-free and reference-based), while comparing outputs from different MT services (DeepL, ChatGPT, Google); and (3) use a large language model to produce span-level error annotations, building a fine-grained “translation error landscape” that categorizes issues such as accuracy and mistranslation errors across datasets.
AI.result: Across datasets, they find consistent patterns: benchmarks with lower COMET scores contain a higher proportion of accuracy/mistranslation errors at the span level, with HellaSwag notably noisy and ARC relatively clean. For MMLU, reference-based COMET scores, validated against human-edited samples, align with these trends, supporting the reliability of their automatic QA indicators. They also produce and release cleaned and corrected versions of the EU20 benchmarks along with the code used for their analyses.
AI.conclusion: Automated quality assurance, combining structural checks, neural quality metrics, and LLM-based span-level error analysis, can effectively and scalably highlight problematic segments in large machine-translated benchmarks. This approach cannot replace human gold standards but can serve as a practical, high-level filter that prioritizes where human review is most needed, thus making the maintenance and improvement of multilingual benchmark suites more feasible and trustworthy.

title: Scale over Preference: The Impact of AI-Generated Content on Online Content Ecology
summary: The rapid proliferation of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Content (AIGC) is fundamentally restructuring online content ecologies, necessitating a rigorous examination of its behavioral and distributional implications. Leveraging a comprehensive longitudinal dataset comprising tens of millions of users from a leading Chinese video-sharing platform, this study elucidated the distinct creation and consumption behaviors characterizing AIGC versus Human-Generated Content (HGC). We identified a prevalent scale-over-preference dynamic, wherein AIGC creators achieve aggregate engagement comparable to HGC creators through high-volume production, despite a marked consumer preference for HGC. Deeper analysis uncovered the ability of the algorithmic content distribution mechanism in moderating these competing interests regarding AIGC. These findings advocated for the implementation of AIGC-sensitive distribution algorithms and precise governance frameworks to ensure the long-term health of the online content platforms.

AI.tldr: Study compares behavior and impact of AI-generated vs human-generated videos on a major Chinese platform, finding AIGC can match overall engagement via volume despite users preferring human content, and algorithms play a key moderating role.
AI.motivation: With AIGC rapidly expanding on online platforms, there is limited empirical evidence about how AI vs human content differ in creation, consumption, and algorithmic distribution dynamics, and how this affects platform health and governance.
AI.method: Use a large-scale longitudinal dataset from a leading Chinese video-sharing platform, encompassing tens of millions of users, to quantify and compare creation patterns, user engagement, and algorithmic distribution behaviors for AIGC and HGC. Conduct deeper analyses of how recommendation algorithms modulate the tension between user preference for HGC and high-volume AIGC production.
AI.result: AIGC creators, despite users showing a clear preference for human-generated content at the per-item level, achieve total engagement similar to HGC creators by producing content at much higher volume, a pattern termed scale-over-preference. The platform’s algorithmic distribution mechanism can moderate or balance these competing forces between user preferences and AIGC supply.
AI.conclusion: Platforms need AIGC-aware recommendation and distribution algorithms, along with precise governance frameworks, to manage the trade-offs between efficiency, user preferences, and ecosystem health, thereby sustaining the long-term health of online content environments in the era of large-scale AIGC.

title: SAFE: Stepwise Atomic Feedback for Error correction in Multi-hop Reasoning
summary: Multi-hop QA benchmarks frequently reward Large Language Models (LLMs) for spurious correctness, masking ungrounded or flawed reasoning steps. To shift toward rigorous reasoning, we propose SAFE, a dynamic benchmarking framework that replaces the ungrounded Chain-of-Thought (CoT) with a strictly verifiable sequence of grounded entities. Our framework operates across two phases: (1) train-time verification, where we establish an atomic error taxonomy and a Knowledge Graph (KG)-grounded verification pipeline to eliminate noisy supervision in standard benchmarks, identifying up to 14% of instances as unanswerable, and (2) inference-time verification, where a feedback model trained on this verified dataset dynamically detects ungrounded steps in real-time. Experimental results demonstrate that SAFE not only exposes the critical flaws of existing benchmarks at train-time, but also significantly outperforms standard baselines, achieving an average accuracy gain of 8.4 pp while guaranteeing verifiable trajectories at inference-time.

AI.tldr: SAFE is a benchmarking framework that enforces verifiable, KG-grounded reasoning in multi-hop QA, exposing spurious correctness and improving both data quality and model accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-hop QA benchmarks often judge models only by final answers, so LLMs can appear correct even when their intermediate reasoning is ungrounded, flawed, or lucky. This spurious correctness hides real weaknesses in reasoning and limits progress on true multi-step reasoning abilities. The authors want a benchmark that rewards only rigorously grounded reasoning steps, not just correct end answers.
AI.method: They introduce SAFE, a two-phase dynamic framework. In train-time verification, they define an atomic error taxonomy and create a verification pipeline grounded in a Knowledge Graph (KG) to check and clean standard multi-hop QA benchmarks, marking noisy or unanswerable instances and discarding ungrounded supervision. In inference-time verification, they train a feedback model on this verified data to monitor model reasoning in real time, detecting ungrounded or erroneous steps and enforcing that reasoning chains consist of verifiable, KG-linked entities instead of free-form CoT text.
AI.result: Using SAFE, they find that up to 14% of instances in existing multi-hop QA benchmarks are actually unanswerable, revealing substantial label or supervision noise. Models evaluated under SAFE outperform standard baselines by an average of 8.4 percentage points in accuracy while also producing strictly verifiable reasoning trajectories during inference, showing both better performance and higher reasoning fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Replacing free-form CoT with KG-grounded, verifiable entity sequences and adding automated verification at both training and inference exposes flaws in current multi-hop QA benchmarks and leads to more reliable multi-step reasoning. SAFE demonstrates that enforcing grounded verification not only cleans benchmarks but also improves model accuracy and guarantees interpretable, checkable reasoning paths.

title: LiteInception: A Lightweight and Interpretable Deep Learning Framework for General Aviation Fault Diagnosis
summary: General aviation fault diagnosis and efficient maintenance are critical to flight safety; however, deploying deep learning models on resource-constrained edge devices poses dual challenges in computational capacity and interpretability. This paper proposes LiteInception--a lightweight interpretable fault diagnosis framework designed for edge deployment. The framework adopts a two-stage cascaded architecture aligned with standard maintenance workflows: Stage 1 performs high-recall fault detection, and Stage 2 conducts fine-grained fault classification on anomalous samples, thereby decoupling optimization objectives and enabling on-demand allocation of computational resources. For model compression, a multi-method fusion strategy based on mutual information, gradient analysis, and SE attention weights is proposed to reduce the input sensor channels from 23 to 15, and a 1+1 branch LiteInception architecture is introduced that compresses InceptionTime parameters by 70%, accelerates CPU inference by over 8x, with less than 3% F1 loss. Furthermore, knowledge distillation is introduced as a precision-recall regulation mechanism, enabling the same lightweight model to adapt to different scenarios--such as safety-critical and auxiliary diagnosis--by switching training strategies. Finally, a dual-layer interpretability framework integrating four attribution methods is constructed, providing traceable evidence chains of "which sensor x which time period." Experiments on the NGAFID dataset demonstrate a fault detection accuracy of 81.92% with 83.24% recall, and a fault identification accuracy of 77.00%, validating the framework's favorable balance among efficiency, accuracy, and interpretability.

AI.tldr: LiteInception is a lightweight, interpretable, two-stage deep learning framework for general aviation fault detection and diagnosis on edge devices, achieving large model compression and speedup with small performance loss while providing sensor-time based explanations.
AI.motivation: General aviation requires timely and reliable fault diagnosis to ensure flight safety, but typical deep learning models are too heavy for resource-constrained edge devices and lack interpretability, making them hard to deploy in real-world maintenance workflows.
AI.method: The paper designs a two-stage cascaded architecture where Stage 1 is a high-recall fault detector and Stage 2 is a fine-grained classifier for faulty samples, allowing separate optimization and adaptive computation. It applies a multi-method fusion channel selection scheme using mutual information, gradient analysis, and SE attention to prune input sensors from 23 to 15, and proposes a 1+1 branch LiteInception network that compresses InceptionTime by 70% and accelerates CPU inference by over 8x. Knowledge distillation is used to tune the precision–recall tradeoff for different application scenarios. A dual-layer interpretability framework integrating four attribution methods generates sensor-by-time explanations for model decisions.
AI.result: Using the NGAFID dataset, the framework achieves 81.92% accuracy and 83.24% recall for fault detection and 77.00% accuracy for fault identification, with more than 70% parameter reduction and over 8x CPU inference speedup and under 3% F1-score drop compared to the original model.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LiteInception framework offers a practical balance among efficiency, accuracy, and interpretability for aviation fault diagnosis on edge devices, aligning with real maintenance workflows, enabling scenario-dependent precision–recall adjustment, and providing traceable sensor-time evidence for model decisions.

title: $k$NNProxy: Efficient Training-Free Proxy Alignment for Black-Box Zero-Shot LLM-Generated Text Detection
summary: LLM-generated text (LGT) detection is essential for reliable forensic analysis and for mitigating LLM misuse. Existing LGT detectors can generally be categorized into two broad classes: learning-based approaches and zero-shot methods. Compared with learning-based detectors, zero-shot methods are particularly promising because they eliminate the need to train task-specific classifiers. However, the reliability of zero-shot methods fundamentally relies on the assumption that an off-the-shelf proxy LLM is well aligned with the often unknown source LLM, a premise that rarely holds in real-world black-box scenarios. To address this discrepancy, existing proxy alignment methods typically rely on supervised fine-tuning of the proxy or repeated interactions with commercial APIs, thereby increasing deployment costs, exposing detectors to silent API changes, and limiting robustness under domain shift. Motivated by these limitations, we propose the $k$-nearest neighbor proxy ($k$NNProxy), a training-free and query-efficient proxy alignment framework that repurposes the $k$NN language model ($k$NN-LM) retrieval mechanism as a domain adapter for a fixed proxy LLM. Specifically, a lightweight datastore is constructed once from a target-reflective LGT corpus, either via fixed-budget querying or from existing datasets. During inference, nearest-neighbor evidence induces a token-level predictive distribution that is interpolated with the proxy output, yielding an aligned prediction without proxy fine-tuning or per-token API outputs. To improve robustness under domain shift, we extend $k$NNProxy into a mixture of proxies (MoP) that routes each input to a domain-specific datastore for domain-consistent retrieval. Extensive experiments demonstrate strong detection performance of our method.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces kNNProxy, a training-free, query-efficient framework that aligns a proxy LLM to an unknown source LLM for detecting LLM-generated text, using kNN-based retrieval and mixture-of-proxies to handle domain shifts.
AI.motivation: LLM-generated text detection is important for forensics and preventing misuse, but existing detectors either require supervised training or rely on zero-shot assumptions that a proxy LLM is naturally aligned with the unknown source LLM. This assumption fails in realistic black-box settings, and current alignment solutions depend on costly fine-tuning or heavy API querying, making them fragile to domain shift and API changes. The authors are motivated to design a detector that can adapt a fixed proxy LLM to different target LLMs and domains without training or extensive queries.
AI.method: The authors propose kNNProxy, which reuses the kNN-LM retrieval idea as a domain adapter over a fixed proxy LLM. They first build a lightweight datastore from a target-reflective corpus of LLM-generated texts, obtained either via a limited number of queries or from existing data. Each token in this corpus is embedded and stored with its context. At inference, for a given input, they retrieve its k-nearest neighbors from the datastore to form a token-level predictive distribution, then interpolate this distribution with the proxy LLM’s output to produce an aligned prediction. To handle domain shift, they extend this to a Mixture of Proxies (MoP) where multiple domain-specific datastores exist and an input is routed to the most appropriate one, enabling domain-consistent retrieval and prediction without modifying the proxy model.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple benchmarks show that kNNProxy achieves strong LLM-generated text detection performance, outperforming or rivaling existing detectors while remaining training-free and query-efficient. The MoP extension further improves robustness under domain shift, maintaining high accuracy across varied domains. The method avoids the instability and cost associated with supervised fine-tuning or intensive API interactions.
AI.conclusion: kNNProxy provides an effective, training-free way to align a fixed proxy LLM to unknown source LLMs for LGT detection by using kNN-based retrieval from a target-reflective datastore and interpolating it with proxy predictions. The mixture-of-proxies extension further boosts robustness across domains. Overall, the approach offers a practical, robust, and cost-efficient alternative to existing supervised or naive zero-shot detectors for detecting LLM-generated text in realistic black-box scenarios.

title: The AnIML Ontology: Enabling Semantic Interoperability for Large-Scale Experimental Data in Interconnected Scientific Labs
comment: Accepted at the 38th International Conference on Advanced Information Systems Engineering (CAiSE 2026)
summary: Achieving semantic interoperability across heterogeneous experimental data systems remains a major barrier to data-driven scientific discovery. The Analytical Information Markup Language (AnIML), a flexible XML-based standard for analytical chemistry and biology, is increasingly used in industrial R&D labs for managing and exchanging experimental data. However, the expressivity of the XML schema permits divergent interpretations across stakeholders, introducing inconsistencies that undermine the interoperability the AnIML schema was designed to support. In this paper, we present the AnIML Ontology, an OWL 2 ontology that formalises the semantics of AnIML and aligns it with the Allotrope Data Format to support future cross-system and cross-lab interoperability. The ontology was developed using an expert-in-the-loop approach combining LLM-assisted requirement elicitation with collaborative ontology engineering. We validate the ontology through a multi-layered approach: data-driven transformation of real-world AnIML files into knowledge graphs, competency question verification via SPARQL, and a novel validation protocol based on adversarial negative competency questions mapped to established ontological anti-patterns and enforced via SHACL constraints.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the AnIML Ontology to provide precise, shared semantics for the AnIML XML standard and align it with the Allotrope Data Format, improving cross-system and cross-lab interoperability for experimental data.
AI.motivation: Although AnIML is widely used as an XML standard for analytical chemistry and biology data, its flexible schema allows divergent interpretations among stakeholders, causing semantic inconsistencies that hinder interoperability and data-driven scientific discovery. There is a need to formalize AnIML’s semantics and align it with other standards like the Allotrope Data Format to enable robust, machine-interpretable integration of heterogeneous experimental data systems.
AI.method: The authors develop the AnIML Ontology as an OWL 2 ontology that formalizes the semantics of AnIML and aligns it with the Allotrope Data Format. They use an expert-in-the-loop methodology that combines large language model (LLM)-assisted requirement elicitation with collaborative ontology engineering. For validation, they transform real-world AnIML files into knowledge graphs, verify the ontology using competency questions expressed and checked via SPARQL, and introduce a novel validation protocol using adversarial negative competency questions tied to known ontological anti-patterns and enforced with SHACL constraints.
AI.result: The outcome is a formally specified OWL 2 ontology for AnIML, semantically aligned with the Allotrope Data Format, that can be instantiated from real-world AnIML data as knowledge graphs. The ontology successfully answers defined competency questions via SPARQL and withstands adversarial negative competency questions checked through SHACL, demonstrating its robustness against common ontological anti-patterns and its suitability for semantic interoperability tasks.
AI.conclusion: The AnIML Ontology addresses key semantic interoperability gaps in existing XML-based AnIML data exchange by providing a rigorous, machine-interpretable semantic layer aligned with the Allotrope Data Format. The proposed expert-in-the-loop, LLM-assisted development and multi-layered validation approach yields an ontology that can reliably support cross-system and cross-lab integration of experimental data, and the validation protocol offers a reusable pattern for testing other ontologies against adversarial inconsistencies.

title: Why Gaussian Diffusion Models Fail on Discrete Data?
summary: Diffusion models have become a standard approach for generative modeling in continuous domains, yet their application to discrete data remains challenging. We investigate why Gaussian diffusion models with the DDPM solver struggle to sample from discrete distributions that are represented as a mixture of delta-distributions in the continuous space. Using a toy Random Hierarchy Model, we identify a critical sampling interval in which the density of noisified data becomes multimodal. In this regime, DDPM occasionally enters low-density regions between modes producing out-of-distribution inputs for the model and degrading sample quality. We show that existing heuristics, including self-conditioning and a solver we term q-sampling, help alleviate this issue. Furthermore, we demonstrate that combining self-conditioning with switching from DDPM to q-sampling within the critical interval improves generation quality on real data.
