We validate these findings across conditional and unconditional tasks in multiple domains, including text, programming code, and proteins.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why standard Gaussian diffusion models struggle with discrete data and proposes using self-conditioning plus a modified sampling scheme (q-sampling) in a critical interval to improve generation quality on text, code, and proteins.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models work well for continuous data like images but perform poorly on inherently discrete data (e.g., text, code, protein sequences) when naively embedded into continuous spaces. The authors want to understand the failure modes of Gaussian diffusion with DDPM solvers on such discrete distributions and find principled ways to improve sampling quality rather than relying on ad-hoc tricks.
AI.method: They analyze diffusion on discrete distributions modeled as mixtures of Dirac delta functions embedded in continuous space. Using a controlled toy setting called the Random Hierarchy Model, they study the evolution of the noisy data distribution over time and identify a time interval where the distribution becomes multimodal and low-density regions emerge between modes. They then examine how the DDPM sampling trajectory behaves in this regime and evaluate two modifications: self-conditioning and a new sampling scheme (q-sampling). Finally, they test combinations of these techniques on real discrete-data generative tasks across multiple domains.
AI.result: They find a critical sampling interval where the noisy data distribution is multimodal; within this interval, DDPM trajectories can pass through low-density regions between modes, causing out-of-distribution inputs to the denoiser and degraded sample quality. Both self-conditioning and q-sampling reduce these issues, and the combination of self-conditioning with a schedule that switches from DDPM to q-sampling within the critical interval yields further improvements. Experiments across conditional and unconditional generation tasks in text, code, and protein domains show improved sample quality compared to standard DDPM-based diffusion baselines.
AI.conclusion: The main difficulty of applying Gaussian diffusion with DDPM solvers to discrete data arises in a specific time interval where the noisy data distribution becomes multimodal. Standard DDPM sampling often traverses low-density gaps between modes, harming generation quality. Carefully designed sampling strategies—particularly combining self-conditioning with a targeted switch from DDPM to q-sampling during this critical interval—can substantially mitigate this problem and improve discrete-data diffusion models across several domains.

title: Solving the Two-dimensional single stock size Cuting Stock Problem with SAT and MaxSAT
summary: Cutting rectangular items from stock sheets to satisfy demands while minimizing waste is a central manufacturing task. The Two-Dimensional Single Stock Size Cutting Stock Problem (2D-CSSP) generalizes bin packing by requiring multiple copies of each item type, which causes a strong combinatorial blow-up. We present a SAT-based framework where item types are expanded by demand, each copy has a sheet-assignment variable and non-overlap constraints are activated only for copies assigned to the same sheet. We also introduce an infeasible-orientation elimination rule that fixes rotation variables when only one orientation can fit the sheet. For minimizing the number of sheets, we compare three approaches: non-incremental SAT with binary search, incremental SAT with clause reuse across iterations and weighted partial MaxSAT. On the Cui--Zhao benchmark suite, our best SAT configurations certify two to three times more instances as provably optimal and achieve lower optimality gaps than OR-Tools, CPLEX and Gurobi. The relative ranking among SAT approaches depends on rotation: incremental SAT is strongest without rotation, while non-incremental SAT is more effective when rotation increases formula size.

AI.tldr: SAT-based methods for the 2D cutting stock problem substantially outperform leading OR solvers on a standard benchmark, especially in proving optimality.
AI.motivation: The 2D Single Stock Size Cutting Stock Problem is fundamental in manufacturing but is computationally very challenging due to the need to place many copies of each item type on stock sheets while minimizing waste. Classical approaches (MIP, CP, heuristic OR tools) struggle to prove optimality and scale on hard benchmarks. The authors are motivated to explore whether modern SAT and MaxSAT technologies can model this problem effectively and outperform leading OR solvers in terms of optimality proofs and solution quality.
AI.method: They formulate the 2D-CSSP as a SAT/MaxSAT problem by expanding each item type into separate copies according to demand. Each copy is associated with a Boolean variable indicating which sheet it is assigned to. Non-overlap constraints are encoded so that they only apply to pairs of item copies that are assigned to the same sheet, reducing unnecessary constraints. They also add an infeasible-orientation elimination rule: if an item cannot fit in one orientation on the sheet, the corresponding rotation variable is fixed to forbid that orientation, shrinking the search space. For minimizing the number of used sheets, they experiment with three optimization strategies over this SAT encoding: (1) non-incremental SAT with a binary search over the number of sheets; (2) incremental SAT that reuses clauses when tightening bounds across iterations; and (3) a weighted partial MaxSAT formulation that directly optimizes sheet usage.
AI.result: On the Cui–Zhao benchmark suite, the proposed SAT-based frameworks solve substantially more instances to proven optimality than leading OR solvers (OR-Tools, CPLEX, Gurobi), roughly by a factor of two to three in terms of optimality certificates, and they also achieve smaller optimality gaps on the remaining instances. Within their SAT approaches, performance depends on whether item rotation is allowed: incremental SAT is best when rotation is disabled (smaller formulas), whereas non-incremental SAT becomes better when rotation is enabled and the encoding size grows.
AI.conclusion: SAT and MaxSAT encodings of the 2D Single Stock Size Cutting Stock Problem are highly competitive and often superior to state-of-the-art OR solvers, especially for proving optimality on challenging instances. Careful encoding choices—such as demand-based expansion, conditional non-overlap constraints, and pruning infeasible orientations—are crucial for efficiency. The relative advantage of incremental versus non-incremental SAT depends on the problem encoding size, particularly the presence of rotation-related variables and constraints.

title: Tracking the emergence of linguistic structure in self-supervised models learning from speech
summary: Self-supervised speech models learn effective representations of spoken language, which have been shown to reflect various aspects of linguistic structure. But when does such structure emerge in model training? We study the encoding of a wide range of linguistic structures, across layers and intermediate checkpoints of six Wav2Vec2 and HuBERT models trained on spoken Dutch. We find that different levels of linguistic structure show notably distinct layerwise patterns as well as learning trajectories, which can partially be explained by differences in their degree of abstraction from the acoustic signal and the timescale at which information from the input is integrated. Moreover, we find that the level at which pre-training objectives are defined strongly affects both the layerwise organization and the learning trajectories of linguistic structures, with greater parallelism induced by higher-order prediction tasks (i.e. iteratively refined pseudo-labels).

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes when and how different types of linguistic structure emerge inside self-supervised speech models during training, focusing on layer-wise and temporal learning dynamics in Wav2Vec2 and HuBERT for Dutch.
AI.motivation: Self-supervised speech models like Wav2Vec2 and HuBERT are known to encode rich linguistic information, but it is unclear at what stages of training and in which layers specific linguistic structures (e.g., phonetic, lexical, syntactic) emerge. Understanding these dynamics can guide model design, interpretability, and downstream use, especially for non-English languages such as Dutch.
AI.method: The authors train six self-supervised speech models (Wav2Vec2 and HuBERT variants) on spoken Dutch and save multiple intermediate checkpoints. They probe model representations across layers and training steps for a wide range of linguistic structures, then analyze layerwise patterns and learning trajectories. They compare structures that differ in abstraction from the acoustic signal and in temporal integration needs, and they contrast models with different pre-training objectives—particularly those based on higher-order prediction with iteratively refined pseudo-labels.
AI.result: Different linguistic levels (e.g., lower-level acoustic/phonetic vs higher-level lexical/syntactic) emerge at different layers and at different times during training, showing distinct learning curves. These patterns correlate with how abstract the structure is from raw acoustics and with the timescale of information integration. Models whose pre-training objectives rely on higher-order prediction and refined pseudo-labels show more parallel, aligned layerwise organization and more synchronized learning trajectories across structures.
AI.conclusion: Linguistic structure in self-supervised speech models does not emerge uniformly: levels of structure follow distinct layerwise and temporal dynamics, shaped by their abstraction from the signal and temporal requirements. Furthermore, the design of the pre-training objective—especially using higher-order prediction and refined pseudo-labels—strongly influences how and when these structures appear, promoting more parallel and organized internal representations.

title: AeroTherm-GPT: A Verification-Centered LLM Framework for Thermal Protection System Engineering Workflows
summary: Integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) into hypersonic thermal protection system (TPS) design is bottlenecked by cascading constraint violations when generating executable simulation artifacts. General-purpose LLMs, treating generation as single-pass text completion, fail to satisfy the sequential, multi-gate constraints inherent in safety-critical engineering workflows. To address this, we propose AeroTherm-GPT, the first TPS-specialized LLM Agent, instantiated through a Constraint-Closed-Loop Generation (CCLG) framework. CCLG organizes TPS artifact generation as an iterative workflow comprising generation, validation, CDG-guided repair, execution, and audit. The Constraint Dependency Graph (CDG) encodes empirical co-resolution structure among constraint categories, directing repair toward upstream fault candidates based on lifecycle ordering priors and empirical co-resolution probabilities. This upstream-priority mechanism resolves multiple downstream violations per action, achieving a Root-Cause Fix Efficiency of 4.16 versus 1.76 for flat-checklist repair. Evaluated on HyTPS-Bench and validated against external benchmarks, AeroTherm-GPT achieves 88.7% End-to-End Success Rate (95% CI: 87.5-89.9), a gain of +12.5 pp over the matched non-CDG ablation baseline, without catastrophic forgetting on scientific reasoning and code generation tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AeroTherm-GPT, a TPS-specialized LLM agent using a Constraint-Closed-Loop Generation (CCLG) framework to reliably generate and repair simulation artifacts under complex engineering constraints, significantly improving end-to-end success on a hypersonic TPS benchmark.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLMs struggle in hypersonic thermal protection system design because they treat generation as one-shot text completion and frequently violate the many sequential, interdependent constraints in safety-critical simulation workflows. This leads to cascading errors and invalid artifacts, blocking automation and practical deployment. The authors aim to create an LLM-based system that can robustly satisfy these multi-stage constraints and integrate into TPS design pipelines without sacrificing general reasoning ability.
AI.method: They propose AeroTherm-GPT, a domain-specialized LLM agent built using a Constraint-Closed-Loop Generation (CCLG) framework. Instead of single-pass generation, CCLG structures TPS artifact creation as an iterative loop: the model generates artifacts, validators check constraints, a Constraint Dependency Graph (CDG) identifies likely upstream root-cause constraints, repair is guided toward these upstream issues, artifacts are re-executed, and the process is audited. The CDG captures empirical co-resolution patterns among constraint categories and lifecycle ordering priors, allowing the system to focus on fixes that are likely to resolve multiple downstream violations simultaneously. They compare against a flat-checklist repair strategy and conduct evaluations on the HyTPS-Bench benchmark and external tasks.
AI.result: AeroTherm-GPT achieves an 88.7% end-to-end success rate on HyTPS-Bench, with a 95% confidence interval of 87.5–89.9%. Compared to a non-CDG ablation (flat checklist repair), it improves success rate by 12.5 percentage points. The CDG-based upstream-priority repair yields a Root-Cause Fix Efficiency of 4.16, substantially higher than 1.76 for the flat-checklist approach, meaning each repair action tends to resolve more violations. Additionally, the model maintains its performance on general scientific reasoning and code generation benchmarks, showing no catastrophic forgetting from domain specialization.
AI.conclusion: By organizing simulation artifact generation as a constraint-aware, iterative closed loop and encoding constraint dependencies via a CDG, AeroTherm-GPT can reliably satisfy complex, cascading constraints in hypersonic TPS design workflows. The system significantly outperforms non-CDG baselines in both end-to-end success and repair efficiency while preserving general LLM capabilities, suggesting that constraint-structured agent architectures are a promising way to deploy LLMs in safety-critical engineering domains.

title: BidirLM: From Text to Omnimodal Bidirectional Encoders by Adapting and Composing Causal LLMs
summary: Transforming causal generative language models into bidirectional encoders offers a powerful alternative to BERT-style architectures. However, current approaches remain limited: they lack consensus on optimal training objectives, suffer from catastrophic forgetting at scale, and fail to flexibly integrate the vast ecosystem of specialized generative models. In this work, through systematic ablations on the Gemma3 and Qwen3 families, we identify the key factors driving successful adaptation, highlighting the critical role of an often-omitted prior masking phase. To scale this process without original pre-training data, we introduce a dual strategy combining linear weight merging with a lightweight multi-domain data mixture that mitigates catastrophic forgetting. Finally, we augment our encoders by merging them with specialized causal models, seamlessly transferring modality- and domain-specific capabilities. This open-source recipe, designed for any causal decoder LLM, yields BidirLM, a family of five encoders that outperform alternatives on text, vision, and audio representation benchmarks.

AI.tldr: They propose a general recipe to turn any causal LLM into a strong bidirectional encoder, fixing forgetting and integration issues, and show the resulting BidirLM encoders beat existing methods on text, vision, and audio benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing methods that adapt causal generative language models into bidirectional encoders are unsatisfying: there is no agreement on the best training objective, models forget their original capabilities when scaled, and it is hard to plug in the growing zoo of specialized generative models (e.g., domain- or modality-specific) into a unified encoder. The authors aim to develop a scalable, data-efficient, and modular way to repurpose powerful decoder-only LLMs into robust, general-purpose bidirectional encoders that can leverage specialized models and work well across modalities.
AI.method: 1) Perform systematic ablation studies on Gemma3 and Qwen3 families to understand what matters in adapting causal LLMs into encoders, identifying an important but often-missing prior masking phase. 2) Propose a scalable adaptation pipeline that does not require original pretraining data, using: (a) linear weight merging to combine different model states; and (b) a lightweight multi-domain data mixture to fine-tune and reduce catastrophic forgetting. 3) Further enhance the resulting encoders by merging them with specialized causal models so that modality- and domain-specific skills (e.g., for vision or audio) are transferred into the encoder. 4) Implement this as an open-source recipe that can, in principle, be applied to any causal decoder LLM, and instantiate a family of five such encoders called BidirLM.
AI.result: The proposed BidirLM encoders, derived from causal decoder LLMs using the described adaptation and merging recipe, outperform existing adapted encoders and other alternatives on a variety of representation-learning benchmarks. These benchmarks span multiple modalities—text, vision, and audio—showing that the method yields strong general-purpose encoders with good multimodal capabilities and mitigated catastrophic forgetting compared to prior work.
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed adaptation strategy can reliably convert strong causal decoder LLMs into competitive bidirectional encoders without needing original pretraining data and while flexibly incorporating specialized generative models. The key components—an initial masking phase, linear weight merging, and multi-domain fine-tuning—jointly prevent catastrophic forgetting and enable multi-modality. The resulting BidirLM family demonstrates that this recipe is practical, generalizable to many base LLMs, and yields state-of-the-art representation encoders across text, vision, and audio tasks.

title: Domain-constrained knowledge representation: A modal framework
summary: Knowledge graphs store large numbers of relations efficiently, but they remain weak at representing a quieter difficulty: the meaning of a concept often shifts with the domain in which it is used. A triple such as Apple, instance-of, Company may be acceptable in one setting while being misleading or unusable in another. In most current systems, domain information is attached as metadata, qualifiers, or graph-level organization. These mechanisms help with filtering and provenance, but they usually do not alter the formal status of the assertion itself. This paper argues that domain should be treated as part of knowledge representation rather than as supplementary annotation. It introduces the Domain-Contextualized Concept Graph (DCG), a framework in which domain is written into the relation and interpreted as a modal world constraint. In the DCG form (C, R at D, C'), the marker at D identifies the world in which the relation holds. Formally, the relation is interpreted through a domain-indexed necessity operator, so that truth, inference, and conflict checking are all scoped to the relevant world. This move has three consequences: ambiguous concepts can be disambiguated at the point of representation; invalid assertions can be challenged against their domain; cross-domain relations can be connected through explicit predicates. The paper develops this claim through a Kripke-style semantics, a compact predicate system, a Prolog implementation, and mappings to RDF, OWL, and relational databases. The contribution is a representational reinterpretation of domain itself. The central claim is that many practical failures in knowledge systems begin when domain is treated as external to the assertion. DCG addresses that by giving domain a structural and computable role inside the representation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Domain-Contextualized Concept Graphs (DCGs), where domain is built into relations as a modal constraint, enabling domain-scoped truth, inference, and conflict checking in knowledge graphs.
AI.motivation: Existing knowledge graphs handle domain as external metadata or qualifiers, which does not change the formal status of assertions. This leads to issues when the meaning of concepts shifts across domains (e.g., "Apple" as a company vs. other interpretations), causing ambiguity, invalid assertions, and poor cross-domain reasoning. The authors want a representation where domain directly shapes truth conditions and inference.
AI.method: They introduce the DCG representation (C, R at D, C'), where the domain marker "at D" specifies the world in which the relation holds. They formalize this using a domain-indexed necessity operator in a Kripke-style modal semantics, define a compact predicate system, implement the framework in Prolog, and show how DCGs map to existing standards like RDF, OWL, and relational databases.
AI.result: DCGs allow assertions to be evaluated, inferred, and checked for conflicts only within the specified domain-world, which enables (1) disambiguation of concepts at representation time, (2) the ability to challenge or invalidate assertions by reference to their domain, and (3) explicit modeling of cross-domain links through dedicated predicates. They demonstrate that DCGs can be implemented and integrated with existing KR technologies.
AI.conclusion: Treating domain as an intrinsic, modal component of knowledge representation—rather than as external annotation—can reduce practical failures in knowledge systems. By embedding domain into the structure of relations, DCGs provide a computable way to handle domain-specific meaning, truth, and conflicts across heterogeneous knowledge graphs.

title: Goose: Anisotropic Speculation Trees for Training-Free Speculative Decoding
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates large language model inference by drafting multiple candidate tokens and verifying them in a single forward pass. Candidates are organized as a tree: deeper trees accept more tokens per step, but adding depth requires sacrificing breadth (fallback options) under a fixed verification budget. Existing training-free methods draft from a single token source and shape their trees without distinguishing candidate quality across origins. We observe that two common training-free token sources - n-gram matches copied from the input context, and statistical predictions from prior forward passes - differ dramatically in acceptance rate (~6x median gap, range 2-18x across five models and five benchmarks). We prove that when such a quality gap exists, the optimal tree is anisotropic (asymmetric): reliable tokens should form a deep chain while unreliable tokens spread as wide branches, breaking through the depth limit of balanced trees. We realize this structure in GOOSE, a training-free framework that builds an adaptive spine tree - a deep chain of high-acceptance context-matched tokens with wide branches of low-acceptance alternatives at each node. We prove that the number of tokens accepted per step is at least as large as that of either source used alone. On five LLMs (7B-33B) and five benchmarks, GOOSE achieves 1.9-4.3x lossless speedup, outperforming balanced-tree baselines by 12-33% under the same budget.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GOOSE, a training-free speculative decoding framework that exploits differing quality between token sources to build anisotropic draft trees, achieving higher acceptance per step and faster, lossless LLM inference.
AI.motivation: Speculative decoding speeds up LLM inference by proposing multiple tokens and verifying them together, but existing training-free approaches assume a single, homogeneous token source and build balanced trees that ignore quality differences between candidate types. However, common token sources like context n-grams and statistical model predictions have very different acceptance rates, meaning the current tree structures are suboptimal under a fixed verification budget.
AI.method: The authors first empirically show that context-matched n-grams and prior-step model predictions have large and consistent gaps in token acceptance rate across several models and benchmarks. They then provide a theoretical analysis proving that when such quality gaps exist, the optimal speculative decoding tree should be anisotropic: high-quality tokens are placed in a deep chain, while low-quality tokens form wide branches. Based on this, they propose GOOSE, a training-free framework that constructs an adaptive spine tree with a deep backbone of high-acceptance context tokens and wide, low-acceptance alternatives branching from each node, all within a fixed verification budget. They also prove that this structure guarantees at least as many accepted tokens per step as using each source alone.
AI.result: On experiments with five LLMs ranging from 7B to 33B parameters and five benchmarks, GOOSE yields 1.9–4.3× lossless speedup in inference. Under the same verification budget, it surpasses balanced-tree speculative decoding baselines by 12–33% in accepted tokens per step / effective speedup.
AI.conclusion: By recognizing and exploiting the quality disparity between different training-free token sources, an anisotropic tree structure can break the depth–breadth trade-off of conventional balanced speculative decoding trees. GOOSE’s adaptive spine tree design is theoretically justified and empirically validated, delivering strictly better or equal acceptance per step than either source alone and significantly higher lossless speedups across multiple LLMs and benchmarks.

title: Not All Tokens See Equally: Perception-Grounded Policy Optimization for Large Vision-Language Models
summary: While Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has advanced reasoning in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), prevailing frameworks suffer from a foundational methodological flaw: by distributing identical advantages across all generated tokens, these methods inherently dilute the learning signals essential for optimizing the critical, visually-grounded steps of multimodal reasoning. To bridge this gap, we formulate \textit{Token Visual Dependency}, quantifying the causal information gain of visual inputs via the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between visual-conditioned and text-only predictive distributions. Revealing that this dependency is highly sparse and semantically pivotal, we introduce Perception-Grounded Policy Optimization (PGPO), which is a novel fine-grained credit assignment framework that dynamically reshapes advantages at the token level. Through a threshold-gated, mass-conserving mechanism, PGPO actively amplifies learning signals for visually-dependent tokens while suppressing gradient noise from linguistic priors. Extensive experiments based on the Qwen2.5-VL series across seven challenging multimodal reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that PGPO boosts models by 18.7% on average. Both theoretical and empirical analyses confirm that PGPO effectively reduces gradient variance, prevents training collapse, and acts as a potent regularizer for robust, perception-grounded multimodal reasoning. Code will be published on https://github.com/Yzk1114/PGPO.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a flaw in existing RL from verifiable rewards for LVLMs—uniformly distributing reward across tokens—and proposes a new token-level, vision-aware credit assignment method (PGPO) based on token visual dependency, significantly improving multimodal reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: Existing RLVR methods for large vision-language models assign identical advantages to all tokens in a generated sequence. This uniform credit assignment dilutes the learning signal for tokens that truly depend on visual input, especially in complex multimodal reasoning where only a few steps are visually grounded. As a result, models overfit to linguistic priors, suffer higher gradient variance, and may collapse during training. The authors are motivated to better distinguish and emphasize visually-grounded tokens so the model can perform more robust, perception-grounded reasoning.
AI.method: The authors define Token Visual Dependency as the KL divergence between the model’s predictive distribution with both vision and text inputs and its predictive distribution with text-only inputs, measured at each token. This quantifies how much each token’s prediction depends on visual information. They show this dependency is sparse and semantically important, and then design Perception-Grounded Policy Optimization (PGPO), a reinforcement learning framework that reshapes the per-token advantages based on this dependency. PGPO employs a threshold-gated, mass-conserving mechanism: only tokens whose visual dependency exceeds a threshold receive amplified advantages, while others get reduced or suppressed advantages so as not to increase the total advantage mass. This creates a fine-grained, token-level credit assignment that focuses the learning signal on visually grounded reasoning steps and reduces noise from text-only priors.
AI.result: Using Qwen2.5-VL models on seven challenging multimodal reasoning benchmarks, PGPO improves performance by an average of 18.7% over baselines that use standard RLVR. The authors also provide theoretical analysis showing that their token-level visual dependency-based reshaping reduces gradient variance and mitigates risk of training collapse. Empirically, they observe more stable optimization dynamics and stronger alignment between model reasoning steps and visual evidence, indicating that PGPO indeed concentrates learning on perception-relevant tokens.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that current RLVR frameworks for LVLMs are limited by uniform advantage assignment, which weakens learning on visually grounded reasoning steps. By introducing Token Visual Dependency and the PGPO framework, the authors provide a principled way to perform perception-aware, token-level credit assignment. This approach both stabilizes reinforcement learning (through lower gradient variance and regularization effects) and substantially enhances multimodal reasoning performance, demonstrating that fine-grained, vision-aware optimization is crucial for robust LVLM reasoning.

title: Optimizing RAG Rerankers with LLM Feedback via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Rerankers play a pivotal role in refining retrieval results for Retrieval-Augmented Generation. However, current reranking models are typically optimized on static human annotated relevance labels in isolation, decoupled from the downstream generation process. This isolation leads to a fundamental misalignment: documents identified as topically relevant by information retrieval metrics often fail to provide the actual utility required by the LLM for precise answer generation. To bridge this gap, we introduce ReRanking Preference Optimization (RRPO), a reinforcement learning framework that directly aligns reranking with the LLM's generation quality. By formulating reranking as a sequential decision-making process, RRPO optimizes for context utility using LLM feedback, thereby eliminating the need for expensive human annotations. To ensure training stability, we further introduce a reference-anchored deterministic baseline. Extensive experiments on knowledge-intensive benchmarks demonstrate that RRPO significantly outperforms strong baselines, including the powerful list-wise reranker RankZephyr. Further analysis highlights the versatility of our framework: it generalizes seamlessly to diverse readers (e.g., GPT-4o), integrates orthogonally with query expansion modules like Query2Doc, and remains robust even when trained with noisy supervisors.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RRPO, a reinforcement-learning-based reranking framework that directly optimizes retrieval for LLM answer quality instead of traditional relevance labels, achieving state-of-the-art performance and robust generalization across models and settings.
AI.motivation: Existing rerankers for retrieval-augmented generation are trained on static, human-annotated relevance labels and optimized with IR metrics, which are misaligned with what an LLM actually needs to generate accurate answers. Documents that look topically relevant may not be the most useful for generation, and human annotations are expensive to obtain at scale. The authors are motivated to design a reranking method that is directly aligned with downstream generation utility and can leverage LLM feedback instead of human labels.
AI.method: They propose ReRanking Preference Optimization (RRPO), a reinforcement learning framework that treats reranking as a sequential decision-making process. The reranker selects documents to build the context for the LLM, and the LLM’s generation quality provides feedback (a utility signal). RRPO uses this feedback to optimize the reranker for context utility rather than traditional relevance. To stabilize training, they introduce a reference-anchored deterministic baseline, which serves as a stable reference policy/value to reduce variance during optimization. The approach removes the need for human relevance labels and instead relies on LLM-based preferences or signals.
AI.result: On multiple knowledge-intensive benchmarks, RRPO substantially outperforms strong reranking baselines, including the advanced list-wise reranker RankZephyr. Experiments show that RRPO-trained rerankers lead to better downstream answer quality. Additional experiments demonstrate that the approach can be used with different reader LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o), works well in combination with query expansion methods such as Query2Doc, and maintains performance even when the supervisory signals (LLM feedback) are noisy.
AI.conclusion: Aligning rerankers directly with LLM generation quality via reinforcement learning yields better retrieval-augmented generation performance than training on static relevance labels. RRPO provides a general, label-free framework that is stable to train, improves over strong baselines, generalizes across different LLM readers and setups, and remains robust under noisy supervision, making it a practical approach for optimizing reranking in RAG systems.

title: Retrieval-aligned Tabular Foundation Models Enable Robust Clinical Risk Prediction in Electronic Health Records Under Real-world Constraints
summary: Clinical prediction from structured electronic health records (EHRs) is challenging due to high dimensionality, heterogeneity, class imbalance, and distribution shift. While tabular in-context learning (TICL) and retrieval-augmented methods perform well on generic benchmarks, their behavior in clinical settings remains unclear. We present a multi-cohort EHR benchmark comparing classical, deep tabular, and TICL models across varying data scale, feature dimensionality, outcome rarity, and cross-cohort generalization. PFN-based TICL models are sample-efficient in low-data regimes but degrade under naive distance-based retrieval as heterogeneity and imbalance increase. We propose AWARE, a task-aligned retrieval framework using supervised embedding learning and lightweight adapters. AWARE improves AUPRC by up to 12.2% under extreme imbalance, with gains increasing with data complexity. Our results identify retrieval quality and retrieval-inference alignment as key bottlenecks for deploying tabular in-context learning in clinical prediction.

AI.tldr: Benchmarking clinical prediction models on structured EHRs and proposing AWARE, a task-aligned retrieval framework that improves tabular in-context learning under challenging conditions.
AI.motivation: To understand how classical, deep tabular, and tabular in-context learning models behave on real-world clinical EHR prediction tasks characterized by high dimensionality, heterogeneity, class imbalance, and distribution shift, and to overcome the limitations of existing retrieval strategies in such settings.
AI.method: The authors build a multi-cohort EHR benchmark and systematically compare classical methods, deep tabular models, and PFN-based tabular in-context learning across varying data scales, feature dimensionalities, outcome rarities, and cross-cohort generalization scenarios. They analyze the failures of naive distance-based retrieval in heterogeneous and imbalanced clinical data and then introduce AWARE, a task-aligned retrieval framework that uses supervised embedding learning and lightweight adapters to improve retrieval quality and its alignment with the prediction task.
AI.result: PFN-based tabular in-context learning models show strong sample efficiency in low-data regimes but performance deteriorates when naive distance-based retrieval is applied in settings with high heterogeneity and class imbalance. The proposed AWARE framework enhances retrieval quality and alignment, yielding up to a 12.2% AUPRC improvement in highly imbalanced scenarios, with larger performance gains as data complexity grows.
AI.conclusion: Tabular in-context learning has promise for clinical prediction from structured EHRs, especially in low-data settings, but its effectiveness is constrained by retrieval quality and retrieval–inference alignment. AWARE alleviates these bottlenecks by learning task-aligned embeddings and adapters, leading to substantial performance gains under realistic clinical conditions, particularly extreme class imbalance and complex data. The study highlights that careful design of retrieval mechanisms is critical for safely and effectively deploying TICL in healthcare applications.

title: Prosodic ABX: A Language-Agnostic Method for Measuring Prosodic Contrast in Speech Representations
summary: Speech representations from self-supervised speech models (S3Ms) are known to be sensitive to phonemic contrasts, but their sensitivity to prosodic contrasts has not been directly measured. The ABX discrimination task has been used to measure phonemic contrast in S3M representations via minimal pairs. We introduce prosodic ABX, an extension of this framework to evaluate prosodic contrast with only a handful of examples and no explicit labels. Also, we build and release a dataset of English and Japanese minimal pairs and use it along with a Mandarin dataset to evaluate contrast in English stress, Japanese pitch accent, and Mandarin tone. Finally, we show that model and layer rankings are often preserved across several experimental conditions, making it practical for low-resource settings.

AI.tldr: They propose and validate a new way (“prosodic ABX”) to measure how well self-supervised speech models capture prosodic contrasts like stress, pitch accent, and tone, and release cross-linguistic minimal-pair datasets for this purpose.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of self-supervised speech models focus mostly on segmental/phonemic contrasts using ABX tasks. It is unclear how well these models encode suprasegmental prosodic features (stress, pitch accent, tone), partly because suitable evaluation methods and datasets are lacking, especially ones that do not require detailed labels or large resources.
AI.method: 1) Extend the ABX discrimination framework from phonemic to prosodic minimal pairs by defining a “prosodic ABX” task that can work with few examples and no explicit prosody labels; 2) Construct and release new minimal-pair datasets for English stress and Japanese pitch accent, and use an existing Mandarin tone dataset; 3) Extract representations from different self-supervised speech models and layers; 4) Compute ABX discrimination scores for the various prosodic contrasts across models/layers and compare rankings under multiple experimental conditions (e.g., languages, contrasts, data regimes).
AI.result: The prosodic ABX task yields meaningful discrimination scores that reveal how different S3Ms and layers encode prosodic contrasts. The datasets for English stress and Japanese pitch accent, combined with a Mandarin tone dataset, enable cross-linguistic comparison. Across several experimental conditions (e.g., varying data amounts or setups), the relative ranking of models and layers remains largely consistent.
AI.conclusion: Prosodic contrasts in self-supervised speech representations can be systematically evaluated with a label-efficient ABX-based method. The newly proposed prosodic ABX task and released multilingual minimal-pair datasets provide a practical tool for assessing prosodic encoding, and the stability of model/layer rankings suggests the method is suitable for low-resource scenarios.

title: Efficient Constraint Generation for Stochastic Shortest Path Problems
summary: Stochastic Shortest Path problems (SSPs) are traditionally solved by computing each state's cost-to-go by applying Bellman backups. A Bellman backup updates a state's cost-to-go by iterating through every applicable action, computing the cost-to-go after applying each one, and selecting a minimal action's cost-to-go. State-of-the-art algorithms use heuristic functions; these give an initial estimate of costs-to-go, and lets the algorithm apply Bellman backups only to promising states, determined by low estimated costs-to-go. However, each Bellman backup still considers all applicable actions, even if the heuristic tells us that some of these actions are too expensive, with the effect that such algorithms waste time on unhelpful actions. To address this gap we present a technique that uses the heuristic to avoid expensive actions, by reframing heuristic search in terms of linear programming and introducing an efficient implementation of constraint generation for SSPs. We present CG-iLAO*, a new algorithm that adapts iLAO* with our novel technique, and considers only 40% of iLAO*'s actions on many problems, and as few as 1% on some. Consequently, CG-iLAO* computes on average 3.5x fewer costs-to-go for actions than the state-of-the-art iLAO* and LRTDP, enabling it to solve problems faster an average of 2.8x and 3.7x faster, respectively.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CG-iLAO*, a heuristic search algorithm for Stochastic Shortest Path problems that uses a linear-programming-based constraint generation technique to prune expensive actions, substantially reducing Bellman backup effort and runtime versus iLAO* and LRTDP.
AI.motivation: Traditional SSP solvers rely on Bellman backups that evaluate all applicable actions in a state. Even heuristic search variants like iLAO* and LRTDP, which focus on promising states using heuristic cost-to-go estimates, still waste computation on actions that are clearly suboptimal according to the heuristic. This creates a gap: heuristics guide state selection but not action selection, leading to unnecessary computation and slower solution times. The authors are motivated to exploit heuristic information more fully by also pruning actions that are likely too costly, thereby accelerating solution of SSPs.
AI.method: The authors reframe heuristic search for SSPs as a linear programming (LP) problem and introduce an efficient constraint generation scheme tailored to SSPs. They use the heuristic to decide which action-induced constraints to add to the LP, avoiding constraints corresponding to obviously expensive actions. Building on this idea, they design CG-iLAO*, an adaptation of the iLAO* algorithm that integrates LP-based constraint generation with heuristic action pruning so that, during Bellman-like updates, only a subset of promising actions is considered.
AI.result: Empirical results show that CG-iLAO* examines significantly fewer actions than iLAO*: about 40% of the actions on many benchmark problems and as little as 1% on some instances. This reduction in action consideration leads to CG-iLAO* computing, on average, 3.5 times fewer action cost-to-go values compared to iLAO* and LRTDP. These computational savings translate into substantial time improvements: CG-iLAO* solves problems on average 2.8 times faster than iLAO* and 3.7 times faster than LRTDP.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that integrating heuristic information into action selection, via an LP-based constraint generation framework, effectively reduces unnecessary computation in SSP heuristic search. CG-iLAO* demonstrates that it is possible to maintain solution quality while dramatically cutting the number of evaluated actions and overall runtime, outperforming leading algorithms like iLAO* and LRTDP. This approach highlights a promising direction for more efficient SSP solvers by combining heuristics with optimization-based pruning.

title: Reliable Control-Point Selection for Steering Reasoning in Large Language Models
summary: Steering vectors offer a training-free mechanism for controlling reasoning behaviors in large language models, but constructing effective vectors requires identifying genuine behavioral signals in the model's hidden states. For behaviors that can be toggled via prompts, this is straightforward. However, many reasoning behaviors -- such as self-reflection -- emerge spontaneously and resist prompt-level control. Current methods detect these behaviors through keyword matching in chain-of-thought traces, implicitly assuming that every detected boundary encodes a genuine behavioral signal. We show that this assumption is overwhelmingly wrong: across 541 keyword-detected boundaries, 93.3\% are behaviorally unstable, failing to reproduce the detected behavior under re-generation from the same prefix. We develop a probabilistic model that formalizes intrinsic reasoning behaviors as stochastic events with context-dependent trigger probabilities, and show that unstable boundaries dilute the steering signal. Guided by this analysis, we propose stability filtering, which retains only boundaries where the model consistently reproduces the target behavior. Combined with a content-subspace projection that removes residual question-specific noise, our method achieves 0.784 accuracy on MATH-500 (+5.0 over the strongest baseline). The resulting steering vectors transfer across models in the same architecture family without re-extraction, improving Nemotron-Research-Reasoning-1.5B (+5.0) and DeepScaleR-1.5B-Preview (+6.0). Code is available at https://github.com/zhmzm/stability-steering.

AI.tldr: The paper improves training-free control of reasoning behaviors in LLMs by filtering for stable behavioral signals in hidden states and projecting out question-specific noise, resulting in more effective steering vectors that transfer across models.
AI.motivation: Steering vectors can control reasoning behaviors in LLMs without retraining, but for spontaneous behaviors (like self-reflection) current methods misidentify behavioral signals by relying on keyword-detected boundaries in chain-of-thought. Most such boundaries are behaviorally unstable and do not consistently represent the target behavior, which weakens steering performance. A more principled way is needed to isolate genuine, stable behavioral signals in hidden activations.
AI.method: The authors empirically show that keyword-based boundary detection mostly yields behaviorally unstable points (93.3% fail to reproduce the same behavior on re-generation). They introduce a probabilistic model treating reasoning behaviors as stochastic, context-dependent events, and use it to argue that unstable boundaries dilute the steering signal. Based on this, they propose stability filtering: from all keyword-detected boundaries, they only retain those where the model consistently reproduces the target behavior when re-generating from the same prefix. They further apply a content-subspace projection to remove residual question-specific information from the hidden states before computing steering vectors. They then use the resulting vectors as steering directions and evaluate performance on reasoning tasks and cross-model transfer.
AI.result: On the MATH-500 benchmark, their stability-filtered and content-projected steering vectors achieve 0.784 accuracy, which is a +5.0 point improvement over the strongest baseline method. The steering vectors also transfer without re-extraction to other models in the same architecture family, yielding +5.0 accuracy on Nemotron-Research-Reasoning-1.5B and +6.0 on DeepScaleR-1.5B-Preview.
AI.conclusion: Unfiltered keyword-based methods largely misidentify genuine reasoning behaviors in LLM hidden states, leading to weak steering vectors. Modeling behaviors as stochastic events and filtering for boundaries that are behaviorally stable, combined with projecting out question-specific content, produces much stronger and more transferable steering directions. This yields sizable accuracy gains on math reasoning benchmarks and demonstrates that careful identification of stable behavioral signals is crucial for effective, training-free control of LLM reasoning.

title: Bayesian Elicitation with LLMs: Model Size Helps, Extra "Reasoning" Doesn't Always
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have been proposed as alternatives to human experts for estimating unknown quantities with associated uncertainty, a process known as Bayesian elicitation. We test this by asking eleven LLMs to estimate population statistics, such as health prevalence rates, personality trait distributions, and labor market figures, and to express their uncertainty as 95\% credible intervals. We vary each model's reasoning effort (low, medium, high) to test whether more "thinking" improves results. Our findings reveal three key results. First, larger, more capable models produce more accurate estimates, but increasing reasoning effort provides no consistent benefit. Second, all models are severely overconfident: their 95\% intervals contain the true value only 9--44\% of the time, far below the expected 95\%. Third, a statistical recalibration technique called conformal prediction can correct this overconfidence, expanding the intervals to achieve the intended coverage. In a preliminary experiment, giving models web search access degraded predictions for already-accurate models, while modestly improving predictions for weaker ones. Models performed well on commonly discussed topics but struggled with specialized health data. These results indicate that LLM uncertainty estimates require statistical correction before they can be used in decision-making.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well various large language models can perform Bayesian elicitation—producing numerical estimates of real-world population statistics along with calibrated uncertainty intervals—and finds that while stronger models are more accurate on point estimates, all are highly overconfident, requiring statistical recalibration (e.g., via conformal prediction) before their uncertainties can be trusted in decision-making.
AI.motivation: Bayesian elicitation is often done with human experts who provide both estimates and associated uncertainty, but this process is expensive, slow, and hard to scale. With the rapid progress of large language models, there is interest in using them as cheaper, scalable substitutes for expert judgment, particularly for tasks involving forecasting or estimating unknown quantities with credible intervals. However, it is unclear whether LLMs can provide not just good point estimates but also well-calibrated uncertainty. This paper is motivated by the need to systematically test the accuracy and calibration of LLM-based Bayesian elicitation across a range of real-world population statistics and model configurations.
AI.method: The authors evaluate eleven different LLMs on a benchmark of real-world population statistics, including health prevalence rates, distributions of personality traits, and labor market indicators. For each quantity, models are prompted to provide a point estimate and a 95% credible interval. The experiments vary the level of chain-of-thought or reasoning effort (low, medium, high) to test whether more elaborate reasoning improves accuracy or calibration. Coverage is assessed by checking the proportion of times the true value lies within the stated 95% interval. The authors also apply a statistical recalibration method—conformal prediction—to the models’ outputs to adjust intervals so that empirical coverage approaches the nominal 95%. Additionally, they run a preliminary experiment where some models are given web search capabilities to see whether external information improves or harms performance, and they compare performance on widely discussed versus specialized topics (e.g., niche health statistics).
AI.result: The study finds that larger and more capable LLMs produce more accurate point estimates of the target statistics than smaller, weaker ones. However, increasing the level of reasoning effort (e.g., more extensive chain-of-thought) does not reliably improve either accuracy or calibration. Across all models, the reported 95% credible intervals are substantially overconfident, with true values lying inside the intervals only 9–44% of the time instead of the intended 95%. Conformal prediction-based recalibration successfully widens the intervals, bringing empirical coverage closer to the nominal 95%. In early tests involving web search, access to external information harms the performance of models that are already strong but provides modest gains to weaker models. The models perform relatively well on topics that are frequently discussed or well-represented in their training data, yet they perform poorly on specialized health statistics and other niche quantities.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can generate reasonably accurate point estimates for many population-level quantities, particularly when using larger, more capable models, but their self-reported uncertainty is badly miscalibrated and systematically overconfident. Simply prompting models to “think more” does not fix this issue. To make LLM-based Bayesian elicitation suitable for real-world decision-making, post-hoc statistical calibration methods such as conformal prediction are necessary to adjust the uncertainty intervals. Reliance on raw LLM credible intervals without such correction is likely to give a false sense of confidence, especially for specialized domains where the models perform worse. Web search integration is not a blanket improvement and may even degrade performance for strong models, highlighting the need for careful design and evaluation of tools that augment LLMs with external information.

title: GaelEval: Benchmarking LLM Performance for Scottish Gaelic
comment: 13 pages, to be published in Proceedings of LLMs4SSH (workshop co-located with LREC 2026; Mallorca, Spain; May 2026)
summary: Multilingual large language models (LLMs) often exhibit emergent 'shadow' capabilities in languages without official support, yet their performance on these languages remains uneven and under-measured. This is particularly acute for morphosyntactically rich minority languages such as Scottish Gaelic, where translation benchmarks fail to capture structural competence. We introduce GaelEval, the first multi-dimensional benchmark for Gaelic, comprising: (i) an expert-authored morphosyntactic MCQA task; (ii) a culturally grounded translation benchmark and (iii) a large-scale cultural knowledge Q&A task. Evaluating 19 LLMs against a fluent-speaker human baseline ($n=30$), we find that Gemini 3 Pro Preview achieves $83.3\%$ accuracy on the linguistic task, surpassing the human baseline ($78.1\%$). Proprietary models consistently outperform open-weight systems, and in-language (Gaelic) prompting yields a small but stable advantage (+$2.4\%$). On the cultural task, leading models exceed $90\%$ accuracy, though most systems perform worse under Gaelic prompting and absolute scores are inflated relative to the manual benchmark. Overall, GaelEval reveals that frontier models achieve above-human performance on several dimensions of Gaelic grammar, demonstrates the effect of Gaelic prompting and shows a consistent performance gap favouring proprietary over open-weight models.

AI.tldr: GaelEval is a new benchmark evaluating how well multilingual LLMs handle Scottish Gaelic grammar and culture, showing that top proprietary models can outperform fluent humans on several linguistic tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing multilingual LLMs show undocumented capabilities in unsupported, low-resource, morphosyntactically rich minority languages, but their performance is uneven and poorly measured. For Scottish Gaelic, current evaluation via translation is especially inadequate because it does not capture deeper structural and grammatical competence or culturally grounded knowledge. The authors aim to create a rigorous, multi-faceted benchmark to systematically assess LLM abilities in Gaelic across grammar and culture, and to compare proprietary and open-weight models as well as prompting strategies.
AI.method: The authors build GaelEval, a multi-dimensional Scottish Gaelic benchmark with three components: (i) an expert-authored multiple-choice question answering task targeting morphosyntactic phenomena; (ii) a culturally grounded translation benchmark; and (iii) a large-scale cultural knowledge Q&A dataset. They evaluate 19 large language models, including both proprietary and open-weight systems, and compare their performance to a human baseline of 30 fluent speakers. They also investigate the impact of prompt language by comparing English vs. in-language (Gaelic) prompting on different tasks.
AI.result: On the morphosyntactic MCQA task, Gemini 3 Pro Preview obtains 83.3% accuracy, exceeding the fluent-speaker human baseline of 78.1%. Across tasks, proprietary LLMs consistently outperform open-weight models. In-language Gaelic prompting yields a modest but consistent average improvement of about 2.4% on relevant tasks. On the cultural knowledge Q&A task, leading models surpass 90% accuracy, but most systems actually perform worse when prompted in Gaelic, and the automatically measured scores appear inflated when contrasted with a manually constructed benchmark.
AI.conclusion: GaelEval shows that state-of-the-art proprietary LLMs can reach and even exceed fluent human performance on several aspects of Scottish Gaelic grammar, while systematically outperforming open-weight models. Prompting in Gaelic provides a small but reliable benefit for some tasks, though it may hurt performance on cultural Q&A. The benchmark exposes both the impressive shadow capabilities of frontier LLMs in a low-resource, morphosyntactically rich minority language and the limitations of current evaluation, underscoring the need for nuanced, multi-dimensional benchmarks like GaelEval for other under-resourced languages.

title: BraiNCA: brain-inspired neural cellular automata and applications to morphogenesis and motor control
summary: Most of the Neural Cellular Automata (NCAs) defined in the literature have a common theme: they are based on regular grids with a Moore neighborhood (one-hop neighbour). They do not take into account long-range connections and more complex topologies as we can find in the brain. In this paper, we introduce BraiNCA, a brain-inspired NCA with an attention layer, long-range connections and complex topology. BraiNCAs shows better results in terms of robustness and speed of learning on the two tasks compared to Vanilla NCAs establishing that incorporating attention-based message selection together with explicit long-range edges can yield more sample-efficient and damage-tolerant self-organization than purely local, grid-based update rules. These results support the hypothesis that, for tasks requiring distributed coordination over extended spatial and temporal scales, the choice of interaction topology and the ability to dynamically route information will impact the robustness and speed of learning of an NCA. More broadly, BraiNCA provides brain-inspired NCA formulation that preserves the decentralized local update principle while better reflecting non-local connectivity patterns, making it a promising substrate for studying collective computation under biologically-realistic network structure and evolving cognitive substrates.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes BraiNCA, a brain-inspired neural cellular automaton that augments standard grid-based NCAs with attention, long-range connections, and complex topology, achieving more robust and faster learning than vanilla NCAs.
AI.motivation: Existing NCAs mostly operate on regular grids with purely local Moore neighborhoods, which fails to capture the long-range connections and irregular topology characteristic of biological brains. This limitation may hinder robustness, learning speed, and the ability to handle tasks requiring coordination over large spatial and temporal scales. The authors seek to design an NCA that is more brain-like in its connectivity while preserving decentralized local update rules, to improve self-organization and better study collective computation under realistic network structures.
AI.method: They introduce BraiNCA, a neural cellular automaton architecture that adds (1) an attention layer for selective message passing, (2) explicit long-range edges to enable non-local interactions, and (3) a complex, brain-inspired connectivity topology rather than a regular grid. The model still uses local update rules at each unit but can dynamically route information over the extended network via attention-weighted communication along both local and long-range connections. BraiNCA is evaluated on two tasks and compared to standard grid-based (vanilla) NCAs.
AI.result: On both evaluated tasks, BraiNCA outperforms vanilla NCAs in robustness and learning speed. It yields more sample-efficient self-organization, is more tolerant to damage or perturbations, and learns target behaviors faster, demonstrating the benefits of attention-based message selection combined with explicit long-range connectivity over purely local, grid-based update rules.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that interaction topology and dynamic information routing are crucial design dimensions for NCAs, especially in tasks that require distributed coordination across space and time. Incorporating brain-inspired non-local connectivity and attention can significantly improve robustness and learning efficiency while preserving decentralized updates. BraiNCA thus offers a promising framework for exploring collective computation and evolving cognitive-like behaviors in systems that more closely mirror the connectivity patterns of biological brains.

title: Brief Is Better: Non-Monotonic Chain-of-Thought Budget Effects in Function-Calling Language Agents
summary: How much should a language agent think before taking action? Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is widely assumed to improve agent performance, but the relationship between reasoning length and accuracy in structured tool-use settings remains poorly understood. We present a systematic study of CoT budget effects on function-calling agents, sweeping six token budgets (0--512) across 200 tasks from the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard v3 Multiple benchmark. Our central finding is a striking non-monotonic pattern on Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct: brief reasoning (32 tokens) dramatically improves accuracy by 45% relative over direct answers, from 44.0% to 64.0%, while extended reasoning (256 tokens) degrades performance well below the no-CoT baseline, to 25.0% (McNemar p < 0.001). A three-way error decomposition reveals the mechanism. At d = 0, 30.5% of tasks fail because the model selects the wrong function from the candidate set; brief CoT reduces this to 1.5%, effectively acting as a function-routing step, while long CoT reverses the gain, yielding 28.0% wrong selections and 18.0% hallucinated functions at d = 256. Oracle analysis shows that 88.6% of solvable tasks require at most 32 reasoning tokens, with an average of 27.6 tokens, and a finer-grained sweep indicates that the true optimum lies at 8--16 tokens. Motivated by this routing effect, we propose Function-Routing CoT (FR-CoT), a structured brief-CoT method that templates the reasoning phase as "Function: [name] / Key args: [...]," forcing commitment to a valid function name at the start of reasoning. FR-CoT achieves accuracy statistically equivalent to free-form d = 32 CoT while reducing function hallucination to 0.0%, providing a structural reliability guarantee without budget tuning.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how much chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning a function-calling language agent should use and finds that short reasoning is highly beneficial while long reasoning is harmful.
AI.motivation: Although CoT is believed to generally improve LLM agent performance, its impact as a function of reasoning length in structured tool-use settings is not well understood, especially regarding when more thinking helps versus hurts.
AI.method: The authors run a systematic sweep of CoT token budgets (0, 8, 16, 32, 128, 256, 512) for a Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct function-calling agent on 200 tasks from the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard v3 Multiple benchmark, decompose errors into types (wrong function selection, function hallucination, etc.), and perform an oracle analysis to estimate the minimal reasoning needed. They then design a structured brief-CoT prompting method (FR-CoT) that forces early commitment to a valid function and evaluate it against free-form CoT.
AI.result: They discover a non-monotonic relationship: allowing a brief CoT (32 tokens) boosts accuracy from 44.0% (no CoT) to 64.0%, but long CoT (256 tokens) lowers accuracy to 25.0%, worse than no CoT, with statistically significant differences. Error decomposition shows short CoT mainly fixes function-routing errors while long CoT reintroduces wrong selections and induces function hallucinations. Oracle analysis shows that nearly all solvable tasks need at most 32 CoT tokens, with an optimum around 8–16 tokens. The proposed FR-CoT matches the accuracy of free-form 32-token CoT while eliminating function hallucination (0.0%).
AI.conclusion: More reasoning is not always better for function-calling agents: most benefits come from a short, structured reasoning phase that primarily helps route to the correct function, whereas extended CoT can degrade performance and reliability. Imposing a structured brief-CoT format like FR-CoT can capture the routing benefits, avoid hallucinated tools, and remove the need for delicate CoT budget tuning.

title: Probabilistic classification from possibilistic data: computing Kullback-Leibler projection with a possibility distribution
summary: We consider learning with possibilistic supervision for multi-class classification. For each training instance, the supervision is a normalized possibility distribution that expresses graded plausibility over the classes. From this possibility distribution, we construct a non-empty closed convex set of admissible probability distributions by combining two requirements: probabilistic compatibility with the possibility and necessity measures induced by the possibility distribution, and linear shape constraints that must be satisfied to preserve the qualitative structure of the possibility distribution. Thus, classes with the same possibility degree receive equal probabilities, and if a class has a strictly larger possibility degree than another class, then it receives a strictly larger probability. Given a strictly positive probability vector output by a model for an instance, we compute its Kullback-Leibler projection onto the admissible set. This projection yields the closest admissible probability distribution in Kullback-Leibler sense. We can then train the model by minimizing the divergence between the prediction and its projection, which quantifies the smallest adjustment needed to satisfy the induced dominance and shape constraints. The projection is computed with Dykstra's algorithm using Bregman projections associated with the negative entropy, and we provide explicit formulas for the projections onto each constraint set. Experiments conducted on synthetic data and on a real-world natural language inference task, based on the ChaosNLI dataset, show that the proposed projection algorithm is efficient enough for practical use, and that the resulting projection-based learning objective can improve predictive performance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a way to train classifiers when labels are imprecise and given as possibility distributions rather than exact class probabilities, by projecting model predictions onto a convex set of probability distributions compatible with the possibilistic information using KL-based Bregman projections.
AI.motivation: In many real-world scenarios, supervision is imprecise: instead of a single true class or calibrated probabilities, we may only know that some classes are more plausible than others, expressed as a possibility distribution. Existing learning methods are mostly designed for precise labels (one-hot or probabilistic). There is a need for a principled way to exploit such graded, possibilistic information while still training probabilistic classifiers effectively.
AI.method: For each training instance, the supervision is a normalized possibility distribution over classes. From this, the authors construct a non-empty closed convex set of admissible probability distributions by enforcing (1) probabilistic compatibility with the induced possibility and necessity measures and (2) linear shape constraints preserving the qualitative order: equal possibility → equal probabilities; strictly higher possibility → strictly higher probabilities. Given any strictly positive model prediction vector, they compute its Kullback–Leibler (KL) projection onto this admissible set. Training minimizes the KL divergence between the prediction and its projection. The projection is implemented via Dykstra's algorithm using Bregman projections tied to negative entropy, with closed-form updates for each constraint subset.
AI.result: The authors derive explicit projection formulas for each constraint set in the Dykstra procedure and show that the overall KL projection can be computed efficiently in practice. Experiments on synthetic datasets and on the ChaosNLI natural language inference benchmark demonstrate that the proposed projection-based objective is computationally feasible and can yield improved predictive performance compared to baselines that do not fully exploit the possibilistic supervision structure.
AI.conclusion: Learning from possibilistic supervision can be cast as training a probabilistic classifier whose outputs are minimally adjusted (in KL sense) to satisfy constraints induced by a possibility distribution. The proposed convex admissible-set construction plus KL projection via Dykstra's algorithm provides a practical learning objective that preserves the qualitative ordering of possibilities and can enhance performance in tasks with inherently imprecise labels.

title: AstroConcepts: A Large-Scale Multi-Label Classification Corpus for Astrophysics
summary: Scientific multi-label text classification suffers from extreme class imbalance, where specialized terminology exhibits severe power-law distributions that challenge standard classification approaches. Existing scientific corpora lack comprehensive controlled vocabularies, focusing instead on broad categories and limiting systematic study of extreme imbalance. We introduce AstroConcepts, a corpus of English abstracts from 21,702 published astrophysics papers, labeled with 2,367 concepts from the Unified Astronomy Thesaurus. The corpus exhibits severe label imbalance, with 76% of concepts having fewer than 50 training examples. By releasing this resource, we enable systematic study of extreme class imbalance in scientific domains and establish strong baselines across traditional, neural, and vocabulary-constrained LLM methods. Our evaluation reveals three key patterns that provide new insights into scientific text classification. First, vocabulary-constrained LLMs achieve competitive performance relative to domain-adapted models in astrophysics classification, suggesting a potential for parameter-efficient approaches. Second, domain adaptation yields relatively larger improvements for rare, specialized terminology, although absolute performance remains limited across all methods. Third, we propose frequency-stratified evaluation to reveal performance patterns that are hidden by aggregate scores, thereby making robustness assessment central to scientific multi-label evaluation. These results offer actionable insights for scientific NLP and establish benchmarks for research on extreme imbalance.

AI.tldr: The paper presents AstroConcepts, a new astrophysics abstract corpus annotated with a large, highly imbalanced concept vocabulary, and uses it to benchmark and analyze methods for scientific multi-label classification under extreme class imbalance.
AI.motivation: Scientific multi-label classification, especially in domains like astrophysics, faces extreme label imbalance due to specialized terminology following power-law distributions. Existing datasets mostly use broad, coarse-grained categories and therefore do not support systematic study of extreme imbalance at the concept level. There is a need for a realistic, fine-grained, controlled-vocabulary corpus to analyze and improve methods for handling rare labels in scientific NLP.
AI.method: The authors construct AstroConcepts, a corpus of 21,702 English abstracts from published astrophysics papers labeled with 2,367 concepts drawn from the Unified Astronomy Thesaurus. They characterize its label-frequency distribution, highlighting the extreme imbalance where most concepts have very few training examples. They then benchmark multiple classification approaches, including traditional ML models, neural models, and vocabulary-constrained large language models, and evaluate them with a proposed frequency-stratified evaluation protocol to expose performance across label-frequency bands.
AI.result: AstroConcepts exhibits severe label imbalance, with 76% of concepts appearing in fewer than 50 training examples. Experiments show: (1) vocabulary-constrained LLMs perform competitively with domain-adapted models on astrophysics concept classification, indicating that parameter-efficient approaches are viable; (2) domain adaptation helps more for rare and specialized terminology, though absolute performance for rare labels remains low across all methods; and (3) frequency-stratified evaluation uncovers robustness differences that standard aggregate metrics obscure.
AI.conclusion: AstroConcepts provides a realistic benchmark for studying extreme label imbalance in scientific multi-label classification and enables systematic evaluation of methods for rare scientific concepts. Vocabulary-constrained LLMs are promising, domain adaptation particularly benefits rare labels, but challenges remain in achieving strong performance on infrequent concepts. Frequency-stratified evaluation should be adopted to better assess robustness and guide progress in scientific NLP under extreme class imbalance.

title: Qiana: A First-Order Formalism to Quantify over Contexts and Formulas with Temporality
summary: We introduce Qiana, a logic framework for reasoning on formulas that are true only in specific contexts. In Qiana, it is possible to quantify over both formulas and contexts to express, e.g., that ``everyone knows everything Alice says''. Qiana also permits paraconsistent logics within contexts, so that contexts can contain contradictions. Furthermore, Qiana is based on first-order logic, and is finitely axiomatizable, so that Qiana theories are compatible with pre-existing first-order logic theorem provers. We show how Qiana can be used to represent temporality, event calculus, and modal logic. We also discuss different design alternatives of Qiana.

AI.tldr: Qiana is a first-order, finitely axiomatizable framework that allows reasoning about context-dependent truths, supporting quantification over both formulas and contexts and tolerating contradictions within contexts, and can encode standard temporal, event-calculus, and modal logics.
AI.motivation: Existing logical systems struggle to uniformly handle statements that are true only in particular contexts (e.g., what a specific agent knows, or what holds at a particular time), especially when those contexts may themselves be inconsistent. There is a need for a general, first-order-compatible framework that can represent context-dependent truth, quantify over contexts and formulas, and remain usable with standard theorem provers while subsuming various specialized logics such as temporal and modal logics.
AI.method: The authors define Qiana, a new logic framework based on first-order logic, that explicitly treats contexts as objects over which one can quantify, and similarly allows quantification over formulas. They design a semantics that permits paraconsistent reasoning inside each context so that contradictions do not lead to triviality. They provide a finite axiomatization of the system and demonstrate encodings of temporality, event calculus, and modal logic within Qiana. They also compare and discuss alternative possible design choices for the framework.
AI.result: Qiana is shown to be (1) expressively rich enough to represent statements involving quantification over both contexts and formulas, such as agent-centric knowledge claims; (2) compatible with paraconsistent reasoning at the contextual level, allowing inconsistent but non-trivial contexts; (3) finitely axiomatizable and grounded in first-order logic, making it compatible with existing first-order theorem provers; and (4) capable of representing several established logical formalisms, including temporal logic, event calculus, and modal logic, within a single unified framework.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that Qiana provides a unified, first-order, and finitely axiomatizable logic for context-dependent truth that can accommodate contradictions locally within contexts and still support powerful reasoning. Because it can encode standard temporal, event calculus, and modal logics, Qiana is positioned as a flexible foundational framework that integrates diverse logical systems while remaining compatible with existing automated reasoning tools. The authors note trade-offs and alternatives in its design, suggesting avenues for further refinement and applications.

title: Do Lexical and Contextual Coreference Resolution Systems Degrade Differently under Mention Noise? An Empirical Study on Scientific Software Mentions
summary: We present our participation in the SOMD 2026 shared task on cross-document software mention coreference resolution, where our systems ranked second across all three subtasks. We compare two fine-tuning-free approaches: Fuzzy Matching (FM), a lexical string-similarity method, and Context Aware Representations (CAR), which combines mention-level and document-level embeddings. Both achieve competitive performance across all subtasks (CoNLL F1 of 0.94-0.96), with CAR consistently outperforming FM by 1 point on the official test set, consistent with the high surface regularity of software names, which reduces the need for complex semantic reasoning.
