A controlled noise-injection study reveals complementary failure modes: as boundary noise increases, CAR loses only 0.07 F1 points from clean to fully corrupted input, compared to 0.20 for FM, whereas under mention substitution, FM degrades more gracefully (0.52 vs. 0.63). Our inference-time analysis shows that FM scales superlinearly with corpus size, whereas CAR scales approximately linearly, making CAR the more efficient choice at large scale. These findings suggest that system selection should be informed by both the noise profile of the upstream mention detector and the scale of the target corpus. We release our code to support future work on this underexplored task.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates two fine-tuning-free systems for cross-document software mention coreference, finding both highly effective, with a context-aware embedding method slightly outperforming fuzzy matching in accuracy and scalability, and offering guidance on method choice based on noise conditions and corpus scale.
AI.motivation: Cross-document coreference of software mentions is important for building accurate software knowledge bases and tools, but the SOMD 2026 task is underexplored, and it is unclear how far one can go with simple lexical methods versus more advanced representation-based models, especially without costly fine-tuning. The authors aim to benchmark simple but strong baselines, understand their robustness to noise and scalability, and provide practical guidance for system selection.
AI.method: They develop and compare two fine-tuning-free approaches on the SOMD 2026 shared task: (1) Fuzzy Matching (FM), a lexical string similarity approach that clusters mentions based on surface-form similarity; and (2) Context Aware Representations (CAR), which builds mention-level and document-level embeddings and uses these representations for cross-document coreference. They run controlled noise-injection experiments that corrupt mention boundaries or substitute mentions to study robustness, and they perform inference-time scaling analysis to understand how runtime grows with corpus size.
AI.result: Both methods achieve strong performance (CoNLL F1 between 0.94 and 0.96) on all shared-task subtasks, with CAR consistently about 1 F1 point better than FM on the official test set. Under boundary noise, CAR is more robust, losing only 0.07 F1 from clean to fully corrupted input compared with 0.20 for FM. Under mention substitution noise, FM degrades more gracefully (0.52 vs. 0.63 F1 drop). Runtime analysis shows FM scales superlinearly with corpus size, while CAR scales approximately linearly, making CAR more efficient for large corpora. Their systems achieve second place across all subtasks in the shared task.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuning-free systems can perform extremely well on cross-document software mention coreference, largely because software names are lexically regular, reducing the need for complex semantic modeling. Context-aware representations offer slightly better accuracy, greater robustness to boundary noise, and better scalability, while fuzzy matching can be more robust under certain substitution noises. System choice should consider the upstream noise characteristics and target corpus size. The released code provides strong baselines and tools for further research on this relatively unexplored task.

title: Abnormal Head Movements in Neurological Conditions: A Knowledge-Based Dataset with Application to Cervical Dystonia
summary: Abnormal head movements (AHMs) manifest across a broad spectrum of neurological disorders; however, the absence of a multi-condition resource integrating kinematic measurements, clinical severity scores, and patient demographics constitutes a persistent barrier to the development of AI-driven diagnostic tools. To address this gap, this study introduces NeuroPose-AHM, a knowledge-based dataset of neurologically induced AHMs constructed through a multi-LLM extraction framework applied to 1,430 peer-reviewed publications. The dataset contains 2,756 patient-group-level records spanning 57 neurological conditions, derived from 846 AHM-relevant papers. Inter-LLM reliability analysis confirms robust extraction performance, with study-level classification achieving strong agreement (kappa = 0.822). To demonstrate the dataset's analytical utility, a four-task framework is applied to cervical dystonia (CD), the condition most directly defined by pathological head movement. First, Task 1 performs multi-label AHM type classification (F1 = 0.856). Task 2 constructs the Head-Neck Severity Index (HNSI), a unified metric that normalizes heterogeneous clinical rating scales. The clinical relevance of this index is then evaluated in Task 3, where HNSI is validated against real-world CD patient data, with aligned severe-band proportions (6.7%) providing a preliminary plausibility indication for index calibration within the high severity range. Finally, Task 4 performs bridge analysis between movement-type probabilities and HNSI scores, producing significant correlations (p less than 0.001). These results demonstrate the analytical utility of NeuroPose-AHM as a structured, knowledge-based resource for neurological AHM research. The NeuroPose-AHM dataset is publicly available on Zenodo (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19386862).

AI.tldr: The paper presents NeuroPose-AHM, a large, structured, knowledge-based dataset on abnormal head movements across many neurological disorders, built automatically from literature using multiple LLMs, and demonstrates its utility via several analytical tasks focused on cervical dystonia.
AI.motivation: Abnormal head movements occur in many neurological diseases, but there is no unified, multi-condition resource that combines movement kinematics, clinical severity scores, and demographics. This lack of an integrated dataset limits the development of AI-based diagnostic and analytic tools. The authors are motivated to fill this gap by systematically extracting and structuring existing knowledge scattered across hundreds of publications.
AI.method: The authors design a multi-LLM extraction framework that processes 1,430 peer-reviewed papers to identify and extract information on abnormal head movements. They aggregate kinematic data, clinical severity ratings, and demographic variables into a structured, patient-group-level dataset (NeuroPose-AHM). They assess inter-LLM reliability (e.g., using Cohen’s kappa) to ensure extraction consistency. To showcase how the dataset can be used, they focus on cervical dystonia and define four tasks: (1) multi-label classification of AHM types; (2) construction of a unified Head-Neck Severity Index (HNSI) that harmonizes different clinical rating scales; (3) external validation of HNSI with real-world cervical dystonia patient data by comparing severity distributions; and (4) bridge analysis linking movement-type probabilities with HNSI scores via correlation analysis.
AI.result: The multi-LLM pipeline yields NeuroPose-AHM, a dataset with 2,756 patient-group-level records across 57 neurological conditions, distilled from 846 relevant articles. Inter-LLM reliability at the study classification level is strong with kappa = 0.822. In the cervical dystonia case study, Task 1 achieves high performance in multi-label AHM type classification (F1 = 0.856). Task 2 yields the new Head-Neck Severity Index. Task 3 shows that the severe-band proportion in HNSI (6.7%) aligns well with that in real-world cervical dystonia data, suggesting plausible calibration in the high-severity range. Task 4 identifies significant correlations (p < 0.001) between predicted movement-type probabilities and HNSI scores, indicating meaningful links between movement patterns and severity.
AI.conclusion: NeuroPose-AHM is a robust, publicly available, knowledge-based dataset capturing abnormal head movements across numerous neurological conditions. Its construction via a multi-LLM pipeline is reliable, and the cervical dystonia case study illustrates how the dataset can support classification, severity harmonization, external validation, and mechanistic analyses connecting movement types to severity. This resource is positioned to facilitate further AI-driven research and clinical applications in neurological movement disorders involving abnormal head movements.

title: Adam's Law: Textual Frequency Law on Large Language Models
summary: While textual frequency has been validated as relevant to human cognition in reading speed, its relatedness to Large Language Models (LLMs) is seldom studied. We propose a novel research direction in terms of textual data frequency, which is an understudied topic, to the best of our knowledge. Our framework is composed of three units. First, this paper proposes Textual Frequency Law (TFL), which indicates that frequent textual data should be preferred for LLMs for both prompting and fine-tuning. Since many LLMs are closed-source in their training data, we propose using online resources to estimate the sentence-level frequency. We then utilize an input paraphraser to paraphrase the input into a more frequent textual expression. Next, we propose Textual Frequency Distillation (TFD) by querying LLMs to conduct story completion by further extending the sentences in the datasets, and the resulting corpora are used to adjust the initial estimation. Finally, we propose Curriculum Textual Frequency Training (CTFT) that fine-tunes LLMs in an increasing order of sentence-level frequency. Experiments are conducted on our curated dataset Textual Frequency Paired Dataset (TFPD) on math reasoning, machine translation, commonsense reasoning and agentic tool calling. Results show the effectiveness of our framework.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how textual frequency affects LLMs and proposes using more frequent paraphrases, frequency-based data augmentation, and curriculum training ordered by frequency to improve LLM performance.
AI.motivation: Although word/text frequency is known to affect human reading and cognition, its influence on Large Language Models has rarely been examined. Most work ignores how often particular phrasings appear in pretraining corpora, despite LLMs being highly sensitive to training distributions. Moreover, because many LLMs are closed-source with unknown training data, it is hard to reason about or control the frequency of inputs used for prompting or fine-tuning. The authors want to systematically exploit textual frequency as a signal to improve prompting, data construction, and fine-tuning for better downstream performance.
AI.method: They introduce a three-part framework centered on sentence-level textual frequency. (1) Textual Frequency Law (TFL): the hypothesis that more frequent textual realizations are better for LLM prompting and fine-tuning. Since actual pretraining data is unavailable, they approximate frequency using online resources to estimate sentence-level frequency and employ an input paraphraser that rewrites inputs into more frequent variants. (2) Textual Frequency Distillation (TFD): they query LLMs for story-completion style continuations of sentences in their datasets, thereby expanding them into richer corpora; these generated continuations are then used to refine or adjust the initial frequency estimates. (3) Curriculum Textual Frequency Training (CTFT): they fine-tune LLMs on task data ordered by increasing sentence-level frequency, effectively creating a frequency-based curriculum. They evaluate their framework on a newly curated Textual Frequency Paired Dataset (TFPD) across tasks including math reasoning, machine translation, commonsense reasoning, and agentic tool calling.
AI.result: On the TFPD benchmark spanning math reasoning, translation, commonsense reasoning, and tool-using agents, models that use their textual-frequency-aware framework—paraphrasing inputs to higher-frequency forms, incorporating frequency-distilled data, and applying curriculum training ordered by frequency—achieve improved performance compared to baselines that do not consider textual frequency. The abstract reports consistent effectiveness but does not provide numerical results.
AI.conclusion: Textual frequency is a useful and previously underexplored dimension for working with LLMs. Preferring more frequent sentence realizations, refining frequency estimates via LLM-based data extension, and adopting a frequency-based curriculum for fine-tuning collectively improve LLM performance across diverse tasks. This suggests that frequency-aware prompting and training can systematically leverage properties of LLM pretraining and should be considered in future LLM design and usage.

title: SenseMath: Do LLMs Have Number Sense? Evaluating Shortcut Use, Judgment, and Generation
summary: Large language models often default to step-by-step computation even when efficient numerical shortcuts are available. This raises a basic question: do they exhibit number sense in a human-like behavioral sense, i.e., the ability to recognize numerical structure, apply shortcuts when appropriate, and avoid them when they are not? We introduce SenseMath, a controlled benchmark for evaluating structure-sensitive numerical reasoning in LLMs. SenseMath contains 4,800 items spanning eight shortcut categories and four digit scales, with matched strong-shortcut, weak-shortcut, and control variants. It supports three evaluation settings of increasing cognitive demand: Shortcut Use (whether models can apply shortcuts on shortcut-amenable problems); Applicability Judgment (whether they can recognize when a shortcut is appropriate or misleading); and Problem Generation (whether they can generate new problem items that correctly admit a given type of shortcut). Our evaluation across five LLMs, ranging from GPT-4o-mini to Llama-3.1-8B, shows a consistent pattern: when explicitly prompted, models readily adopt shortcut strategies and achieve substantial accuracy gains on shortcut-amenable items (up to 15%), yet under standard chain-of-thought prompting they spontaneously employ such strategies in fewer than 40% of cases, even when they demonstrably possess the requisite capability. Moreover, this competence is confined to the Use level; models systematically over-generalise shortcuts to problems where they do not apply, and fail to generate valid shortcut-bearing problems from scratch. Together, these results suggest that current LLMs exhibit procedural shortcut fluency without the structural understanding of when and why shortcuts work that underlies human number sense.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SenseMath, a benchmark to test whether large language models exhibit human-like number sense by using or rejecting numerical shortcuts appropriately, and finds they can execute shortcuts when prompted but lack structural understanding of when they apply.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs are strong at step-by-step numerical reasoning, they tend to compute laboriously even when simple shortcuts exist. The authors want to know whether these models possess a human-like number sense—recognizing numerical structure, selectively applying shortcuts, and avoiding them when misleading—rather than just procedural calculation skills.
AI.method: The authors build SenseMath, a 4,800-item benchmark covering eight categories of numerical shortcuts (e.g., exploiting symmetry or cancelation) across four digit scales. Each item has strong-shortcut, weak-shortcut, and control variants. They test multiple LLMs (including GPT-4o-mini and Llama-3.1-8B) under three tasks: (1) Shortcut Use, where models must solve shortcut-amenable problems; (2) Applicability Judgment, where models must judge whether a shortcut is appropriate or misleading for a given problem; and (3) Problem Generation, where models must construct new problems that admit a specified shortcut type. Models are evaluated under standard chain-of-thought and explicit shortcut-oriented prompting.
AI.result: Across all tested models, explicit prompting to use shortcuts substantially raises accuracy on shortcut-amenable problems (up to 15% improvement). However, under standard chain-of-thought prompting, models spontaneously use shortcuts in fewer than 40% of cases, despite clearly being capable of them when asked. Performance gains are mostly restricted to the Shortcut Use task: models tend to over-apply shortcuts to cases where they fail, and they struggle to generate new problems that correctly admit given shortcuts, indicating weak structural understanding of applicability conditions.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs show procedural fluency with numerical shortcuts—they can execute them and benefit from them when explicitly instructed—but lack robust structural number sense. They do not reliably recognize when shortcuts are applicable, overgeneralize them to inappropriate problems, and cannot systematically generate valid shortcut-bearing problems. This dissociation suggests that present models’ numerical reasoning is more about surface-level procedures than the deeper structural understanding observed in human number sense.

title: The Expert Strikes Back: Interpreting Mixture-of-Experts Language Models at Expert Level
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures have become the dominant choice for scaling Large Language Models (LLMs), activating only a subset of parameters per token. While MoE architectures are primarily adopted for computational efficiency, it remains an open question whether their sparsity makes them inherently easier to interpret than dense feed-forward networks (FFNs). We compare MoE experts and dense FFNs using $k$-sparse probing and find that expert neurons are consistently less polysemantic, with the gap widening as routing becomes sparser. This suggests that sparsity pressures both individual neurons and entire experts toward monosemanticity. Leveraging this finding, we zoom out from the neuron to the expert level as a more effective unit of analysis. We validate this approach by automatically interpreting hundreds of experts. This analysis allows us to resolve the debate on specialization: experts are neither broad domain specialists (e.g., biology) nor simple token-level processors. Instead, they function as fine-grained task experts, specializing in linguistic operations or semantic tasks (e.g., closing brackets in LaTeX). Our findings suggest that MoEs are inherently interpretable at the expert level, providing a clearer path toward large-scale model interpretability. Code is available at: https://github.com/jerryy33/MoE_analysis

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models are easier to interpret than dense models and finds that MoE experts are more monosemantic and interpretable at the expert level.
AI.motivation: MoE LLMs are popular for efficient scaling by activating only a subset of parameters, but it is unclear if their sparsity also improves interpretability. Prior work has focused on neurons in dense FFNs and debated what MoE experts specialize in (broad domains vs simple token operations). The authors want to rigorously evaluate polysemanticity and specialization in MoEs and see if their structure offers interpretability advantages.
AI.method: They compare MoE experts with dense FFNs using k-sparse probing to quantify polysemanticity, i.e., how many unrelated features a neuron implements. They vary routing sparsity to examine how it affects neuron and expert monosemanticity. They then move from neuron-level to expert-level analysis, automatically assigning interpretations to hundreds of experts based on the tasks or linguistic operations they seem to perform (using large-scale behavioral/feature analysis).
AI.result: They find that MoE expert neurons are consistently less polysemantic than neurons in dense FFNs, and that this effect becomes stronger as routing sparsity increases. Sparsity encourages both neurons and whole experts to become more monosemantic. At the expert level, they automatically interpret hundreds of experts and show that experts do not correspond to broad domains or simple token processors but rather to fine-grained task specializations (e.g., particular linguistic or semantic operations such as closing LaTeX brackets).
AI.conclusion: MoE architectures, due to their sparse routing, naturally encourage monosemantic neurons and experts. As a result, MoEs are more interpretable when viewed at the expert level, where experts can be understood as fine-grained task specialists. This provides a promising and scalable direction for interpretability research in large MoE-based language models.

title: GenGait: A Transformer-Based Model for Human Gait Anomaly Detection and Normative Twin Generation
comment: 13 pages, 6 figures. Preprint submitted to a journal
summary: Gait analysis provides an objective characterization of locomotor function and is widely used to support diagnosis and rehabilitation monitoring across neurological and orthopedic disorders. Deep learning has been increasingly applied to this domain, yet most approaches rely on supervised classifiers trained on disease-labeled data, limiting generalization to heterogeneous pathological presentations. This work proposes a label-free framework for joint-level anomaly detection and kinematic correction based on a Transformer masked autoencoder trained exclusively on normative gait sequences from 150 adults, acquired with a markerless multi-camera motion-capture system. At inference, a two-pass procedure is applied to potentially pathological input sequences, first it estimates joint inconsistency scores by occluding individual joints and measuring deviations from the learned normative prior. Then, it withholds the flagged joints from the encoder input and reconstructs the full skeleton from the remaining spatiotemporal context, yielding corrected kinematic trajectories at the flagged positions. Validation on 10 held-out normative participants, who mimicked seven simulated gait abnormalities, showed accurate localization of biomechanically inconsistent joints, a significant reduction in angular deviation across all analyzed joints with large effect sizes, and preservation of normative kinematics. The proposed approach enables interpretable, subject-specific localization of gait impairments without requiring disease labels. Video is available at https://youtu.be/Rcm3jqR5pN4.

AI.tldr: A label-free Transformer masked autoencoder is trained only on normal gait to detect and correct joint-level gait abnormalities without disease labels.
AI.motivation: Existing deep-learning gait analysis methods depend on supervised training with disease labels, which limits their ability to generalize to diverse, heterogeneous pathological gait patterns and makes them less suitable for subject-specific, interpretable impairment localization. There is a need for a more general, label-free approach that can quantify joint-level abnormalities and suggest kinematic corrections using only normative data.
AI.method: Train a Transformer-based masked autoencoder exclusively on markerless motion-capture data from 150 adults with normative gait. At inference, apply a two-pass procedure on potentially pathological gait sequences: (1) estimate joint inconsistency scores by systematically occluding each joint and measuring reconstruction deviation from the learned normative prior; (2) withhold joints flagged as inconsistent from the encoder input and reconstruct the full skeleton using the remaining spatiotemporal context to obtain corrected trajectories for abnormal joints.
AI.result: On a test set of 10 healthy participants instructed to mimic seven different gait abnormalities, the method accurately localized biomechanically inconsistent joints, significantly reduced angular deviations at all analyzed joints with large effect sizes, and maintained normative kinematic patterns in the reconstructed motion.
AI.conclusion: A label-free Transformer masked autoencoder trained only on normal gait can provide interpretable, subject-specific localization of gait impairments and generate corrected kinematic trajectories, without requiring disease labels. This offers a generalizable tool for objective gait analysis and potential clinical decision support in diagnosis and rehabilitation monitoring.

title: Neuro-RIT: Neuron-Guided Instruction Tuning for Robust Retrieval-Augmented Language Model
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Language Models (RALMs) have demonstrated significant potential in knowledge-intensive tasks; however, they remain vulnerable to performance degradation when presented with irrelevant or noisy retrieved contexts. Existing approaches to enhance robustness typically operate via coarse-grained parameter updates at the layer or module level, often overlooking the inherent neuron-level sparsity of Large Language Models (LLMs). To address this limitation, we propose Neuro-RIT (Neuron-guided Robust Instruction Tuning), a novel framework that shifts the paradigm from dense adaptation to precision-driven neuron alignment. Our method explicitly disentangles neurons that are responsible for processing relevant versus irrelevant contexts using attribution-based neuron mining. Subsequently, we introduce a two-stage instruction tuning strategy that enforces a dual capability for noise robustness: achieving direct noise suppression by functionally deactivating neurons exclusive to irrelevant contexts, while simultaneously optimizing targeted layers for evidence distillation. Extensive experiments across diverse QA benchmarks demonstrate that Neuro-RIT consistently outperforms strong baselines and robustness-enhancing methods.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Neuro-RIT, a neuron-level robust instruction tuning framework for retrieval-augmented language models to handle noisy or irrelevant retrieved contexts, achieving better QA robustness than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Retrieval-augmented LLMs are powerful for knowledge-intensive tasks but their performance drops when retrieval brings in irrelevant or noisy context. Existing robustness methods adapt entire layers or modules and ignore that LLM activations are neuron-sparse, missing a chance for more precise control. The paper aims to exploit neuron-level structure to make models more robust to retrieval noise while preserving or improving performance.
AI.method: The authors introduce Neuro-RIT (Neuron-guided Robust Instruction Tuning). They first perform attribution-based neuron mining to disentangle neurons mainly responsible for processing relevant versus irrelevant retrieved contexts. Then they apply a two-stage instruction tuning: (1) directly suppress noise by functionally deactivating neurons identified as exclusive to irrelevant contexts, and (2) fine-tune targeted layers for better evidence distillation from relevant contexts. This shifts adaptation from coarse, dense parameter updates to precise neuron-level alignment guided by attributions.
AI.result: On multiple QA benchmarks, Neuro-RIT outperforms strong baselines and existing robustness-enhancing approaches for RALMs. The method yields improved robustness to noisy or irrelevant retrieval while maintaining or improving answer quality across diverse datasets.
AI.conclusion: Precision neuron-level alignment using attribution-guided neuron mining and a two-stage tuning scheme can significantly enhance the robustness of retrieval-augmented language models to noisy contexts, outperforming coarse-grained layer/module-based adaptation strategies on QA tasks.

title: How and why does deep ensemble coupled with transfer learning increase performance in bipolar disorder and schizophrenia classification?
summary: Transfer learning (TL) and deep ensemble learning (DE) have recently been shown to outperform simple machine learning in classifying psychiatric disorders. However, there is still a lack of understanding as to why that is. This paper aims to understand how and why DE and TL reduce the variability of single-subject classification models in bipolar disorder (BD) and schizophrenia (SCZ). To this end, we investigated the training stability of TL and DE models. For the two classification tasks under consideration, we compared the results of multiple trainings with the same backbone but with different initializations. In this way, we take into account the epistemic uncertainty associated with the uncertainty in the estimation of the model parameters. It has been shown that the performance of classifiers can be significantly improved by using TL with DE. Based on these results, we investigate i) how many models are needed to benefit from the performance improvement of DE when classifying BD and SCZ from healthy controls, and ii) how TL induces better generalization, with and without DE. In the first case, we show that DE reaches a plateau when 10 models are included in the ensemble. In the second case, we find that using a pre-trained model constrains TL models with the same pre-training to stay in the same basin of the loss function. This is not the case for DL models with randomly initialized weights.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes why transfer learning and deep ensembles improve psychiatric disorder classification, focusing on training stability and epistemic uncertainty, showing that ensembles plateau at about 10 models and that transfer learning constrains solutions to a shared loss basin.
AI.motivation: Although transfer learning and deep ensemble learning have been empirically shown to outperform simpler classifiers in diagnosing bipolar disorder and schizophrenia from brain data, the mechanisms behind their improved performance and reduced variability remain unclear. Understanding this can guide more efficient, stable, and interpretable clinical model deployment.
AI.method: The authors repeatedly train classification models for bipolar disorder and schizophrenia versus healthy controls, using the same network backbones but different random initializations. They compare: (1) standard deep learning models with random weights, (2) transfer learning models initialized from a pre-trained network, and (3) deep ensembles that aggregate multiple such models. This setup is used to probe training stability, epistemic uncertainty, and generalization behavior as the number of models in the ensemble grows.
AI.result: Transfer learning combined with deep ensembles substantially improves single-subject classification performance compared with models trained from scratch. Ensemble performance increases with the number of models and saturates around 10 models. Transfer learning models that share the same pre-trained initialization converge to solutions lying in the same basin of the loss landscape, whereas randomly initialized models do not exhibit this constrained convergence.
AI.conclusion: Deep ensembles yield diminishing returns beyond roughly 10 models for classifying bipolar disorder and schizophrenia from healthy controls, providing a practical limit for ensemble size. Transfer learning acts as a strong prior on the optimization trajectory, keeping different runs in the same loss basin and thereby stabilizing training and improving generalization, unlike randomly initialized deep networks. Together, transfer learning and deep ensembles reduce epistemic uncertainty and variability in psychiatric disorder classification models.

title: Towards Position-Robust Talent Recommendation via Large Language Models
summary: Talent recruitment is a critical, yet costly process for many industries, with high recruitment costs and long hiring cycles. Existing talent recommendation systems increasingly adopt large language models (LLMs) due to their remarkable language understanding capabilities. However, most prior approaches follow a pointwise paradigm, which requires LLMs to repeatedly process some text and fails to capture the relationships among candidates in the list, resulting in higher token consumption and suboptimal recommendations. Besides, LLMs exhibit position bias and the lost-in-the-middle issue when answering multiple-choice questions and processing multiple long documents. To address these issues, we introduce an implicit strategy to utilize LLM's potential output for the recommendation task and propose L3TR, a novel framework for listwise talent recommendation with LLMs. In this framework, we propose a block attention mechanism and a local positional encoding method to enhance inter-document processing and mitigate the position bias and concurrent token bias issue. We also introduce an ID sampling method for resolving the inconsistency between candidate set sizes in the training phase and the inference phase. We design evaluation methods to detect position bias and token bias and training-free debiasing methods. Extensive experiments on two real-world datasets validated the effectiveness of L3TR, showing consistent improvements over existing baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes L3TR, a listwise talent recommendation framework using large language models that reduces token cost, mitigates position and token biases, and improves recommendation performance over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Talent recruitment is expensive and slow, and existing LLM-based recommendation systems are inefficient and suboptimal because they use pointwise ranking, repeatedly process the same text, and fail to model relationships among multiple candidates. Moreover, LLMs suffer from position bias and lost-in-the-middle issues when handling lists of candidates. The paper aims to design a more efficient and accurate LLM-based recommendation method that explicitly addresses these biases and listwise interactions.
AI.method: The authors propose L3TR, a listwise talent recommendation framework built on LLMs. It introduces: (1) a block attention mechanism to better process multiple candidate documents jointly and capture inter-document relationships; (2) a local positional encoding method to mitigate position bias and concurrent token bias when candidates are presented as a list; (3) an ID sampling method to address the mismatch between candidate set sizes during training and inference; and (4) evaluation protocols to detect position bias and token bias along with training-free debiasing procedures. The model operates listwise on candidate sets instead of pointwise on single candidates.
AI.result: On two real-world recruitment datasets, L3TR consistently outperforms existing baselines in talent recommendation quality. It also reduces token consumption compared to pointwise LLM-based methods and demonstrates reduced position and token biases according to the proposed evaluation methods.
AI.conclusion: L3TR provides an effective and efficient listwise LLM-based framework for talent recommendation. By using block attention, local positional encoding, and ID sampling, it better models relationships among candidates, reduces token and position biases, and improves recommendation performance. The proposed bias evaluation and debiasing methods offer practical tools for more robust LLM-based recommendation systems in recruitment and potentially other listwise ranking tasks.

title: ProCeedRL: Process Critic with Exploratory Demonstration Reinforcement Learning for LLM Agentic Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) significantly enhances the reasoning abilities of large language models (LLMs), yet applying it to multi-turn agentic tasks remains challenging due to the long-horizon nature of interactions and the stochasticity of environmental feedback. We identify a structural failure mode in agentic exploration: suboptimal actions elicit noisy observations into misleading contexts, which further weaken subsequent decision-making, making recovery increasingly difficult. This cumulative feedback loop of errors renders standard exploration strategies ineffective and susceptible to the model's reasoning and the environment's randomness. To mitigate this issue, we propose ProCeedRL: Process Critic with Explorative Demonstration RL, shifting exploration from passive selection to active intervention. ProCeedRL employs a process-level critic to monitor interactions in real time, incorporating reflection-based demonstrations to guide agents in stopping the accumulation of errors. We find that this approach significantly exceeds the model's saturated exploration performance, demonstrating substantial exploratory benefits. By learning from exploratory demonstrations and on-policy samples, ProCeedRL significantly improves exploration efficiency and achieves superior performance on complex deep search and embodied tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ProCeedRL, a reinforcement learning framework that addresses structural failure modes in multi-turn agentic LLM tasks by using a process-level critic and reflection-based exploratory demonstrations, leading to more efficient exploration and better performance on complex tasks.
AI.motivation: While RL improves LLM reasoning, applying it to multi-turn, long-horizon, agentic tasks is difficult because small mistakes early on create misleading contexts and noisy observations that compound over time. Standard exploration strategies cannot reliably recover from these cascading errors, especially under environmental randomness. The authors aim to address this structural failure mode in exploration so that LLM-based agents can handle complex, interactive tasks more robustly.
AI.method: The authors introduce ProCeedRL (Process Critic with Explorative Demonstration RL). Instead of passively sampling actions, ProCeedRL uses an active, process-level critic that monitors multi-turn interactions in real time. When it detects that errors are accumulating, it uses reflection-based demonstrations—constructed examples that show how to correct or avoid these failure trajectories—to intervene and guide the agent. The agent is trained jointly on these exploratory demonstrations and standard on-policy samples, allowing it to learn better exploration behaviors that avoid compounding errors.
AI.result: Using ProCeedRL, the authors report that LLM agents achieve exploration performance beyond the saturation point seen with conventional RL exploration. Empirically, the method substantially improves exploration efficiency and leads to higher task performance on challenging benchmarks, including deep search problems and embodied tasks that require long-horizon reasoning and interaction.
AI.conclusion: Structural failure modes in long-horizon agentic RL for LLMs stem from compounding errors driven by noisy, misleading observations. By introducing a process-level critic and reflection-based exploratory demonstrations, ProCeedRL transforms exploration into an active, corrective process. This approach yields more robust exploration, better handles stochastic environments, and significantly improves performance on complex multi-step reasoning and embodied tasks.

title: CV-18 NER: Augmented Common Voice for Named Entity Recognition from Arabic Speech
summary: End-to-end speech Named Entity Recognition (NER) aims to directly extract entities from speech. Prior work has shown that end-to-end (E2E) approaches can outperform cascaded pipelines for English, French, and Chinese, but Arabic remains under-explored due to its morphological complexity, the absence of short vowels, and limited annotated resources. We introduce CV-18 NER, the first publicly available dataset for NER from Arabic speech, created by augmenting the Arabic Common Voice 18 corpus with manual NER annotations following the fine-grained Wojood schema (21 entity types). We benchmark both pipeline systems (ASR + text NER) and E2E models based on Whisper and AraBEST-RQ. E2E systems substantially outperform the best pipeline configuration on the test set, reaching 37.0% CoER (AraBEST-RQ 300M) and 38.0% CVER (Whisper-medium). Further analysis shows that Arabic-specific self-supervised pretraining yields strong ASR performance, while multilingual weak supervision transfers more effectively to joint speech-to-entity learning, and that larger models may be harder to adapt in this low-resource setting. Our dataset and models are publicly released, providing the first open benchmark for end-to-end named entity recognition from Arabic speech https://huggingface.co/datasets/Elyadata/CV18-NER.

AI.tldr: This paper presents CV-18 NER, the first public dataset and benchmark for end-to-end named entity recognition (NER) directly from Arabic speech, and shows that end-to-end models outperform traditional ASR+text NER pipelines in this low-resource, morphologically complex language.
AI.motivation: End-to-end speech NER has shown advantages over cascaded ASR+NER pipelines in several major languages, but Arabic has been neglected, largely because of its morphological richness, lack of short vowels in standard orthography, and scarcity of annotated resources. This gap prevents researchers from properly studying and benchmarking Arabic speech NER and comparing modeling strategies. The authors seek to fill this gap by creating a publicly available dataset and providing systematic benchmarks to understand what architectures and pretraining strategies work best for Arabic.
AI.method: The authors construct CV-18 NER by taking the existing Arabic portion of the Common Voice 18 speech corpus and adding manual NER labels that follow the fine-grained Wojood schema with 21 named entity types. They then implement and compare two main approaches: (1) pipeline systems where an automatic speech recognition (ASR) model first transcribes the speech and a text-based NER model processes the transcription, and (2) end-to-end (E2E) speech-to-entities models built on top of Whisper (a multilingual speech model) and AraBEST-RQ (an Arabic-focused self-supervised model). They train and evaluate different model sizes and pretraining strategies, and measure entity extraction quality using character-level error rate (CoER) and character-level value error rate (CVER). They also analyze how Arabic-specific self-supervised pretraining and multilingual weak supervision affect ASR and NER performance, and how model size impacts adaptation in this low-resource scenario.
AI.result: On the new CV-18 NER test set, end-to-end systems clearly outperform the strongest pipeline configuration. The AraBEST-RQ 300M model achieves 37.0% character-level entity error rate (CoER), and a Whisper-medium model reaches 38.0% character-level value error rate (CVER). The analysis indicates that Arabic-specific self-supervised pretraining delivers strong ASR performance, but multilingual weakly supervised pretraining (as in Whisper) is more beneficial for direct speech-to-entity learning. Additionally, scaling models up in this low-resource Arabic setting does not straightforwardly improve performance and may make adaptation more difficult.
AI.conclusion: CV-18 NER provides the first open, fine-grained benchmark for named entity recognition directly from Arabic speech, enabling systematic research on this under-served language. Empirical results show that end-to-end speech NER models can surpass traditional ASR+text NER pipelines for Arabic, mirroring findings in other languages, but with unique dynamics: Arabic-specific self-supervised pretraining is best for ASR, while multilingual weak supervision is more effective for joint speech-to-entity learning, and large models are not always easier to adapt in low-resource conditions. By releasing both the dataset and baseline models, the work lays a foundation for future advances in Arabic speech NER and more generally in multilingual end-to-end spoken language understanding.

title: ATBench: A Diverse and Realistic Trajectory Benchmark for Long-Horizon Agent Safety
summary: Evaluating the safety of LLM-based agents is increasingly important because risks in realistic deployments often emerge over multi-step interactions rather than isolated prompts or final responses. Existing trajectory-level benchmarks remain limited by insufficient interaction diversity, coarse observability of safety failures, and weak long-horizon realism. We introduce ATBench, a trajectory-level benchmark for structured, diverse, and realistic evaluation of agent safety. ATBench organizes agentic risk along three dimensions: risk source, failure mode, and real-world harm. Based on this taxonomy, we construct trajectories with heterogeneous tool pools and a long-context delayed-trigger protocol that captures realistic risk emergence across multiple stages. The benchmark contains 1,000 trajectories (503 safe and 497 unsafe), averaging 9.01 turns and 3.95k tokens, with 1,954 invoked tools drawn from pools spanning 2,084 available tools. Data quality is supported by rule-based and LLM-based filtering plus full human audit. Experiments on frontier LLMs, open-source models, and specialized guard systems show that ATBench is challenging even for strong evaluators, while enabling taxonomy-stratified analysis, cross-benchmark comparison, and diagnosis of long-horizon failure patterns.

AI.tldr: ATBench is a new benchmark to evaluate the safety of LLM-based agents over full interaction trajectories, focusing on realistic, multi-step risk emergence.
AI.motivation: Safety issues in deployed LLM agents often arise over long, multi-step interactions with tools and users, but existing benchmarks mostly test single prompts or lack diversity and realism in trajectories, making it hard to properly assess agentic risk.
AI.method: The authors design ATBench, a trajectory-level benchmark structured by a three-axis taxonomy (risk source, failure mode, real-world harm). They build 1,000 multi-turn trajectories using heterogeneous tool pools and a delayed-trigger protocol that makes risks appear only after several steps. Tools and trajectories are curated via rule-based filters, LLM filters, and full human audits to ensure quality and realism.
AI.result: ATBench comprises 1,000 trajectories (roughly half safe, half unsafe), with an average of 9 turns and nearly 4k tokens each, covering over 2,000 available tools and nearly 2,000 tool invocations. Experiments show that both frontier and open-source LLMs, as well as specialized guard models, struggle on ATBench, revealing nontrivial safety weaknesses over long horizons.
AI.conclusion: ATBench offers a challenging, realistic benchmark for evaluating LLM agent safety at the trajectory level, supporting detailed analysis by risk type, comparison with other benchmarks, and diagnosis of long-horizon safety failures that existing evaluations often miss.

title: No Single Best Model for Diversity: Learning a Router for Sample Diversity
summary: When posed with prompts that permit a large number of valid answers, comprehensively generating them is the first step towards satisfying a wide range of users. In this paper, we study methods to elicit a comprehensive set of valid responses. To evaluate this, we introduce \textbf{diversity coverage}, a metric that measures the total quality scores assigned to each \textbf{unique} answer in the predicted answer set relative to the best possible answer set with the same number of answers. Using this metric, we evaluate 18 LLMs, finding no single model dominates at generating diverse responses to a wide range of open-ended prompts. Yet, per each prompt, there exists a model that outperforms all other models significantly at generating a diverse answer set. Motivated by this finding, we introduce a router that predicts the best model for each query. On NB-Wildchat, our trained router outperforms the single best model baseline (26.3% vs $23.8%). We further show generalization to an out-of-domain dataset (NB-Curated) as well as different answer-generation prompting strategies. Our work lays foundation for studying generating comprehensive answers when we have access to a suite of models.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to get language models to produce a comprehensive, diverse set of valid answers for open-ended prompts, introduces a new evaluation metric (diversity coverage), shows that different models specialize on different prompts, and proposes a router that chooses the best model per query to improve diversity coverage over any single model.
AI.motivation: Many real-world queries allow many different valid responses (e.g., ideas, explanations, options). Current LLM evaluations and generation strategies often focus on single best answers or lack systematic ways to assess and improve how comprehensively models cover the space of valid answers. Moreover, no single LLM seems to be uniformly best across all prompts, suggesting an opportunity to exploit complementary strengths of multiple models. The paper is motivated by the need for a principled metric and practical method to elicit broad, high-quality, non-redundant sets of responses when multiple models are available.
AI.method: 1) Define a metric, diversity coverage, which computes, for a given number of generated answers, the sum of quality scores of unique responses in a model’s answer set, normalized by the sum achievable by an optimal set of the same size. 2) Evaluate 18 different LLMs on open-ended prompts using this metric to measure how well each covers the space of high-quality, distinct answers. 3) Empirically analyze performance per prompt and observe that while no model is globally best, for each prompt some model significantly dominates others. 4) Train a routing model that, given a query, predicts which LLM will yield the best diversity coverage for that query. 5) Evaluate the router on NB-Wildchat and assess its generalization to another dataset (NB-Curated) and to different answer-generation prompting strategies.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across 18 LLMs shows that none is consistently best in diversity coverage across all prompts, but for each prompt at least one model substantially outperforms the rest. The proposed router, trained to select the best model per query, achieves higher diversity coverage than always using the single best individual model: 26.3% vs 23.8% on the NB-Wildchat benchmark. The router’s performance also transfers to an out-of-domain dataset (NB-Curated) and to alternative prompting strategies for answer generation, indicating robustness and generalization.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that generating comprehensive sets of valid answers for open-ended prompts benefits from considering a suite of models rather than a single one. The newly proposed diversity coverage metric provides a principled way to quantify breadth and quality of unique answers. Because different models excel on different prompts, learning a router that chooses the best model per query measurably improves diversity coverage and generalizes across datasets and prompting setups. This establishes an initial framework for systematically studying and improving comprehensive answer generation using multiple LLMs.

title: Systematic Analyses of Reinforcement Learning Controllers in Signalized Urban Corridors
summary: In this work, we extend our systematic capacity region perspective to multi-junction traffic networks, focussing on the special case of an urban corridor network. In particular, we train and evaluate centralized, fully decentralized, and parameter-sharing decentralized RL controllers, and compare their capacity regions and ATTs together with a classical baseline MaxPressure controller. Further, we show how the parametersharing controller may be generalised to be deployed on a larger network than it was originally trained on. In this setting, we show some initial findings that suggest that even though the junctions are not formally coordinated, traffic may self organise into `green waves'.

AI.tldr: Study of different RL-based traffic signal controllers in an urban corridor, comparing their capacity regions and performance against a MaxPressure baseline, and exploring scalability via parameter sharing.
AI.motivation: Urban traffic networks, especially corridors with multiple junctions, require efficient signal control to maximise throughput and minimise travel time. Existing methods like MaxPressure perform well but may not fully exploit network capacity or adapt well to complex, scalable settings. There is a need to understand how different RL controller architectures affect the capacity region of traffic networks and whether they can generalise to larger networks.
AI.method: Develop a capacity region perspective for multi-junction urban corridor networks. Train and evaluate several reinforcement learning-based traffic signal controllers: centralized, fully decentralized, and parameter-sharing decentralized controllers. Compare their capacity regions and average travel times with a classical MaxPressure controller. Investigate how a parameter-sharing controller trained on a smaller network can be deployed on a larger network, and observe emergent behaviour such as informal `green waves'.
AI.result: The RL controllers (centralized, decentralized, and parameter-sharing decentralized) are successfully trained and their capacity regions and ATTs are computed and compared to the MaxPressure baseline. The parameter-sharing controller can be generalised to a larger network than it was originally trained on, and in that setting, initial results indicate that traffic can self-organise into green-wave-like patterns even without explicit junction coordination.
AI.conclusion: From a capacity region perspective, different RL controller architectures offer distinct performance trade-offs compared to classical MaxPressure control in multi-junction urban corridors. Parameter-sharing decentralized RL controllers show promising scalability and the ability to induce emergent coordination, such as self-organised green waves, when deployed on larger networks than those used for training.

title: Grounded Token Initialization for New Vocabulary in LMs for Generative Recommendation
summary: Language models (LMs) are increasingly extended with new learnable vocabulary tokens for domain-specific tasks, such as Semantic-ID tokens in generative recommendation. The standard practice initializes these new tokens as the mean of existing vocabulary embeddings, then relies on supervised fine-tuning to learn their representations. We present a systematic analysis of this strategy: through spectral and geometric diagnostics, we show that mean initialization collapses all new tokens into a degenerate subspace, erasing inter-token distinctions that subsequent fine-tuning struggles to fully recover. These findings suggest that \emph{token initialization} is a key bottleneck when extending LMs with new vocabularies. Motivated by this diagnosis, we propose the \emph{Grounded Token Initialization Hypothesis}: linguistically grounding novel tokens in the pretrained embedding space before fine-tuning better enables the model to leverage its general-purpose knowledge for novel-token domains. We operationalize this hypothesis as GTI (Grounded Token Initialization), a lightweight grounding stage that, prior to fine-tuning, maps new tokens to distinct, semantically meaningful locations in the pretrained embedding space using only paired linguistic supervision. Despite its simplicity, GTI outperforms both mean initialization and existing auxiliary-task adaptation methods in the majority of evaluation settings across multiple generative recommendation benchmarks, including industry-scale and public datasets. Further analyses show that grounded embeddings produce richer inter-token structure that persists through fine-tuning, corroborating the hypothesis that initialization quality is a key bottleneck in vocabulary extension.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that the common practice of initializing new vocabulary tokens in language models by mean embedding is fundamentally flawed, proposes a linguistically grounded initialization method (GTI), and demonstrates that better initialization significantly improves downstream performance and preserves meaningful token structure.
AI.motivation: Language models are often adapted to new domains by adding new vocabulary tokens (e.g., Semantic-ID tokens in recommendation), but the standard mean-embedding initialization collapses all new tokens into a nearly identical, degenerate subspace. This harms the model’s ability to distinguish among new tokens and limits how well fine-tuning can recover useful structure. The authors are motivated to understand and fix this bottleneck so that LMs can better leverage their pretrained knowledge when extended with novel vocabularies.
AI.method: The authors first conduct spectral and geometric analyses of the embedding space under mean initialization to diagnose how new tokens are represented and how this affects learning. Based on these insights, they propose Grounded Token Initialization (GTI), a lightweight pre-fine-tuning stage that uses paired linguistic supervision (e.g., natural language descriptions aligned with new tokens) to learn distinct, semantically meaningful embeddings for the new tokens in the original pretrained embedding space. They then compare GTI against mean initialization and auxiliary-task adaptation baselines on multiple generative recommendation benchmarks, including both industry-scale and public datasets, and analyze the resulting embedding geometries before and after fine-tuning.
AI.result: Empirically, GTI consistently outperforms the standard mean initialization and existing auxiliary adaptation methods on most evaluated generative recommendation tasks. The diagnostics show that mean-initialized tokens occupy a collapsed, low-variance subspace with minimal inter-token distinction, while GTI yields more dispersed, semantically structured embeddings. This richer structure is shown to be more robust: it is largely preserved after supervised fine-tuning and correlates with better downstream performance.
AI.conclusion: Token initialization is a critical bottleneck when extending LMs with new vocabularies; naive mean initialization leads to degenerate representations that fine-tuning cannot fully fix. Grounding new tokens linguistically within the pretrained embedding space (as done in GTI) produces distinct, semantically meaningful embeddings that are retained through fine-tuning and yield superior performance on generative recommendation benchmarks. This supports the Grounded Token Initialization Hypothesis and suggests that careful initialization should be treated as a first-class design choice in LM vocabulary extension.

title: The Latent Space: Foundation, Evolution, Mechanism, Ability, and Outlook
summary: Latent space is rapidly emerging as a native substrate for language-based models. While modern systems are still commonly understood through explicit token-level generation, an increasing body of work shows that many critical internal processes are more naturally carried out in continuous latent space than in human-readable verbal traces. This shift is driven by the structural limitations of explicit-space computation, including linguistic redundancy, discretization bottlenecks, sequential inefficiency, and semantic loss. This survey aims to provide a unified and up-to-date landscape of latent space in language-based models. We organize the survey into five sequential perspectives: Foundation, Evolution, Mechanism, Ability, and Outlook. We begin by delineating the scope of latent space, distinguishing it from explicit or verbal space and from the latent spaces commonly studied in generative visual models. We then trace the field's evolution from early exploratory efforts to the current large-scale expansion. To organize the technical landscape, we examine existing work through the complementary lenses of mechanism and ability. From the perspective of Mechanism, we identify four major lines of development: Architecture, Representation, Computation, and Optimization. From the perspective of Ability, we show how latent space supports a broad capability spectrum spanning Reasoning, Planning, Modeling, Perception, Memory, Collaboration, and Embodiment. Beyond consolidation, we discuss the key open challenges, and outline promising directions for future research. We hope this survey serves not only as a reference for existing work, but also as a foundation for understanding latent space as a general computational and systems paradigm for next-generation intelligence.

AI.tldr: Survey of how language models use continuous latent spaces instead of explicit token-level representations, organizing the field’s mechanisms, abilities, and future directions.
AI.motivation: Explicit token-level computation in language models suffers from redundancy, discretization bottlenecks, sequential inefficiency, and semantic loss, limiting efficiency and expressivity. There is a growing body of work showing that many important internal computations are more naturally and effectively done in continuous latent space. However, these developments are fragmented and lack a unified conceptual and technical overview.
AI.method: The paper conducts a structured survey of prior work on latent space in language-based models. It defines and bounds the notion of latent space in this context, differentiates it from explicit/verbal space and from latent spaces in visual generative models, and then organizes existing research along two axes: (1) Mechanism—Architecture, Representation, Computation, and Optimization; and (2) Ability—how latent space supports Reasoning, Planning, Modeling, Perception, Memory, Collaboration, and Embodiment. It also traces the historical evolution of the field and analyzes open challenges and future directions.
AI.result: The survey consolidates a diverse literature into a coherent framework, clarifying the role and structure of latent spaces in language-based models. It identifies four major mechanistic lines of development and maps them to a broad spectrum of emergent abilities. The work highlights patterns in how latent-space-centric designs improve internal computation, reasoning, and interaction, and surfaces key limitations and gaps across current approaches.
AI.conclusion: Latent space should be viewed as a central computational substrate for next-generation language-based intelligence rather than just an internal byproduct of token models. By clarifying definitions, categorizing mechanisms, and connecting them to high-level abilities, the survey establishes a foundation and roadmap for future research on latent-space architectures. It emphasizes that leveraging continuous latent computation is critical to overcoming the structural limitations of explicit token-space processing and outlines open problems that must be solved to fully realize this paradigm.

title: AI in Insurance: Adaptive Questionnaires for Improved Risk Profiling
summary:
Insurance application processes often rely on lengthy and standardized questionnaires that struggle to capture individual differences. Moreover, insurers must blindly trust users' responses, increasing the chances of fraud. The ARQuest framework introduces a new approach to underwriting by using Large Language Models (LLMs) and alternative data sources to create personalized and adaptive questionnaires. Techniques such as social media image analysis, geographic data categorization, and Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) are used to extract meaningful user insights and guide targeted follow-up questions.
  A life insurance system integrated into an industry partner mobile app was tested in two experiments. While traditional questionnaires yielded slightly higher accuracy in risk assessment, adaptive versions powered by GPT models required fewer questions and were preferred by users for their more fluid and engaging experience.
  ARQuest shows great potential to improve user satisfaction and streamline insurance processes. With further development, this approach may exceed traditional methods regarding risk accuracy and help drive innovation in the insurance industry.

AI.tldr: ARQuest uses LLMs and alternative data to create adaptive, personalized insurance questionnaires that users prefer, though traditional methods are currently slightly more accurate in risk assessment.
AI.motivation: Traditional insurance questionnaires are long, standardized, and poor at capturing individual differences, while insurers must also trust self-reported answers, increasing fraud risk. There is a need for a more efficient, engaging, and reliable underwriting process.
AI.method: The authors propose ARQuest, a framework that leverages Large Language Models and alternative data sources (e.g., social media images, geographic data, and RAG-based information retrieval) to dynamically generate personalized follow-up questions. They integrate a life insurance prototype into an industry partner’s mobile app and evaluate it through two experiments, comparing adaptive GPT-based questionnaires against traditional static questionnaires in terms of risk assessment accuracy, number of questions, and user experience.
AI.result: In two experimental evaluations, traditional questionnaires achieve slightly higher accuracy in risk assessment. However, GPT-powered adaptive questionnaires require fewer questions and are preferred by users due to their smoother, more engaging interaction.
AI.conclusion: ARQuest demonstrates that LLM-driven adaptive questionnaires are a promising direction for modernizing insurance underwriting. Although current accuracy lags slightly behind traditional methods, the efficiency and user satisfaction gains suggest that with refinement, such systems could match or surpass conventional risk assessment while streamlining the insurance application process.

title: Diff-KD: Diffusion-based Knowledge Distillation for Collaborative Perception under Corruptions
summary: Multi-agent collaborative perception enables autonomous systems to overcome individual sensing limits through collective intelligence. However, real-world sensor and communication corruptions severely undermine this advantage. Crucially, existing approaches treat corruptions as static perturbations or passively conform to corrupted inputs, failing to actively recover the underlying clean semantics. To address this limitation, we introduce Diff-KD, a framework that integrates diffusion-based generative refinement into teacher-student knowledge distillation for robust collaborative perception. Diff-KD features two core components: (i) Progressive Knowledge Distillation (PKD), which treats local feature restoration as a conditional diffusion process to recover global semantics from corrupted observations; and (ii) Adaptive Gated Fusion (AGF), which dynamically weights neighbors based on ego reliability during fusion. Evaluated on OPV2V and DAIR-V2X under seven corruption types, Diff-KD achieves state-of-the-art performance in both detection accuracy and calibration robustness.

AI.tldr: Proposes Diff-KD, a diffusion-based knowledge distillation framework to make multi-agent collaborative perception robust to real-world sensor and communication corruptions.
AI.motivation: In multi-agent collaborative perception, agents share sensor information to overcome individual sensing limitations. In practice, sensor noise, communication dropouts, and corruptions degrade the quality of shared data, negating the benefits of collaboration. Existing methods either treat such corruptions as fixed perturbations or simply operate on corrupted inputs without attempting to recover the underlying clean semantic information, leading to poor robustness. A better approach should actively reconstruct clean, semantically meaningful features from corrupted observations and handle varying reliability among collaborating agents.
AI.method: The paper introduces Diff-KD, which embeds diffusion-based generative refinement into a teacher-student knowledge distillation framework tailored for collaborative perception. The method has two main components: (1) Progressive Knowledge Distillation (PKD), which formulates local feature restoration as a conditional diffusion process. A teacher model (using clean or less corrupted data) guides a student model to iteratively denoise and refine corrupted local features into globally coherent semantics. (2) Adaptive Gated Fusion (AGF), which assigns dynamic, reliability-aware weights to information from neighboring agents during feature fusion. The fusion gate depends on the reliability of the ego agent, allowing the system to downweight unreliable collaborators and better aggregate multi-agent information.
AI.result: On two standard V2X (vehicle-to-everything) collaborative perception benchmarks—OPV2V and DAIR-V2X—the method is tested under seven different types of corruption. Diff-KD outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods in both detection accuracy and the calibration robustness of the perception outputs, demonstrating superior resilience to varied sensor and communication degradations.
AI.conclusion: By combining diffusion-based generative refinement with knowledge distillation and reliability-aware fusion, Diff-KD can actively restore clean semantic features from corrupted multi-agent observations and adaptively select more trustworthy collaborators. This leads to state-of-the-art robustness and accuracy for collaborative perception under diverse corruption scenarios, making the approach more suitable for real-world deployment of multi-agent autonomous systems.

title: LLM-as-a-Judge for Time Series Explanations
summary: Evaluating factual correctness of LLM generated natural language explanations grounded in time series data remains an open challenge. Although modern models generate textual interpretations of numerical signals, existing evaluation methods are limited: reference based similarity metrics and consistency checking models require ground truth explanations, while traditional time series methods operate purely on numerical values and cannot assess free form textual reasoning. Thus, no general purpose method exists to directly verify whether an explanation is faithful to underlying time series data without predefined references or task specific rules. We study large language models as both generators and evaluators of time series explanations in a reference free setting, where given a time series, question, and candidate explanation, the evaluator assigns a ternary correctness label based on pattern identification, numeric accuracy, and answer faithfulness, enabling principled scoring and comparison. To support this, we construct a synthetic benchmark of 350 time series cases across seven query types, each paired with correct, partially correct, and incorrect explanations. We evaluate models across four tasks: explanation generation, relative ranking, independent scoring, and multi anomaly detection. Results show a clear asymmetry: generation is highly pattern dependent and exhibits systematic failures on certain query types, with accuracies ranging from 0.00 to 0.12 for Seasonal Drop and Volatility Shift, to 0.94 to 0.96 for Structural Break, while evaluation is more stable, with models correctly ranking and scoring explanations even when their own outputs are incorrect. These findings demonstrate feasibility of data grounded LLM based evaluation for time series explanations and highlight their potential as reliable evaluators of data grounded reasoning in the time series domain.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and evaluates large language models as reference-free evaluators of natural language explanations grounded in time series data, showing that while generation quality is highly pattern-dependent, evaluation of explanations is relatively robust and reliable.
AI.motivation: There is currently no general-purpose way to automatically verify whether natural language explanations generated by LLMs are actually faithful to the underlying time series data, especially without ground truth textual references or task-specific rules. Existing NLP metrics require reference explanations, and classical time series tools cannot judge free-form text reasoning. This creates a gap for assessing correctness of data-grounded explanations in a scalable way.
AI.method: The authors use large language models in a dual role: as generators and as evaluators of explanations in a reference-free setting. They construct a synthetic benchmark of 350 time series across seven query types. For each case, they provide correct, partially correct, and incorrect explanations. Evaluator LLMs receive a time series, a question, and a candidate explanation, and must output a ternary correctness label based on pattern identification, numerical accuracy, and answer faithfulness. They then assess models on four tasks: (1) generating explanations, (2) relatively ranking multiple explanations, (3) assigning independent scores, and (4) detecting multiple anomalies.
AI.result: The experiments reveal a strong asymmetry: as generators, LLMs show highly pattern-dependent performance, performing very poorly on certain query types such as Seasonal Drop and Volatility Shift (accuracy as low as 0.00–0.12) but very well on others like Structural Break (0.94–0.96). As evaluators, however, the same models are more consistent and robust, correctly ranking and scoring explanations even for query types where their own generated explanations are weak or incorrect.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can function as stable and effective reference-free evaluators of natural language explanations grounded in time series data, despite their uneven performance as generators on different pattern types. This suggests that LLM-based evaluation is a feasible and promising approach for assessing data-grounded reasoning in time series tasks and can be used to reliably compare and score explanations without human-written references or handcrafted rules.

title: MTI: A Behavior-Based Temperament Profiling System for AI Agents
comment: 29 pages, 6 figures, 12 tables. Paper #3 in the Model Medicine Series (Paper #1: arXiv:2603.04722)
summary:
AI models of equivalent capability can exhibit fundamentally different behavioral patterns, yet no standardized instrument exists to measure these dispositional differences. Existing approaches either borrow human personality dimensions and rely on self-report (which diverges from actual behavior in LLMs) or treat behavioral variation as a defect rather than a trait.
  We introduce the Model Temperament Index (MTI), a behavior-based profiling system that measures AI agent temperament across four axes: Reactivity (environmental sensitivity), Compliance (instruction-behavior alignment), Sociality (relational resource allocation), and Resilience (stress resistance). Grounded in the Four Shell Model from Model Medicine, MTI measures what agents do, not what they say about themselves, using structured examination protocols with a two-stage design that separates capability from disposition.
  We profile 10 small language models (1.7B-9B parameters, 6 organizations, 3 training paradigms) and report five principal findings: (1) the four axes are largely independent among instruction-tuned models (all |r| < 0.42); (2) within-axis facet dissociations are empirically confirmed -- Compliance decomposes into fully independent formal and stance facets (r = 0.002), while Resilience decomposes into inversely related cognitive and adversarial facets; (3) a Compliance-Resilience paradox reveals that opinion-yielding and fact-vulnerability operate through independent channels; (4) RLHF reshapes temperament not only by shifting axis scores but by creating within-axis facet differentiation absent in the unaligned base model; and (5) temperament is independent of model size (1.7B-9B), confirming that MTI measures disposition rather than capability.

AI.tldr: They propose a standardized, behavior-based way to measure consistent ‘temperament’ differences between AI models, showing it captures disposition rather than capability and reveals nuanced facets shaped by RLHF.
AI.motivation: Different LLMs with similar capabilities behave quite differently, but there is no standardized, validated instrument to quantify these behavioral or dispositional differences. Existing work either imports human personality frameworks and uses self-report (which misaligns with actual LLM behavior) or frames behavioral variation as bugs instead of stable traits. This paper aims to create a rigorous, behavior-grounded framework to describe, compare, and analyze such differences as temperament.
AI.method: They develop the Model Temperament Index (MTI), a behavior-based profiling framework grounded in the Four Shell Model from Model Medicine. MTI defines four axes of temperament—Reactivity, Compliance, Sociality, and Resilience—and measures them using structured examination protocols that focus on what models do rather than what they claim about themselves. The protocol uses a two-stage design that first isolates capability and then separately probes disposition, and they apply it to 10 small language models (1.7B–9B parameters) from six organizations and three training paradigms.
AI.result: Applying MTI to 10 models, they find: (1) the four temperament axes are mostly independent in instruction-tuned models (correlations |r| < 0.42); (2) within-axis facets can be dissociated, with Compliance splitting into fully independent formal vs. stance facets (r ≈ 0) and Resilience splitting into inversely related cognitive vs. adversarial facets; (3) a Compliance–Resilience paradox, where being willing to change opinions and being vulnerable on factual robustness are governed by distinct mechanisms; (4) RLHF not only shifts overall axis scores but also introduces new within-axis facet differentiation absent in base models; and (5) temperament scores are unrelated to parameter count (1.7B–9B), supporting that MTI captures disposition rather than raw ability.
AI.conclusion: MTI offers a standardized, behavior-based way to profile AI model temperament that is distinct from capability and not reducible to human self-report personality analogies. The demonstrated independence of axes and facets, the identified paradox between compliance and resilience, and the shaping role of RLHF suggest that temperament is a structured, tunable property of models. MTI thus provides a foundation for systematic comparison, safety analysis, and design of AI agents with desired dispositional characteristics.

title: SEAL: An Open, Auditable, and Fair Data Generation Framework for AI-Native 6G Networks
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures, 1 table, accepted at European Conference on Networks and Communications (2026 EuCNC & 6G Summit)
summary: AI-native 6G networks promise to transform the telecom industry by enabling dynamic resource allocation, predictive maintenance, and ultra-reliable low-latency communications across all layers, which are essential for applications such as smart cities, autonomous vehicles, and immersive XR. However, the deployment of 6G systems results in severe data scarcity, hindering the training of efficient AI models. Synthetic data generation is extensively used to fill this gap; however, it introduces challenges related to dataset bias, auditability, and compliance with regulatory frameworks. In this regard, we propose the Synthetic Data Generation with Ethics Audit Loop (SEAL) framework, which extends baseline modular pipelines with an Ethical and Regulatory Compliance by Design (ERCD) module and a Federated Learning (FL) feedback system. The ERCD integrates fairness, bias detection, and standardized audit trails for regulatory mapping, while the FL enables privacy-preserving calibration using aggregated insights from real testbeds to close the reality-simulation gap. Results show that the SEAL framework outperforms existing methods in terms of Frechet Inception Distance, equalized odds, and accuracy.
